from it with suffering all the days of your life.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap3_17.jpg){height="99" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

3:17. **the ground is cursed on your account**. As a consequence of
human behavior, the environment suffers. This phenomenon will recur in
the Torah. See the comment on 4:11.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 3:18*](#Contents.html_rgen3_18)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^18^And it will grow thorn and thistle at you, and you'll eat the
field's vegetation.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap3_18.jpg){height="47" width="181"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

3:18. **thorn and thistle**. The environment now becomes hostile to
humans.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 3:19*](#Contents.html_rgen3_19)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^19^By the sweat of your nostrils you'll eat bread until you go back to
the ground, because you were taken from it; because you are dust and
you'll go back to dust."

![Image](images/Gen_Chap3_19.jpg){height="71" width="365"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 3:20*](#Contents.html_rgen3_20)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^20^And the human called his woman "Eve," because she was mother of all
living.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap3_20.jpg){height="44" width="339"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

3:20. **Eve.** Hebrew *haww![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block
height="11" width="8"}h*, the feminine form of the word for "life"
(*hay*). Interestingly, this Semitic root elsewhere can mean snake!
Again, the story is replete with puns.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 3:21*](#Contents.html_rgen3_21)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^21^ And YHWH God made skin garments for the human and his woman and
dressed them.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap3_21.jpg){height="68" width="354"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 3:22*](#Contents.html_rgen3_22)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^22^And YHWH God said, "Here, the human has become like one of us, to
know good and bad. And now, in case he'll put out his hand and take from
the tree of life as well, and eat and live forever:"

![Image](images/Gen_Chap3_22.jpg){height="95" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

3:22. **to know good and bad**. Together with the artistry of language
(punning, dialogue) and the etiologies, the story merges insight into
the human condition. Like the Greek myth of Prometheus, the first story
of the Bible develops the notion that knowledge is a prize taken from
the divine realm and that the price for this forbidden prize is
suffering. What is particular to the Bible's story is the kind of
knowledge that is at stake: knowledge of good and bad. As to what that
means, see the comment on Gen 2:9.

3:22. **put out.** The text plays on forms of the verb
![Image](images/page25-1.jpg){.block height="16" width="30"}: rather
than "put out" his hand (v. 22) the human is "put out" of the garden (v.
23).

3:22. **tree of life**. The comparative literature is intriguing. In the
ancient Near Eastern epic of Gilgamesh, the hero, seeking immortality,
goes to the bottom of deep waters and brings up a plant that is supposed
to have the power to rejuvenate life. (If one eats from it, one becomes
young again.) While he goes off to bathe, however, a *snake* steals away
this "tree of life;" and this is the etiology of snakes' sloughing their
skin and being rejuvenated (*Gilgamesh*, Tablet XII, lines 263--289).

3:22. **and eat and live forever**. The fact that there is no mention of
afterlife being created at the time that death begins, in Genesis 3, is
very suggestive. See the comment on Gen 2:9.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 3:23*](#Contents.html_rgen3_23)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^23^And YHWH God put him out of the garden of Eden, to work the ground
from which he was taken.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap3_23.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 3:24*](#Contents.html_rgen3_24)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^24^And He expelled the human, and He had the cherubs and the flame of a
revolving sword reside at the east of the garden of Eden to watch over
the way to the tree of life.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap3_24.jpg){height="91" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

3:24. **cherubs.** Sphinxes. Creatures of mixed species, frequently with
the head of a human, the body of a four-legged animal, and the wings of
a bird. Cherubs guard the path to the tree of life. Later, statues of
cherubs will be placed over the ark, seemingly guarding its contents,
which are sources of wisdom and righteousness, which in turn are
pictured as ultimately the key to a return to the closeness with the
deity that is lost here (Prov 3:18; 11:30; see my *The Hidden Face of
God*, pp. 115--116).

[]{#Chapter04.html}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 4:1*](#Contents.html_rgen4_1)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **4** {.h2a}

^1^And the human had known Eve, his **T:** woman, and she became
pregnant and gave birth to Cain and said, "I've created a man with
YHWH."

![Image](images/Gen_Chap4_01.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

4:1. **I've created a man with YHWH.** Hebrew
*q![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11" width="8"}nîtî:* one
of the meanings of this word is "to create." It comes to explain the
name Cain (Hebrew *qayin);* but Eve would not claim to have created a
man herself, and so she says that she has created him together with God.
Thus giving birth, the human act that imitates the divine more than
anything else, is regarded here as being done by humans and God
together.

And note: the first person to pronounce the name of God in the Bible is
the woman. Adam, Cain, Abel, and Seth are never quoted as saying the
name.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 4:2*](#Contents.html_rgen4_2)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^2^And she went on to give birth to his brother, Abel. And Abel was a
shepherd of flocks, and Cain was a worker of ground.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap4_02.jpg){height="69" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 4:3*](#Contents.html_rgen4_3)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^3^And it was at the end of some days, and Cain brought an offering to
YHWH from the fruit of the ground.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap4_03.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 4:4*](#Contents.html_rgen4_4)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^4^And Abel brought, as well, from the firstborn of his flock and their
fat. And YHWH paid attention to Abel and his offering

![Image](images/Gen_Chap4_04.jpg){height="70" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 4:5*](#Contents.html_rgen4_5)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^5^and did not pay attention to Cain and his offering. And Cain was very
upset, and his face was fallen.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap4_05.jpg){height="49" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 4:6*](#Contents.html_rgen4_6)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^6^And YHWH said to Cain, "Why are you upset, and why has your face
fallen?"

![Image](images/Gen_Chap4_06.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 4:7*](#Contents.html_rgen4_7)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^7^Is it not that if you do well you'll be raised, and if you don't do
well then sin crouches at the threshold? And its desire will be for you.
And you'll dominate it."

![Image](images/Gen_Chap4_07.jpg){height="71" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 4:8*](#Contents.html_rgen4_8)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^8^And Cain said to his brother Abel. And it was while they were in the
field, and Cain rose against Abel his brother and killed him.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap4_08.jpg){height="78" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

4:8. **Cain said to his brother Abel**. What did he say? The second half
of the sentence seems to be missing. The Samaritan, Greek (Septuagint),
and Latin (Vulgate) texts read: "And Cain said to his brother Abel,
'Let's go out to the field.'" Cain's words appear to have been skipped
in the Masoretic Text by a scribe whose eye jumped from the first phrase
containing the word "field" to the second.

The Masoretic Text (MT) is known from medieval Hebrew manuscripts (from
the tenth century [C.E.]{.smallcaps} on), which were copied from
manuscripts going back to the late centuries [B.C.E.]{.smallcaps} and
early centuries [C.E.]{.smallcaps} The Greek text of the Torah, the
Septuagint (LXX), is known from manuscripts of the fourth-fifth
centuries [C.E.,]{.smallcaps} which were copied from manuscripts of a
translation of a Hebrew text going back to the same period as the
sources of the Masoretic Text. In order to try to determine the original
text of the Torah, we must read both the Masoretic Text and the
Septuagint text, as well as the Qumran (Dead Sea Scrolls) texts, which
are the oldest surviving manuscripts of the *Tanak*. Sometimes the
Targum (Aramaic), Samaritan, and Vulgate (Latin) are helpful as well.

4:8. **it was while they were in the field**. What is the significance
of informing us that they are in a field at the time? Early biblical
commentators searched for the meaning of this seemingly inconsequential
detail. But to understand it we must observe, first, that fratricide
recurs repeatedly in the *Tanak*. It begins here with Cain and Abel and
ends with King Solomon executing his brother Adonijah; and in between
these the issue of fratricide comes up in the stories of Jacob and Esau,
Joseph and his brothers, Abimelek killing *seventy* of his brothers
(Judges 9), the war between Benjamin and its fellow tribes of Israel
(Judges 20), the struggle between Israel and Judah (2 Sam 2:26--27), and
King David's sons Absalom and Amnon (2 Samuel 13--14). Next, we must
observe that the word "field" repeatedly occurs in these stories. Thus:
Absalom has his brother Amnon killed. In an attempt to get David to
pardon Absalom for this murder, a woman (the "wise woman of Tekoa")
comes and tells David a fake story about her own two sons, claiming that
one of them killed the other. In the course of her tale, she mentions a
seemingly unrelated detail: they fought "in the field" (2 Sam 14:6). The
same "inconsequential" detail that occurs in the Cain-Abel story occurs
there. Likewise in the story of the rivalrous brothers Jacob and Esau,
Esau comes to Jacob "from the field" (Gen 25:29). Indeed, Esau is
introduced as "a man of the field" (25:27). And Joseph begins his report
of his dream that offends his brothers with the words "here we were
binding sheaves *in the field*" (Gen 37:7), which prompts his brothers
to propose fratricide a few verses later (37:19--20). The story of the
war between Benjamin and the rest of the Israelite tribes is also
presented in terms of brothers killing brothers (Judg 20:13,23,28;
21:6); and there, too, the word "field" comes up twice. The recurring
word, therefore, appears to be a means of connecting the many instances
of brother killing brother. It recognizes that sibling rivalry is felt
by nearly all humans, and it warns us to be sensitive to keep our
hostile feelings in check---and to be sensitive to our siblings'
feelings as well. This will be developed through this chain of sibling
stories in Genesis, culminating in Joseph, who offends his brothers in
his youth (Gen 37:2--11) but who learns to show them understanding and
kindness in his mature years (50:15--21).

4:8. **Cain rose against Abel his brother**. It never tells why,
exactly, he kills him. Many suggestions have been made. But it is
significant that the text never tells what the reason is. It implies, of
course, that it is his anger over God's favoring Abel's sacrifice, but
it never says this explicitly. Sometimes a silence in the Torah is
revealing. In this case it suggests that the concern is not Cain's
immediate motive, but rather the deeper, essential fact of sibling
rivalry. Everyone with children learns that it is not the specific
content of their fights that matters so much as the *fact* of the fight.
The issue is the *existence* of the sibling. As the first humans to
*have* a sibling, Cain and Abel are the archetypes for sibling rivalry.
The whole world was Cain's until Abel came along.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 4:9*](#Contents.html_rgen4_9)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^9^And YHWH said to Cain, "Where is Abel your brother?"

And he said, "I don't know. Am I my brother's watchman?"

![Image](images/Gen_Chap4_09.jpg){height="69" width="354"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

4:9. **Am I my brother's watchman?** It is hard to give up the famous
English translation, "Am I my brother's keeper?" but I think it is
important to convey the continuing play on forms of the word for "watch"
(Hebrew *šmr*). Humans had been put in the garden "to watch over it"
(2:15), but in the end the cherubs are put there "to watch over" the way
to the tree of life (3:24). Now the first human to murder another
questions cynically his responsibility to watch out for his brother. The
development of this term climaxes when God declares that the promises to
Abraham will be upheld, including that "all the nations of the earth
will be blessed through your seed because Abraham listened to my voice
and *kept my watch*" (26:4--5). This phrase (which in the Hebrew uses
the root *šmr* twice, emphasizing it by using it in the verb and in its
object) thereafter becomes a standard expression in the Torah for
conveying loyalty to God.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 4:10*](#Contents.html_rgen4_10)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^10^And He said, "What have you done? The sound! Your brother's blood is
crying to me from the ground!

![Image](images/Gen_Chap4_10.jpg){height="51" width="339"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

4:10. **What have you done?** These are the same words that God had said
to Cain's mother in the preceding story (3:13). By opening with a
question (even if one already knows the answer!) one gives a person a
chance to tell the truth and admit to a wrongdoing.

4:10. **The sound! Your brother's blood is crying**. Older translations
make this "The voice of your brother's blood is crying," but that is
wrong. The word for "voice" or "sound" is singular. The word for blood
is plural and must therefore be the subject of the plural verb "crying."
The word "sound" must be understood as an interjection.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 4:11*](#Contents.html_rgen4_11)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^11^And now you're cursed from the ground that opened its mouth to take
your brother's blood from your hand.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap4_11.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

4:11. **you're cursed from the ground**. Central again is the idea that
the environment becomes hostile to humans as a result of human
corruption, an idea to which our own age is becoming acutely sensitive.
YHWH had said to Cain's father, "The ground is cursed on your account"
(3:17). Whereas formerly the man had been able to feed freely from the
fruits of the garden, hereafter he would have to work to get the earth's
bounty, and it would produce thorns for him to cope with. Now Cain takes
his brother Abel's life, and YHWH says to Cain, one step beyond what he
had said to Cain's father, "You're cursed from the ground\.... When you
work the ground it will no longer give its bounty to you" (4:11--12).
(In the first case the curse is on the ground; in this second case the
curse is on the man.) The theme of alienation from the earth and its
vegetation, reiterated in this story, continues and reaches a high point
in the flood story.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 4:12*](#Contents.html_rgen4_12)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^12^When you work the ground it won't continue to give its potency to
you. You'll be a roamer and rover in the earth."

![Image](images/Gen_Chap4_12.jpg){height="69" width="340"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 4:13*](#Contents.html_rgen4_13)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^13^And Cain said to YHWH, "My crime is greater than I can bear.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap4_13.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 4:14*](#Contents.html_rgen4_14)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^14^Here, you've expelled me from the face of the ground today and I'll
be hidden from your presence, and I'll be a roamer and rover in the
earth, and anyone who finds me will kill me."

![Image](images/Gen_Chap4_14.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 4:15*](#Contents.html_rgen4_15)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^15^And YHWH said to him, "Therefore: anyone who kills Cain, he'll be
avenged sevenfold." And YHWH set a sign for Cain so that anyone who
finds him would not strike him.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap4_15.jpg){height="70" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 4:16*](#Contents.html_rgen4_16)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^16^And Cain went out from YHWH's presence and lived in the land of
roving, east of Eden.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap4_16.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 4:17*](#Contents.html_rgen4_17)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^17^And Cain knew his wife, and she became pregnant and gave birth to
Enoch. And he was a builder of a city, and he called the name of the
city like the name of his son: Enoch.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap4_17.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

4:17. **Cain knew his wife**. Where did she come from?! See the comment
on Gen 5:4.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 4:18*](#Contents.html_rgen4_18)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^18^And Irad was born to Enoch, and Irad fathered Mehuya-el, and
Mehuya-el fathered Metusha-el, and Me-tusha-el fathered Lamech.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap4_18.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 4:19*](#Contents.html_rgen4_19)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^19^And Lamech took two wives. The one's name was Adah, and the second's
name was Zillah.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap4_19.jpg){height="50" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 4:20*](#Contents.html_rgen4_20)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^20^And Adah gave birth to Yabal. He was father of tent-dweller and
cattleman.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap4_20.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 4:21*](#Contents.html_rgen4_21)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^21^And his brother's name was Yubal. He was father of every player of
lyre and pipe.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap4_21.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 4:22*](#Contents.html_rgen4_22)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^22^And Zillah, too, gave birth to Tubal-Cain, forger of every implement
of bronze and iron. And Tubal-Cain's sister was Naamah.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap4_22.jpg){height="97" width="359"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 4:23*](#Contents.html_rgen4_23)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^23^And Lamech said to his wives:

Adah and Zillah, listen to my voice,

Wives of Lamech, hear what I say.

For I've killed a man for a wound to me

And a boy for a hurt to me,

![Image](images/Gen_Chap4_23.jpg){height="96" width="307"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 4:24*](#Contents.html_rgen4_24)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^24^For Cain will be avenged sevenfold

And Lamech seventy-seven.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap4_24.jpg){height="26" width="349"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 4:25*](#Contents.html_rgen4_25)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^25^And Adam knew his wife again, and she gave birth to a son, and she
called his name Seth "because God put another seed for me in place of
Abel because Cain killed him."

![Image](images/Gen_Chap4_25.jpg){height="69" width="339"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 4:26*](#Contents.html_rgen4_26)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^26^And a son was born to Seth, him as well, and he called his name
Enosh. Then it was begun to invoke the name YHWH.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap4_26.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

[]{#Chapter05.html}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 5:1*](#Contents.html_rgen5_1)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **5** {.h2a}

^1^This is the Book of Records of the Human. In the day of God's
creating a human, He made it in the likeness of God.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap5_01.jpg){height="47" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

5:1. **Records**. This word has usually been understood to mean
"generations," but that is inadequate. The word is used both to
introduce records of births (as in this verse) and to introduce stories
of events within a family (as in Gen 37:2).

5:1. **human**. In these verses, the Hebrew
*'![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11"
width="8"}d![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11" width="8"}m*
sometimes refers to the species, as in v. 2, where it applies to both
male and female; and it sometimes refers specifically to the first male
human, so that it also functions as his personal name: Adam.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 5:2*](#Contents.html_rgen5_2)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^2^He created them male and female, and He blessed them and called their
name "Human" in the day of their being created.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap5_02.jpg){height="66" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 5:3*](#Contents.html_rgen5_3)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^3^And the human lived a hundred thirty years, and he fathered in his
likeness---like his image---and called his name Seth.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap5_03.jpg){height="51" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

5:3. **he fathered in his likeness---like his image**. The first man's
similarity to his son is described with the same two nouns that are used
to describe the first two humans' similarity to God (1:26--27). It
certainly sounds as if it means something physical here. We surely would
have taken it that way if we had read this verse without having read
Genesis 1. Still, we must be cautious on such a classic biblical
question. In any case, the significance of this verse is to establish
that whatever it is that the first humans acquire from God, it is
something that passes by heredity. It is not only the first two humans
but the entire species that bears God's image.

5:3. **Seth**. With the birth of Adam's and Eve's son Seth, the text
begins a flow of generations, narrowing to a particular family, that
continues unbroken through the book of Genesis and ultimately through
the rest of the Hebrew Bible. Ironically, the element that establishes
this flow, that produces the continuity of Genesis, and that sets the
history of the family into the context of the universal history is the
"begat" lists. It is ironic because these lists are tedious to most
readers. They therefore skip, skim, plow through, or joke about them.
The result is that many (perhaps most) readers never get the feeling of
Genesis as a book. It is a continuous, sensible work, filled with
connections, ironies, puns, and character development---which are
diminished or even lost when one reads it only as a collection of
separate stories.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 5:4*](#Contents.html_rgen5_4)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^4^And the human's days after his fathering Seth were eight hundred
years, and he fathered sons and daughters.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap5_04.jpg){height="69" width="339"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

5:4. **he fathered sons and daughters**. This is the presumed answer to
the question of where Cain's wife came from.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 5:5*](#Contents.html_rgen5_5)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^5^And all of the human's days that he lived were nine hundred years and
thirty years. And he died.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap5_05.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

5:5. **nine hundred years and thirty years**. The long life spans in the
early portions of the Torah are an old question. Some assume that the
ancients must have counted years differently. But that is not correct.
(If we divide Adam's 930 years by ten to get it within normal range,
then how shall we divide Moses' 120?) It is clear that this author
thought of a year as a normal solar year because that is how long the
flood lasts. The point to note is: life spans are pictured as growing
shorter. The ten generations from Adam to Noah approach ages of 1,000.
But the last one to live more than 900 years is Noah. The next ten
generations start with Shem, who lives 600 years, and life spans decline
after him. The last person to live more than 200 years is Terah. Abraham
(175), Isaac (180), and Jacob (147) live long lives, but not as long as
their ancestors. And Moses lives to be 120, which is understood to have
become, at some point, the maximum for human life. (See the comment on
Gen 6:3.)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 5:6*](#Contents.html_rgen5_6)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^6^And Seth lived five years and a hundred years, and he fathered Enosh.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap5_06.jpg){height="50" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 5:7*](#Contents.html_rgen5_7)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^7^And Seth lived after his fathering Enosh seven years and eight
hundred years, and he fathered sons and daughters.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap5_07.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 5:8*](#Contents.html_rgen5_8)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^8^And all of Seth's days were twelve years and nine hundred years. And
he died.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap5_08.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 5:9*](#Contents.html_rgen5_9)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^9^And Enosh lived ninety years, and he fathered Cainan.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap5_09.jpg){height="60" width="354"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 5:10*](#Contents.html_rgen5_10)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^10^And Enosh lived after his fathering Cainan fifteen years and eight
hundred years, and he fathered sons and daughters.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap5_10.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 5:11*](#Contents.html_rgen5_11)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^11^And all of Enosh's days were five years and nine hundred years. And
he died.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap5_11.jpg){height="48" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 5:12*](#Contents.html_rgen5_12)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^12^And Cainan lived seventy years, and he fathered Mahalalel.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap5_12.jpg){height="25" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 5:13*](#Contents.html_rgen5_13)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^13^And Cainan lived after his fathering Mahalalel forty years and eight
hundred years, and he fathered sons and daughters.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap5_13.jpg){height="48" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 5:14*](#Contents.html_rgen5_14)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^14^And all of Cainan's days were ten years and nine hundred years. And
he died.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap5_14.jpg){height="63" width="340"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 5:15*](#Contents.html_rgen5_15)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^15^And Mahalalel lived five years and sixty years, and he fathered
Jared.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap5_15.jpg){height="48" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 5:16*](#Contents.html_rgen5_16)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^16^And Mahalalel lived after his fathering Jared thirty years and eight
hundred years, and he fathered sons and daughters.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap5_16.jpg){height="44" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 5:17*](#Contents.html_rgen5_17)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^17^And all of Mahalalel's days were ninety-five years and eight hundred
years. And he died.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap5_17.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 5:18*](#Contents.html_rgen5_18)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^18^And Jared lived sixty-two years and a hundred years, and he fathered
Enoch.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap5_18.jpg){height="47" width="270"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 5:19*](#Contents.html_rgen5_19)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^19^And Jared lived after his fathering Enoch eight hundred years, and
he fathered sons and daughters.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap5_19.jpg){height="69" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 5:20*](#Contents.html_rgen5_20)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^20^And all of Jared's days were sixty-two years and nine hundred years.
And he died.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap5_20.jpg){height="45" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 5:21*](#Contents.html_rgen5_21)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^21^And Enoch lived sixty-five years, and he fathered Methuselah.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap5_21.jpg){height="44" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 5:22*](#Contents.html_rgen5_22)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^22^And Enoch walked with God after his fathering Methuselah three
hundred years, and he fathered sons and daughters.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap5_22.jpg){height="47" width="339"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 5:23*](#Contents.html_rgen5_23)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^23^And all of Enoch's days were sixty-five years and three hundred
years.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap5_23.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 5:24*](#Contents.html_rgen5_24)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^24^And Enoch walked with God, and he was not, because God took him.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap5_24.jpg){height="45" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

5:24. **Enoch walked with God**. This expression is used in ancient Near
Eastern texts to express continuous fidelity. So here it would mean that
Enoch was faithful to God.

5:24. **and he was not, because God took him**. I do not know what this
means. It was traditionally understood to mean that Enoch does not die.
Alternatively, it could be the report of his death. It comes at the
point at which all the other entries in this list say "And he died." The
same word is used later by Joseph's brothers to express the fact that he
is gone (Gen 42:13). At that point, the brothers do not know whether he
is alive or not. It may possibly mean something like that in the case of
Enoch as well: his fate was unknown. At minimum, it means (as with the
case of Elijah later) that there is something distinctive and unusual
about the man, his relationship to God, and his departure from life
among humans.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 5:25*](#Contents.html_rgen5_25)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^25^And Methuselah lived eighty-seven years and a hundred years, and he
fathered Lamech.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap5_25.jpg){height="51" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 5:26*](#Contents.html_rgen5_26)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^26^And Methuselah lived after his fathering Lamech eighty-two years and
seven hundred years, and he fathered sons and daughters.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap5_26.jpg){height="68" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 5:27*](#Contents.html_rgen5_27)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^27^And all of Methuselah's days were sixty-nine years and nine hundred
years. And he died.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap5_27.jpg){height="69" width="340"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 5:28*](#Contents.html_rgen5_28)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^28^And Lamech lived eighty-two years and a hundred years, and he
fathered a son

![Image](images/Gen_Chap5_28.jpg){height="50" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 5:29*](#Contents.html_rgen5_29)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^29^and called his name Noah, saying, "This one will console us from our
labor and from our hands' suffering from the ground, which YHWH has
cursed."

![Image](images/Gen_Chap5_29.jpg){height="74" width="343"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

5:29. **Noah**. The name is connected here to the Hebrew root
*n![Image](images/hdot.jpg){.block height="15" width="7"}m*, meaning
"console," though we would naturally connect it to a different root,
*nw![Image](images/hdot.jpg){.block height="15" width="7"}*, meaning
"rest," which matches the name Noah and does not have the extra *m*
(Hebrew *mem*) at the end. Biblical names, like contemporary naming of
Jewish children, are not necessarily based on precise etymologies, but
rather may be based on similarity of sounds, involving only some of the
root letters. See, similarly, the comment on the name Cain (Gen 4:1).

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 5:30*](#Contents.html_rgen5_30)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^30^And Lamech lived after his fathering Noah ninety-five years and five
hundred years, and he fathered sons and daughters.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap5_30.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 5:31*](#Contents.html_rgen5_31)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^31^And all of Lamech's days were seventy-seven years and seven hundred
years. And he died.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap5_31.jpg){height="50" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 5:32*](#Contents.html_rgen5_32)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^32^And Noah was five hundred years old, and Noah fathered Shem, Ham,
and Yaphet.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap5_32.jpg){height="93" width="354"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

[]{#Chapter06.html}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 6:1*](#Contents.html_rgen6_1)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **6** {.h2a}

^1^And it was when humankind began to multiply on the face of the ground
and daughters were born to them:

![Image](images/Gen_Chap6_01.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 6:2*](#Contents.html_rgen6_2)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^2^and the sons of God saw the daughters of humankind, that they were
attractive, and they took women, from all they chose.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap6_02.jpg){height="70" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 6:3*](#Contents.html_rgen6_3)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^3^And YHWH said, "My spirit won't stay in humankind forever, since
they're also flesh; and their days shall be a hundred twenty years."

![Image](images/Gen_Chap6_03.jpg){height="50" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

6:3. **My spirit won't stay in humankind forever**. Meaning: humans will
not live forever.

6:3. **their days shall be a hundred twenty years**. God sets a maximum
of 120 years for a human life. The reduction takes place gradually from
a peak in the 900s (Methuselah and Noah) to ages in the 100s for the
patriarchs. At the end of the Torah, Moses is said to have lived the
full 120 years.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 6:4*](#Contents.html_rgen6_4)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^4^The Nephilim were in the earth in those days and after that as well,
when the sons of God came to the daughters of humankind, and they gave
birth by them. They were the heroes who were of old, people of renown.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap6_04.jpg){height="80" width="339"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

6:4. **Nephilim**. Some Bible stories are virtually self-contained. Even
though they may have implications elsewhere in the *Tanak*, we can still
read them as sensible, comprehensible individual units. But this account
of the giants is an example of another type of story: those whose
elements are widely separated, distributed across great stretches of the
narrative. These stories provide the connections that make the *Tanak* a
united work, telling a continuous story, rather than a patchwork of
little tales.

The issue is that there are giants: uncommonly big, powerful persons,
who are frightening. The first question is: from where did they come?
Answer: "*b![Image](images/e-image.jpg){.block height="12" width="7"}nê
'![Image](images/e-image.jpg){.block height="12"
width="7"}l![Image](images/o.jpg){.block height="11" width="7"}hîm*"
have relations with human women, and they give birth to giants,
Nephilim. Whatever the biblical author thought
*b![Image](images/e-image.jpg){.block height="12" width="7"}nê
'![Image](images/e-image.jpg){.block height="12"
width="7"}l![Image](images/o.jpg){.block height="11" width="7"}hîm*
were, we can say at minimum that it refers here to some sort of (male)
creatures from the divine realm (see my comment on Deut 32:8). As in an
extremely common mythological theme, such mixed divine-human breeding
produces beings who are bigger and stronger than regular humans.

This does not come up again in the story until thousands of years later.
When Moses sends men to scout the promised land, they see the giants:
"the Nephilim" (Num 13:33). That is what scares the scouts, and their
fear infects the Israelites, changing the destiny of the wilderness
generation. A generation later, Joshua eliminates all the giants from
the land except from the Philistine cities, particularly the city of
*Gath* (Josh 11:21--22). And later still, the most famous Philistine
giant, Goliath, comes from *Gath* (1 Sam 17:4). And David defeats him.

We can read each of these stories without noticing that they are a
connected account, building to a climactic scene, but obviously we miss
something that way. Such widely distributed stories are there because
the Bible is *not* a loose collection of stories. It is an intricate,
elegant, exquisite, long work with continuity and coherence. When we
know our Bible well, we read this story about the giants in creation,
and we are aware that they will play a part in the tragedy of the
wilderness generation, that Joshua will defeat them, and that David will
face the most famous (and last?) of them.

This episode, coming at the end of the first parashah of the year, is a
reminder that one cannot really learn the Torah without learning the
rest of the *Tanak* as well.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 6:5*](#Contents.html_rgen6_5)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^5^And YHWH saw that human bad was multiplied in the earth, and every
inclination of their heart's thoughts was only bad all the day.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap6_05.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 6:6*](#Contents.html_rgen6_6)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^6^And YHWH regretted that He had made humankind in the earth.

And He was grieved to His heart.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap6_06.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

6:6. **And YHWH regretted**. What could this mean? If God knows the
future, how could God regret something once it has happened? Compare
what the prophet Samuel says about God: "He's not a human that He should
regret" (1 Sam 15:29); yet it says twice in that same chapter that God
*does* regret making Saul the king of Israel (15:11,35)! This word is
commonly understood to mean "repented," especially when referring to
human beings, but the question still remains what it would mean for God.
The problem may be more linguistic than theological, as a result of a
lack of a satisfactory word in English to capture the wider range of the
Hebrew word. It refers to a change of heart or making a reversal of
direction. The nature of this change may vary according to the
situation, so that it may mean "repent" or "regret" or "relent," and in
biblical terms it may apply to either God or humans.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 6:7*](#Contents.html_rgen6_7)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^7^And YHWH said, "I'll wipe out the human whom I've created from the
face of the earth, from human to animal to creeping thing, and to the
bird of the skies, because I regret that I made them."

![Image](images/Gen_Chap6_07.jpg){height="68" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 6:8*](#Contents.html_rgen6_8)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^8^But Noah had found favor in YHWH's eyes.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap6_08.jpg){height="22" width="202"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

6:8. **Noah found favor**. Parashat Bereshit ends not with the deity's
mournful statement that "I regret that I made them" (6:7) but rather
with a point of hope: that Noah found favor in the divine sight.

6:8. **Noah found favor**. Note the artistry: This is the beginning of a
chain of wordplay on the name Noah, as its two strong root letters, *N*
and guttural ***![Image](images/H.jpg){.block height="14"
width="11"}***, recur frequently:

 

::: quote
"This one will comfort us" (5:29) *y![Image](images/e-image.jpg){.block
height="12" width="7"}Na![Image](images/H.jpg){.block height="14"
width="11"}![Image](images/a1.jpg){.block height="12"
width="8"}m![Image](images/e-image.jpg){.block height="12"
width="7"}n![Image](images/u.jpg){.block height="11" width="8"}*

"He regretted" (6:6) *wayyiNN![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block
height="11" width="8"}![Image](images/H.jpg){.block height="14"
width="11"}em*

"I regret" (6:7) *Ni![Image](images/H.jpg){.block height="14"
width="11"}amtî*

"Noah found favor" (6:8) *N![Image](images/o.jpg){.block height="11"
width="7"}a![Image](images/H.jpg){.block height="14" width="11"}
m![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11"
width="8"}![Image](images/sdot.jpg){.block height="11"
width="6"}![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11" width="8"}'
H![Image](images/e1.jpg){.block height="14" width="6"}N*

"And the ark rested" (8:4) *watt![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block
height="11" width="8"}Na![Image](images/H.jpg){.block height="14"
width="11"}*

"pleasant smell" (8:21) *Nî ![Image](images/H.jpg){.block height="14"
width="11"}![Image](images/o.jpg){.block height="11"
width="7"}a![Image](images/H.jpg){.block height="14" width="11"}*
:::

 

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 6:9*](#Contents.html_rgen6_9)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## N[OAH]{.smallcaps1} {.h2}

^9^These are the records of Noah: Noah was a virtuous man. He was
unblemished in his generations. Noah walked with God.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap6.jpg){height="62" width="73"}

![Image](images/Gen_Chap6_09.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

6:9. **unblemished in his generations**. Is it not redundant to say "in
his generations"? When else could he have lived?! The classic rabbinic
interpretation of this is two-sided. It can be taken to his credit: that
he could be good even in so corrupt an era. Or it can be taken as
diminishing his credit: that he is good only in comparison to his
corrupt era, but that he would appear less outstanding in a more moral
generation. But really the word here means "without a blemish." It is
used elsewhere to refer to a sacrificial animal, which can have no
injury or else it would be disqualified for sacrifice. There are no
degrees of "unblemished." One either is unblemished or is not. So the
words "in his generations" do not diminish this. Noah's virtue is being
established here. And it is important that a story composed by Jews
emphasized the virtue of someone who is not a Jew---and who is the
father of *all* people. (A related term is used to describe Job, another
virtuous man who is not a Jew; see Job 1:8; 2:3.)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 6:10*](#Contents.html_rgen6_10)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^10^And Noah fathered three sons: Shem, Ham, and Yaphet.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap6_10.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 6:11*](#Contents.html_rgen6_11)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^11^And the earth was corrupted before God, and the earth was filled
with violence.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap6_11.jpg){height="46" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

6:11. **the earth was corrupted**. The Hebrew word, *'eres*, can mean
"land" or "earth." Here it refers to all the earth but still applies
just to the dry land, and not the sea. (Cf. Exod 8:20.) That is why
there will be no destruction of sea creatures. It is the land that will
disappear, and land creatures that will die.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 6:12*](#Contents.html_rgen6_12)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^12^And God saw the earth; and, here, it was corrupted, because all
flesh had corrupted its way on the earth.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap6_12.jpg){height="62" width="354"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

6:12. **all flesh had corrupted its way**. All animal life, not just
humans.

6:12. **on the earth**. Land animals, not sea creatures. Sadly, in the
current era, we are corrupting the sea (and the sky, and space) as well.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 6:13*](#Contents.html_rgen6_13)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^13^And God said to Noah, "The end of all flesh has come before me,
because the earth is filled with violence because of them. And here: I'm
destroying them with the earth.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap6_13.jpg){height="72" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

6:13. **destroying**. The word in Hebrew,
*maš![Image](images/hdot.jpg){.block height="15"
width="7"}ît![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11"
width="8"}m*, has the same root meaning as the word for what all flesh
have done in the preceding verse: corrupted, Hebrew
*hiš![Image](images/hdot.jpg){.block height="15" width="7"}ît*. The same
pun is used in Moses' description of the golden calf event (Deut
9:12,26). This is not just wordplay for its own sake. It conveys the
principle that punishment is meant to be in proportion to the crime. It
also shows once again the environment suffering because of human
corruption.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 6:14*](#Contents.html_rgen6_14)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^14^Make yourself an ark of gopher wood, make rooms with the ark, and
pitch it outside and inside with pitch.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap6_14.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 6:15*](#Contents.html_rgen6_15)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^15^And this is how you shall make it: three hundred cubits the length
of the ark, fifty cubits its width, and thirty cubits its height.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap6_15.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 6:16*](#Contents.html_rgen6_16)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^16^You shall make a window for the ark, and you shall finish it to a
cubit from the top, and you shall make the ark's entrance in its side.
You shall make lower, second, and third stories for it.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap6_16.jpg){height="70" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 6:17*](#Contents.html_rgen6_17)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^17^And I, here: I'm bringing the flood, water on the earth, to destroy
all flesh in which is the breath of life from under the skies.
Everything that is in the earth will expire.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap6_17.jpg){height="73" width="329"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 6:18*](#Contents.html_rgen6_18)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^18^And I shall establish my covenant with you. And you'll come to the
ark, you and your sons and your wife and your sons' wives with you.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap6_18.jpg){height="72" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 6:19*](#Contents.html_rgen6_19)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^19^And of all the living, of all flesh, you shall bring two of each to
the ark to keep alive with you. They shall be male and female.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap6_19.jpg){height="72" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 6:20*](#Contents.html_rgen6_20)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^20^Of the birds by their kind and of the domestic animals by their
kind, of all the creeping things of the ground by their kind, two of
each will come to you to keep alive.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap6_20.jpg){height="87" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 6:21*](#Contents.html_rgen6_21)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^21^And you, take some of every food that will be eaten and gather it to
you, and it will be for you and for them for food."

![Image](images/Gen_Chap6_21.jpg){height="79" width="329"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 6:22*](#Contents.html_rgen6_22)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^22^And Noah did it. According to everything that God commanded him, he
did so.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap6_22.jpg){height="61" width="331"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

[]{#Chapter07.html}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 7:1*](#Contents.html_rgen7_1)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **7** {.h2a}

^1^And YHWH said to Noah, "Come, you and all your household, into an
ark, for I've seen you as virtuous in front of me in this generation.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap7_01.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 7:2*](#Contents.html_rgen7_2)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^2^Of all the pure animals, take seven pairs, man and his woman; and of
the animals that are not pure, two, man and his woman.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap7_02.jpg){height="71" width="339"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

7:2. **pure**. This has also been translated as "clean." Neither English
word captures the sense of the Hebrew. Here it refers to animals that
are fit for sacrifice, which is to say: for humans to eat. Noah takes
seven pairs of the "pure" animals and only one pair of the "not pure,"
because he will offer sacrifices after the flood. If he were to have
only two sheep, then his sacrifice would wipe out the species.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 7:3*](#Contents.html_rgen7_3)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^3^Also of the birds of the skies seven pairs, male and female, to keep
seed alive on the face of the earth.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap7_03.jpg){height="69" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 7:4*](#Contents.html_rgen7_4)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^4^Because in seven more days I'll rain on the earth, forty days and
forty nights, and I'll wipe out all the substance that I've made from on
the face of the earth."

![Image](images/Gen_Chap7_04.jpg){height="73" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 7:5*](#Contents.html_rgen7_5)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^5^And Noah did according to all that YHWH had commanded him.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap7_05.jpg){height="23" width="222"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 7:6*](#Contents.html_rgen7_6)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^6^And Noah was six hundred years old when the flood was, water on the
earth.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap7_06.jpg){height="46" width="339"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 7:7*](#Contents.html_rgen7_7)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^7^And Noah and his sons and his wife and his sons' wives with him came
to the ark from before the waters of the flood.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap7_07.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 7:8*](#Contents.html_rgen7_8)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^8^Of the animals that were pure and of the animals that were not pure,
and of the birds and every one that creeps on the ground,

![Image](images/Gen_Chap7_08.jpg){height="74" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 7:9*](#Contents.html_rgen7_9)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^9^they came by twos to Noah, to the ark, male and female, as God had
commanded Noah.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap7_09.jpg){height="85" width="356"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 7:10*](#Contents.html_rgen7_10)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^10^And seven days later the waters of the flood were on the earth.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap7_10.jpg){height="51" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 7:11*](#Contents.html_rgen7_11)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^11^In the six hundredth year of Noah's life, in the second month, in
the seventeenth day of the month, on this day all the fountains of the
great deep were split open, and the apertures of the skies were opened.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap7_11.jpg){height="72" width="340"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

7:11. **the fountains of the great deep ... and the apertures of the
skies were opened**. One must have a picture of the structure of the
universe that is described in Genesis 1 in order to comprehend the
significance of the destruction that is narrated in the flood story. The
creation account pictures a clear firmament or space holding back the
waters that are above the firmament and those that are below. Now the
narrative reports that "all the fountains of the great deep were split
open, and the apertures of the skies were opened." The cosmic waters are
able to spill in from above and below, filling the habitable bubble,
thus:

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 7:12*](#Contents.html_rgen7_12)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^12^And there was rain on the earth, forty days and forty nights.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap7_12.jpg){height="46" width="339"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 7:13*](#Contents.html_rgen7_13)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^13^In this very day Noah came, and Shem and Ham and Yaphet, Noah's
sons, and Noah's wife and his sons' three wives with them to the ark,

![Image](images/Gen_Chap7_13.jpg){height="67" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

7:13. **the ark**. At the same time that the threat at the cosmic level
is more powerful than is usually pictured, the condition of the humans
in the story is more vulnerable. As commonly pictured in art, the "ark"
that bears the humans and animals through the floodwaters is a boat.
This would be a correct view of the vehicle in the Mesopotamian flood
account, now contained in one form in Tablet XI of the epic of
Gilgamesh. There the hero, Utnapishtim, takes his family, his
possessions, and the animals into a boat to escape a flood brought on by
the gods. The parallels to the biblical account are tantalizing:
Utnapishtim's boat comes to rest on a mountaintop; he sends out three
birds, including a dove and a raven; and he builds an altar and offers a
sacrifice. A notable difference, however, is precisely that the biblical
flood hero, Noah, does not build a boat, but rather an ark. An ark is a
box. The measurements given for it in the text are rectangular, and
there is neither a keel nor a rudder nor a sail. The same applies to the
box in which the baby Moses is placed in Exod 2:3. Still, I must admit
that it is notable that the two objects called
*![Image](images/tdot.jpg){.block height="12"
width="5"}![Image](images/e1.jpg){.block height="14"
width="6"}b![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11" width="8"}h*
in the Hebrew Bible both float on water. I have used the word "ark" here
reluctantly because it is now so familiar that readers would find any
other word jarring. Regardless of the word, though, the important fact
is that, in such a vessel, the humans and animals are utterly helpless,
cast about in the waters without any control over their fate. To
appreciate the image that this narrative sets before us, we must picture
this helpless box of life tossed about in a violent universe that is
breaking at its seams.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 7:14*](#Contents.html_rgen7_14)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^14^they and all the wild animals by their kind and all the domestic
animals by their kind and all the creeping animals that creep on the
earth by their kind and all the birds by their kind, all fowl, all
winged things.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap7_14.jpg){height="73" width="344"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

![Image](images/foundation.jpg){height="377" width="450"}

It is far more than an ordinary rain. It is a cosmic crisis, in which
the very structure of the universe is endangered.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 7:15*](#Contents.html_rgen7_15)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^15^And they came to Noah, to the ark, by twos of all flesh in which was
the breath of life,

![Image](images/Gen_Chap7_15.jpg){height="70" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 7:16*](#Contents.html_rgen7_16)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^16^and those that came were male and female; some from all flesh came,
as God had commanded him. And YHWH closed it for him.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap7_16.jpg){height="48" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

7:16. **YHWH closed it for him**. Note the degree of close contact that
is pictured between God and humans at this stage. This will reach a high
point in Moses' experience at Sinai in Exodus, and then it will
gradually diminish through the rest of the *Tanak*.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 7:17*](#Contents.html_rgen7_17)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^17^And the flood was on the earth for forty days, and the waters
multiplied and raised the ark, and it was lifted from the earth.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap7_17.jpg){height="68" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 7:18*](#Contents.html_rgen7_18)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^18^And the waters grew strong and multiplied very much on the earth,
and the ark went on the face of the waters.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap7_18.jpg){height="67" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 7:19*](#Contents.html_rgen7_19)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^19^And the waters had grown very, very strong on the earth, so they
covered all the high mountains that are under all the skies.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap7_19.jpg){height="47" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 7:20*](#Contents.html_rgen7_20)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^20^Fifteen cubits above, the waters grew stronger, and they covered the
mountains.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap7_20.jpg){height="45" width="339"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 7:21*](#Contents.html_rgen7_21)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^21^And all flesh that creep on the earth---of the birds and of the
domestic animals and of the wild animals and of all the swarming
creatures that swarm on the earth, and all the humans---expired.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap7_21.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 7:22*](#Contents.html_rgen7_22)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^22^Everything that had the breathing spirit of life in its nostrils,
everything that was on the ground, died.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap7_22.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 7:23*](#Contents.html_rgen7_23)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^23^And He wiped out all the substance that was on the face of the
earth, from human to animal to creeping thing and to bird of the skies,
and they were wiped out from the earth, and just Noah and those who were
with him in the ark were left.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap7_23.jpg){height="95" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 7:24*](#Contents.html_rgen7_24)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^24^And the water grew strong on the earth a hundred fifty days.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap7_24.jpg){height="44" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

[]{#Chapter08.html}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 8:1*](#Contents.html_rgen8_1)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **8** {.h2a}

^1^And God remembered Noah and all the wild animals and all the domestic
animals that were with him in the ark, and God passed a wind over the
earth, and the water decreased.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap8_01.jpg){height="71" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

8:1. **God remembered**. There is an old tradition that when the deity
is called God (*'![Image](images/e-image.jpg){.block height="12"
width="7"}l![Image](images/o.jpg){.block height="11" width="7"}îm*) this
refers to the deity's aspect of being *just*, and that when the deity is
called YHWH it refers to the divine aspect of being *merciful*. But in
this verse the deity is being merciful but is still called *God*. Rashi
justifies this by saying that the standard of justice is changed into
the standard of compassion by means of the prayers of virtuous people.
That is a lovely interpretation, but it has no basis in the text. Noah
does not pray. (Noah never speaks in this text until after the flood.)
And, earlier, when the deity decides to destroy all flesh, the name
YHWH, which is supposed to stand for divine mercy, is used (6:7). The
fact is that this traditional distinction in the divine names does not
hold up through the biblical text. Rather, the relationship between the
justice and mercy of God is far more complex than this.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 8:2*](#Contents.html_rgen8_2)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^2^And the fountains of the deep and the apertures of the skies were
shut, and the rain was restrained from the skies.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap8_02.jpg){height="71" width="360"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 8:3*](#Contents.html_rgen8_3)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^3^And the waters went back from on the earth, going back continually,
and the water receded at the end of a hundred fifty days.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap8_03.jpg){height="67" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 8:4*](#Contents.html_rgen8_4)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^4^And the ark rested in the seventh month, in the seventeenth day of
the month, on the mountains of Ararat.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap8_04.jpg){height="70" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 8:5*](#Contents.html_rgen8_5)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^5^And the water went on receding until the tenth month. In the tenth
month, in the first of the month, the tops of the mountains appeared.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap8_05.jpg){height="69" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 8:6*](#Contents.html_rgen8_6)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^6^And it was at the end of forty days, and Noah opened the window of
the ark that he had made.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap8_06.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 8:7*](#Contents.html_rgen8_7)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^7^And he let a raven go, and it went back and forth until the water
dried up from the earth.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap8_07.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 8:8*](#Contents.html_rgen8_8)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^8^And he let a dove go from him to see whether the waters had eased
from the face of the earth.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap8_08.jpg){height="70" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 8:9*](#Contents.html_rgen8_9)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^9^And the dove did not find a resting place for its foot, and it came
back to him to the ark, for waters were on the face of the earth, and he
put out his hand and took it and brought it to him to the ark.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap8_09.jpg){height="70" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 8:10*](#Contents.html_rgen8_10)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^10^And he waited still another seven days, and he again let a dove go
from the ark.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap8_10.jpg){height="47" width="339"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 8:11*](#Contents.html_rgen8_11)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^11^And the dove came to him at evening time, and here was an olive leaf
torn off in its mouth, and Noah knew that the waters had eased from the
earth.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap8_11.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 8:12*](#Contents.html_rgen8_12)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^12^And he waited still another seven days, and he let a dove go, and it
did not come back to him ever again.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap8_12.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 8:13*](#Contents.html_rgen8_13)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^13^And it was in the six hundred and first year, in the first month, in
the first of the month: the water dried from on the earth. And Noah
turned back the covering of the ark and looked, and here the face of the
earth had dried.

![Image](images/Gen_Chap8_13.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Genesis 8:14*](#Contents.html_rgen8_14)

------------------------------------------------------------------------
