------------------------------------------------------------------------

^22^And also let the priests who approach YHWH consecrate themselves, or
else YHWH will break out against them."

![Image](images/Exo_Chap19_22.jpg){height="46" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 19:23*](#Contents.html_rExo19_23)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^23^And Moses said to YHWH, "The people is not able to go up to Mount
Sinai, because you warned us, saying, 'Limit the mountain and consecrate
it.'"

![Image](images/Exo_Chap19_23.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 19:24*](#Contents.html_rExo19_24)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^24^And YHWH said to him, "Go. Go down. Then you'll come up, you and
Aaron with you. And let the priests and the people not break through to
come up to YHWH, or else He'll break out against them."

![Image](images/Exo_Chap19_24.jpg){height="75" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 19:25*](#Contents.html_rExo19_25)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^25^And Moses went down to the people, and he said it to them.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap19_25.jpg){height="46" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

[]{#Exodus20.html}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 20:1*](#Contents.html_rExo20_1)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **20** {.h2a}

^1^And God spoke all these words, saying:

![Image](images/Exo_Chap20_01.jpg){height="43" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

20:1. **God spoke all these words**. God speaks the words of the Ten
Commandments aloud directly to the people. The Ten Commandments are the
text of the covenant between God and Israel. One of the most fruitful
discoveries of biblical research was the independent recognition by
three scholars (E. Bickermann, G. Mendenhall, and K. Baltzer) in the
1950s that there is a formal similarity between the Israelite covenant
and international legal documents of the ancient Near Eastern countries.
The treaty documents, dictated by regional kings (suzerains) to the
local city kings (vassals) who were subject to them, formalized the
relationship between the two. They regularly included a specific group
of formal elements:

1.  The suzerain's introduction of himself by name.
2.  An historical prologue, giving the history of the relations between
    the parties, generally showing what the suzerain had done for his
    vassal, and thus establishing that the vassal was in the suzerain's
    debt.
3.  The prime stipulation of the treaty, namely that the vassal was to
    have allegiance to this suzerain and to no other.
4.  The rest of the stipulations, e.g., the vassal's obligation to pay
    tribute, to come when summoned to the suzerain's court, and to
    provide troops for the suzerain's military defense.

The Sinai covenant here exhibits striking parallels to this legal
structure, containing the key elements:

+-----------------------------------+-----------------------------------+
| 1. Introduction:                  | I am YHWH your God                |
+-----------------------------------+-----------------------------------+
| 2. Historical Prologue:           | who brought you out from the land |
|                                   | of Egypt, from a house of slaves  |
+-----------------------------------+-----------------------------------+
| 3. Prime Stipulation:             | You shall not have other gods     |
+-----------------------------------+-----------------------------------+
| 4. Other Stipulations:            | You shall not make a statue       |
|                                   |                                   |
|                                   | You shall not bring up the name   |
|                                   | of YHWH, your God, for a          |
|                                   | falsehood                         |
|                                   |                                   |
|                                   | Remember the Sabbath ...          |
|                                   |                                   |
|                                   | Honor your father and your mother |
|                                   | ...                               |
+-----------------------------------+-----------------------------------+

The Near Eastern suzerainty treaties also include other features that
appear here in the covenant. They included an oath sworn by the vassal
pledging fidelity to the suzerain and to the requirements of the treaty,
and they included provision for the treaty document, requiring that it
be deposited in a sacred place. In Exodus the people pledge their
obedience (24:3,7), and the covenant text is deposited in the ark in the
Tabernacle (25:16,21; 40:20).

Thus Israel's relations with its God are conceived in the legal terms of
that world. The words of the Ten Commandments and of the Covenant Code
are not only orders that must be obeyed. They are legal corpora,
concluded contractually between the creator and a human community.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 20:2*](#Contents.html_rExo20_2)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^2^I am YHWH, your God, who brought you out from the land of Egypt, from
a house of slaves.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap20_02.jpg){height="47" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

20:2. **I am YHWH, your God**. People question whether this first verse
of the covenant is a commandment or a statement, and they argue over
what, exactly, it commands. The evidence from the ancient Near Eastern
documents makes clear that this is not to be counted as one of the
commandments. Introductory statements such as this one are common and
fundamental to these documents: stating the name of the one who is
dictating the terms and stating the history of what this one has done
for the recipient of these terms. They leave no doubt that the first
commandment is not "I am YHWH . . ." but rather "You shall have no other
gods...." This is confirmed by the fact that when one counts "I am
YHWH . . ." as a commandment, one comes out with eleven commandments.
Those who have thought this to be a commandment have had to combine two
of the later commandments into one.

In any case, the question of what the opening words command arises in
part because in English we call this text by the name "the Ten
Commandments." That misleads us, though, because in the Torah itself
(Exod 34:28; Deut 10:4), this text is called simply the Ten Things (or
Ten Words, or Ten Statements; Hebrew
*'![Image](images/a1.jpg){height="12"
width="8"}![Image](images/s1.jpg){height="11" width="7"}eret
hadd![Image](images/e-image.jpg){height="12"
width="7"}b![Image](images/a-image.jpg){height="11" width="8"}rîm*).

20:2. **who brought you out from the land of Egypt**. In the
relationship between the covenant and the ancient Near Eastern legal
texts, the historical prologue has special importance: In the Torah,
*law is set in history*. Law is *never* given in the *Tanak* in the
abstract, apart from a historical context. There is always an
understanding that the commandments are to be followed because God did
something for the humans who are being commanded. There are more than
sixty chapters of Torah before the first law to Israel occurs (Exodus
12). Indeed, as I said in my first comment (Gen 1:1), Rashi began his
commentary with the question of why the Torah starts with creation and
subsequent history rather than being strictly a law code and starting
with the first law in Exodus 12. I would now add another answer: that
biblical Israel understood this law to require historical context. The
law was conceived to have been born at a turning point in history.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 20:3*](#Contents.html_rExo20_3)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^3^You shall not have other gods before my face.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap20_03.jpg){height="44" width="288"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

20:3. **You shall not have other gods**. Various groups have counted the
Ten Commandments differently because of the matter of the historical
prologue. Some identify the first commandment as "I am YHWH, your God,
who brought you out from the land of Egypt, from a house of slaves."
Others list "You shall have no other gods before me" as the first
commandment. The evidence from the Near Eastern treaties indicates that
the latter way is correct. The first line is not a commandment. It is
the historical prologue. (See the comment on 20:2.)

20:3. **before my face**. Hebrew *'al
p![Image](images/a-image.jpg){height="11"
width="8"}n![Image](images/a-image.jpg){height="11" width="8"}y* has no
good English equivalent. The point is that *everywhere* is *'al
p![Image](images/a-image.jpg){height="11"
width="8"}n![Image](images/a-image.jpg){height="11"
width="8"}![Image](images/a-image.jpg){height="11" width="8"}y*, i.e.,
before God's face, so the commandment means that one simply cannot have
any other gods. Monotheism has become so successful in Western
civilization and much of Eastern civilization that it is difficult now
for us to recapture the original force of this commandment. The profound
has become the norm, so people forget that it is profound.

Some argue that the wording of the first commandment is not properly
monotheistic. They say the commandment "You shall not have other gods
before my face (or: in my presence, or: before me)" suggests a
recognition that other gods exist. They therefore argue that the
commandment only prohibits the *worship* of other deities but does not
deny their reality. This appears to be a too-careful reading of these
words. The fact is that it is difficult to word a command of monotheism
without referring to other deities. This is simply a fact of language.
(I frequently suggest to my students that they try to compose a number
of formulations of a command of monotheism, and we regularly find that a
substantial number of the possible formulations will contain reference
to other gods.) Those who hold the view that monotheism came late in
Israel cannot build a case on the wording of the first commandment. The
issue here is linguistic, not theological.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 20:4*](#Contents.html_rExo20_4)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^4^You shall not make a statue or any form that is in the skies above or
that is in the earth below or that is in the water below the earth.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap20_04.jpg){height="74" width="339"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

20:4. **statue**. Pagan religion in the ancient Near East, as in Greece,
was not idol worship. The statues of the gods and goddesses were icons,
symbols that represented the deities' presence. They provided a
direction for prayer, a feeling of awe, a sense of one's god's
closeness. But pagans did not believe that the statue was the god. The
god was a force, known through its particular power in the universe:
sky, earth, sun, wind, grain, fertility. So, if the statues were not
themselves worshiped, why are Israelites forbidden to have them? One
reason may be that they were inconsistent with monotheism: If Israel was
not permitted to have other gods, of course they could not have statues
of them. And the issue then was not making statues of the pagan gods but
making statues of *YHWH*. In the first place, YHWH was not to be
identified with any single force in nature. Likewise, a statue would
limit what YHWH was, identifying God with something visible in this
world. Moreover, multiple statues of YHWH in multiple places would not
be felt to be consistent with God's oneness. In the Torah, only one
Temple is permitted, only one sacrificial altar. Sacrifice to YHWH is
not permitted anywhere else on earth (Leviticus 17; Deuteronomy 12).
Confirmation of the fact that the issue is making statues of YHWH comes
from archaeological evidence: small figures of females are extremely
common finds, but in all the excavations in Israel so far only two male
figures have ever been found. (And one of them, which my friend Yigal
Shiloh uncovered in the City of David excavations of biblical Jerusalem,
was clearly not Israelite.) That is, female figures often escaped the
ban on statues, but male figures did not---not because they were Baal or
Dagon figures, but because they could be YHWH.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 20:5*](#Contents.html_rExo20_5)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^5^You shall not bow to them, and you shall not serve them. Because I,
YHWH, your God, am a jealous God, counting parents' crime on children,
on the third generation, and on the fourth generation of those who hate
me,

![Image](images/Exo_Chap20_05.jpg){height="96" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

20:5. **a jealous God**. What could it mean for a god to be jealous? A
pagan deity might be thought to be jealous of another deity's powers or
possessions but would not be jealous of another god's *existence*.
Jealousy is a profoundly monotheistic quality in a god. The God of
Israel does not tolerate making a statue of another god, bowing to
another god, or serving another god, of any form.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 20:6*](#Contents.html_rExo20_6)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^6^but practicing kindness to thousands for those who love me and for
those who observe my commandments.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap20_06.jpg){height="45" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

20:6. **thousands**. Of generations.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 20:7*](#Contents.html_rExo20_7)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^7^You shall not bring up the name of YHWH, your God, for a falsehood,
because YHWH will not make one innocent who will bring up His name for a
falsehood.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap20_07.jpg){height="61" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

20:7. **bring up His name for a falsehood**. (Traditionally translated
"take His name in vain.") This does not mean that one cannot say the
divine name in an exclamation---much less does it mean saying the words
"God" or "Lord" in an exclamation. It refers to falsehood. The term
refers to a false report (Exod 23:1) and a false witness (Deut 5:17;
which corresponds to the term "lying witness" here in Exod 20:13). This
commandment means that one cannot make an oath in God's name and not
fulfill it. To appreciate the seriousness of this commandment, note the
case of Joshua, who is deceived by the Gibeonites into making an oath of
nonaggression. When Joshua learns that the Gibeonites are among the
Canaanite residents whom he was commanded by God to destroy, he lets
them live, disobeying a direct command from God rather than violate one
of the Ten Commandments (Joshua 9). And note the case of Jephthah, who
makes an oath to sacrifice the first thing to come out of his gate,
which turns out to be his daughter. He sacrifices her rather than
violate this commandment (Judges 11). (It is not reckoned legally as
murder. Human sacrifice is forbidden, but it is legally distinguished
from murder, which involves malice.) As horrible as this is (especially
in light of the Aqedah), it conveys the seriousness of this commandment
and of the principle that the Ten Commandments outweigh other laws.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 20:8*](#Contents.html_rExo20_8)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^8^Remember the Sabbath day, to make it holy.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap20_08.jpg){height="22" width="215"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

20:8. **the Sabbath**. On the significance of the Sabbath, see the
comments on Gen 2:2 and 2:3.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 20:9*](#Contents.html_rExo20_9)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^9^Six days you shall labor and do all your work,

![Image](images/Exo_Chap20_09.jpg){height="47" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 20:10*](#Contents.html_rExo20_10)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^10^and the seventh day is a Sabbath to YHWH, your God. You shall not do
any work: you and your son and your daughter, your servant and your maid
and your animal and your alien who is in your gates.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap20_10.jpg){height="95" width="340"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 20:11*](#Contents.html_rExo20_11)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^11^Because for six days YHWH made the skies and the earth, the sea, and
everything that is in them, and He rested on the seventh day. On account
of this, YHWH blessed the Sabbath day and made it holy.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap20_11.jpg){height="95" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 20:12*](#Contents.html_rExo20_12)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^12^Honor your father and your mother, so that your days will be
extended on the land that YHWH, your God, is giving you.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap20_12.jpg){height="68" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

20:12. **your days will be extended on the land**. My father taught me
that the commandment to honor one's parents is the only commandment that
offers a reward.

People have sometimes understood these words to mean that the reward is
that if one honors one's parents one will have a long life. That is not
a correct understanding. The commandment speaks of duration of days *on
the land*. It means that if the people of Israel is composed of
individuals who honor their parents then they will endure in their land.
This suggests the enormous importance of the fifth commandment relative
to the other nine. Those who honor their parents are likely to be people
who will not murder, steal, or violate the other commandments. In thus
preserving the terms of the covenant, they would be deserving of the
covenant promise of the homeland. At the same time, this is a
commandment on the father and mother as well: to be worthy of honor, and
to convey the value of the commandments themselves in such a way that
their children, in honoring the parents, will be moved to keep the
commandments.

This pivotal importance of the commandment to honor one's parents may
account for its position in the order of the commandments: The first
four commandments concern the relationship between humans and God. The
last five concern the relationship between humans and other humans. And
the commandment that comes between---and connects?---these two groups
is: Honor your father and your mother!

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 20:13*](#Contents.html_rExo20_13)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^13^You shall not murder.

You shall not commit adultery.

You shall not steal.

You shall not testify against your neighbor as a lying witness.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap20_13.jpg){height="22" width="107"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

20:13. **murder**. Hebrew, like English, distinguishes between "murder"
and "killing." "Murder" refers only to the taking of *human* life, and
it is subject to the death penalty only when committed with malicious
intent. The cases of manslaughter, killing through negligence, killing
in war, execution for crimes, killing animals, animals killing humans,
and human sacrifice are all treated separately from this in the Torah,
and terms other than "murder" are used. (On the special circumstance of
the one who murders without malice, see Deut 4:41--42.) At the time that
I am writing this, technology has raised new cases, including abortion
and assisted suicide. Whatever one's view of these matters, one cannot
simply claim that they are prohibited by the commandment against murder.
One must argue whether they are consistent with the other cases that
constitute murder or if they belong among the cases that involve taking
life but still are not murder.

20:13. **adultery**. It is commonly said that adultery in the Torah is
defined as sex between a man and a married woman---but not between a
single woman and a married man. The reason for this definition is that
ancient Israel was polygamous, and it was acceptable for a man to have
several wives or concubines (as long as none of them was married to
another man). But now, for those who live in monogamous societies, the
issue for either spouse is the same: violation of their marriage
commitment to each other. Sex with someone other than one's spouse is
equally prohibited for husbands and wives by this commandment.

20:13. **neighbor**. See the explanation of this term on Lev 19:18.

20:13. **lying witness**. Is false testimony not already forbidden by
the command against taking God's name in vain by lying in an oath sworn
to God? This question assumes that testimony in court was in fact taken
under oath in biblical times. But: (1) The Torah does not say that oaths
are required---except for the case of the suspected adulteress in
Numbers 5. (2) The case of the suspected adulteress may well be the
exception that proves that the rule was *not* to take oaths. (3) The
inclusion in the Decalogue of this commandment against being a false
witness appears to me to be itself the evidence that testimony was not
taken under oath, because, if it were, this commandment would indeed be
unnecessary.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 20:14*](#Contents.html_rExo20_14)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^14^You shall not covet your neighbor's house. You shall not covet your
neighbor's wife or his servant or his maid or his ox or his ass or
anything that your neighbor has.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap20_14.jpg){height="22" width="102"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

20:14. **covet**. This comes at the end of the Ten Commandments, and it
is different: it commands regarding an emotional state. This seems like
an extra burden, but it comes as a help. It is saying: Do you want some
help to avoid breaking the other commandments? Very well, don't covet!

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 20:15*](#Contents.html_rExo20_15)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^15^ And all the people were seeing the thunders and the flashes and the
sound of the horn and the mountain smoking. And the people saw, and they
moved and stood at a distance,

![Image](images/Exo_Chap20_15.jpg){height="22" width="103"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

20:15. **the people saw**. The Masoretic Text reads "saw," but the
Greek, Samaritan, and other versions of the text read "were afraid." The
consonants of the Hebrew words for "saw" and for "were afraid" are the
same (![Image](images/page239-01.jpg){height="13" width="25"}). "Were
afraid" is probably correct, because Moses tells the people, "Don't be
afraid," two verses later. This technical point of Hebrew consonants is
important because it establishes the people's state of mind: hearing the
voice of God terrifies them, and that is why they tell Moses, "Let God
not speak with us or else we'll die."

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 20:16*](#Contents.html_rExo20_16)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^16^and they said to Moses, "*You* speak with us so we may listen, but
let God not speak with us or else we'll die."

![Image](images/Exo_Chap20_16.jpg){height="41" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

20:16. ***You* speak with us**. The absence of prophecy in Genesis---in
the way that it is usually meant in the rest of the Hebrew Bible---is
not extraordinary, for prophecy by its very essence relates to a
community; and Genesis, as we have already observed, is primarily about
individuals, not communities. The birth of prophecy in Exodus is related
to the birth of the Israelite community in this book. In Exodus, YHWH
initially sends Moses with His words to the Israelites and to the
Egyptians. That is already a straightforward depiction of prophecy, but,
more crucially, the book proceeds to formalize the prophetic role. When
the Israelites come to Sinai, YHWH personally speaks directly to the
entire nation, pronouncing the Ten Commandments aloud from the sky. The
Israelites' response here is to tell Moses: "You speak with us so we may
listen, but let God not speak with us or else we'll die." From this
moment on, all revelation to Israel is mediated by a prophet. God never
speaks directly to the people again in any book of the *Tanak*.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 20:17*](#Contents.html_rExo20_17)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^17^And Moses said to the people, "Don't be afraid, because God is
coming for the purpose of testing you and for the purpose that his fear
will be on your faces so that you won't sin."

![Image](images/Exo_Chap20_17.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 20:18*](#Contents.html_rExo20_18)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^18^And the people stood at a distance, and Moses went over to the
nimbus where God was.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap20_18.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 20:19*](#Contents.html_rExo20_19)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^19^ And YHWH said to Moses, "You shall say this to the children of
Israel: You have seen that I have spoken with you from the skies.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap20_19.jpg){height="46" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 20:20*](#Contents.html_rExo20_20)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^20^You shall not make gods of silver with me, and you shall not make
gods of gold for yourselves.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap20_20.jpg){height="71" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 20:21*](#Contents.html_rExo20_21)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^21^You shall make an altar of earth for me, and you shall sacrifice on
it your burnt offerings and your peace offerings, your sheep and your
oxen. In every place where I'll have my name commemorated I'll come to
you, and I'll bless you.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap20_21.jpg){height="44" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 20:22*](#Contents.html_rExo20_22)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^22^And if you'll make an altar of stones for me, you shall not make
them cut. When you have elevated your sword over it, then you have
desecrated it.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap20_22.jpg){height="70" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 20:23*](#Contents.html_rExo20_23)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^23^ And you shall not go up by stairs on my altar, so that your nudity
will not be exposed over it."

![Image](images/Exo_Chap20_23.jpg){height="46" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 20:24*](#Contents.html_rExo20_24)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

![Image](images/Exo_Chap20_24.jpg){height="96" width="339"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 20:25*](#Contents.html_rExo20_25)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

![Image](images/Exo_Chap20_25.jpg){height="69" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 20:26*](#Contents.html_rExo20_26)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

![Image](images/Exo_Chap20_26.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

[]{#Exodus21.html}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:1*](#Contents.html_rExo21_1)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## J[UDGMENTS]{.smallcaps1} {.h201}

## **21** {.h2a}

^1^"And these are the judgments that you shall set before them:

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21.jpg){height="24" width="93"}

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_01.jpg){height="24" width="309"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

21:1. **these are the judgments that you shall set**. These three
chapters, Exodus 21--23, are a corpus of law known as the Covenant Code.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:2*](#Contents.html_rExo21_2)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^2^"When you will buy a Hebrew slave, he shall work six years, and in
the seventh he shall go out liberated for free.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_02.jpg){height="53" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

21:2. **Hebrew slave**. In biblical narrative the term "Hebrew" is used
to identify Israelites only when speaking among foreigners. It is not
the standard term for the people, which is rather "Israelite" at first,
and "Jew" later. Its use here in biblical law, where we would have
expected "Israelite slave," probably occurs because this had become a
fixed phrase through assonance: the two words, "slave"
(![Image](images/page241-01.jpg){height="13" width="29"}, *'ebed*) and
"Hebrew" (![Image](images/page241-02.jpg){height="14" width="34"},
*'ibrî*), begin with the same two letters.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:3*](#Contents.html_rExo21_3)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^3^If he will come by himself, he shall go out by himself. If he is a
woman's husband, then his wife shall go out with him.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_03.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:4*](#Contents.html_rExo21_4)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^4^If his master will give him a wife, and she will give birth to sons
or daughters for him, the wife and her children will be her master's,
and he shall go out by himself.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_04.jpg){height="71" width="340"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:5*](#Contents.html_rExo21_5)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^5^And if the slave will *say:* 'I love my master, my wife, and my
children; I won't go out liberated!'

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_05.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:6*](#Contents.html_rExo21_6)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^6^then his master shall bring him over to God and bring him over to the
door or to the doorpost, and his master shall pierce his ear with an
awl, and he shall serve him forever.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_06.jpg){height="73" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:7*](#Contents.html_rExo21_7)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^7^"And if a man will sell his daughter as a maid, she shall not go out
as the slaves go out.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_07.jpg){height="66" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:8*](#Contents.html_rExo21_8)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^8^If she is bad in the eyes of her master who has designated her for
himself, then he shall let her be redeemed. He shall not dominate so as
to sell her to a foreign people in his betrayal of her.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_08.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

21:8. **He shall not dominate**. Recall that part of the curse on woman
in Eden was that "he'll dominate you" (Gen 3:16). But here we are told
of a case in which a man is explicitly not free to dominate a woman.
Even in that worldview that recognized male control of women, there was
an understanding that this control is not unlimited. This point of
respect for women and recognition of a legal right that they possessed
was a first small step in the breakdown of that worldview. And this
passage teaches that a commandment can change or become limited even
within the Torah itself.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:9*](#Contents.html_rExo21_9)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^9^And if he will designate her for his son, he shall treat her
according to the manner of daughters.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_09.jpg){height="22" width="337"}

![Image](images/f0241-01.jpg){height="26" width="61"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:10*](#Contents.html_rExo21_10)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^10^If he will take another for himself, he shall not subtract from her
food, her apparel, and her hygiene.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_10.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

21:10. **hygiene**. Shalom Paul has shown parallels from ancient Near
Eastern texts that indicate that, beside providing food and clothing,
the husband must provide the necessary cosmetics for his wife's
cleanliness and appearance. A second possibility is that the word here,
*'![Image](images/o.jpg){height="11"
width="7"}n![Image](images/a-image.jpg){height="11"
width="8"}t![Image](images/a-image.jpg){height="11" width="8"}h*, is
cognate to Hebrew *m![Image](images/a-image.jpg){height="11"
width="8"}'ôn*, meaning a dwelling, in which case the text means that
the husband provides food, clothing, and shelter. A third possibility is
the traditional view that this refers to sex, but there is no evidence
in support of this.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:11*](#Contents.html_rExo21_11)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^11^And if he will not do these three for her, then she shall go out
free. There is no money.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_11.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:12*](#Contents.html_rExo21_12)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^12^"One who strikes a man, and he dies, he shall be *put to death*.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_12.jpg){height="20" width="184"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:13*](#Contents.html_rExo21_13)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^13^And one who did not scheme, but God conveyed it to his hand, I shall
set a place for you, that he shall flee there.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_13.jpg){height="71" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:14*](#Contents.html_rExo21_14)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^14^But if a man will plot against his neighbor, to kill him with
treachery, you shall take him from my altar to die.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_14.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

21:14. **plot**. The verb *zyd* elsewhere refers to cooking (e.g.,
Jacob's cooking the stew in Gen 25:29). Here it refers to someone's
committing murder with intent, rather than manslaughter. (It also refers
to the Egyptians' scheme to enslave the Israelites in Exod 18:11.) Like
the English expression "to cook up," the Hebrew word has the range of
both cooking and plotting.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:15*](#Contents.html_rExo21_15)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^15^"And one who strikes his father and his mother shall be *put to
death*.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_15.jpg){height="20" width="206"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:16*](#Contents.html_rExo21_16)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^16^"And one who steals a man and has sold him, or he was found in his
hand, *will be put to death*.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_16.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:17*](#Contents.html_rExo21_17)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^17^"And one who curses his father and his mother shall be *put to
death*.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_17.jpg){height="50" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:18*](#Contents.html_rExo21_18)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^18^"And if people will quarrel, and a man strikes his neighbor with a
stone or with a fist, and he does not die, and he falls to bed,

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_18.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:19*](#Contents.html_rExo21_19)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^19^if he will get up and walk himself outside on his staff, then the
one who struck will be innocent. Only he shall compensate for his
staying home and shall *have him healed*.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_19.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:20*](#Contents.html_rExo21_20)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^20^"And if a man will strike his slave or his maid with a rod, and he
dies by his hand, he shall be *avenged*.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_20.jpg){height="71" width="340"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:21*](#Contents.html_rExo21_21)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^21^Just: if he will stand for a day or two days, he shall not be
avenged, because he is his money.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_21.jpg){height="49" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

21:21. **stand**. If he will not die immediately but will live for a day
or two after being struck.

21:21. **he is his money**. The slave is the master's property. This
indicates that the master is empowered to strike his slave, but he is
not free to be the immediate cause of the slave's death.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:22*](#Contents.html_rExo21_22)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^22^"And if people will fight, and they strike a pregnant woman, and her
children go out, and there will not be an injury, he shall be
*penalized* according to what the woman's husband will impose on him,
and he will give it by the judges.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_22.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

21:22. **her children go out, and there will not be an injury**. This
can mean: if the woman has a miscarriage, but there is no other physical
damage to the woman herself. Or it can mean: if the woman goes into
labor, and the child is born without any physical damage. Scholars
generally understand it to be the former.

This passage has been cited in debates over abortion. However, the
issues in this case are different, and the wording is so complex, and
its meaning is so uncertain, that one should be extremely hesitant to
use this case for either side of that debate.

21:22. **he will give it by the judges**. The meaning is uncertain, but
the penalty is determined and enforced in some way involving both the
husband and the judges.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:23*](#Contents.html_rExo21_23)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^23^And if there will be an injury, then you shall give a life for a
life,

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_23.jpg){height="20" width="293"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

21:23--24. **a life for a life, an eye for an eye** ...The inclusion of
"a burn for a burn" here---in a case in which "burn" has no apparent
relevance---indicates that this list comes to apply a *fundamental
principle* to this case. Concerning the eye-for-an-eye principle, see
the comment on Lev 24:20.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:24*](#Contents.html_rExo21_24)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^24^an eye for an eye, a tooth for a tooth, a hand for a hand, a foot
for a foot,

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_24.jpg){height="44" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:25*](#Contents.html_rExo21_25)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^25^a burn for a burn, a wound for a wound, a hurt for a hurt.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_25.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:26*](#Contents.html_rExo21_26)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^26^"And if a man will strike his slave's eye or his maid's eye and
destroy it, he shall let him go, liberated, for his eye.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_26.jpg){height="46" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

21:26. **liberated, for his eye**. Even in a system that allows slavery,
a master does not own the slave's body. The master can require work from
a slave, but damaging the slave's eye or tooth steps over the line, and
the master no longer owns the slave. This reminds us also of the
Egyptian enslavement of Israel. The Egyptians are not criticized for
having slaves, but rather for the way they *treat* the slaves: "They
degraded them.... They made them serve with harshness ... they made
their lives bitter with hard work ..." They killed the male infants. The
Torah did not bring about the end of slavery by abolishing it. It
established principles regarding slaves' dignity, rights, and treatment.
And these gradually contributed to humans' own rejection of slavery in
much (though not yet all) of the world. This is a crucial point itself:
that some of the laws of the Torah command things outright, while others
lead humans to grow and change themselves.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:27*](#Contents.html_rExo21_27)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^27^And if he will knock out his slave's tooth or his maid's tooth, he
shall let him go liberated for his tooth.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_27.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:28*](#Contents.html_rExo21_28)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^28^"And if an ox will gore a man or a woman and they die, the ox shall
be *stoned*, and its meat shall not be eaten---and the ox's owner is
innocent.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_28.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:29*](#Contents.html_rExo21_29)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^29^And if it was a goring ox from the day before yesterday, and it had
been so testified to its owner, and he did not watch it, and it killed a
man or a woman, the ox will be stoned, and its owner will be put to
death as well.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_29.jpg){height="74" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

21:29. **from the day before yesterday**. Meaning: this ox had gored
before.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:30*](#Contents.html_rExo21_30)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^30^If a ransom will be set on him, then he shall give everything that
will be set on him for the redemption of his life.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_30.jpg){height="44" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:31*](#Contents.html_rExo21_31)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^31^Whether it will gore a son or it will gore a daughter, according to
this judgment shall be done to it.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_31.jpg){height="22" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

21:31--32. **a son or ... a daughter ... a slave or a maid**. No
distinction is made on the basis of sex; a distinction is made on the
basis of class: free versus slave.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:32*](#Contents.html_rExo21_32)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^32^If the ox will gore a slave or a maid, he shall pay thirty silver
shekels to his owner, and the ox shall be stoned.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_32.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:33*](#Contents.html_rExo21_33)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^33^"And if a man will open a pit, or if a man will dig a pit, and he
will not cover it, and an ox or an ass will fall there,

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_33.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:34*](#Contents.html_rExo21_34)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^34^the owner of the pit shall pay; he shall pay back money to its
owner, and the dead one will be his.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_34.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:35*](#Contents.html_rExo21_35)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^35^And if a man's ox will strike his neighbor's ox, and it dies, then
they shall sell the live ox and split the money for it, and they shall
split the dead one as well.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_35.jpg){height="69" width="340"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:36*](#Contents.html_rExo21_36)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^36^Or: if it was known that it was a goring ox from the day before
yesterday, and its owner did not watch it, he shall *pay* an ox for the
ox, and the dead one shall be his.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_36.jpg){height="94" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 21:37*](#Contents.html_rExo21_37)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^37^"If a man will steal an ox or a sheep and slaughter it or sell it,
he shall pay five oxen for the ox and four sheep for the sheep.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap21_37.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

[]{#Exodus22.html}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 22:1*](#Contents.html_rExo22_1)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **22** {.h2a}

^1^"If the thief will be found while breaking in and will be struck, and
he dies, there is no blood for him.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap22_01.jpg){height="44" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

22:1. **no blood for him**. Meaning: there is no bloodguilt for killing
him. The person who killed the thief who was in the act of breaking in
(at night) cannot be held legally responsible for the death.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 22:2*](#Contents.html_rExo22_2)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^2^If the sun has risen on him there is blood for him. He shall *pay*.
If he does not have it, then he shall be sold for his theft.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap22_02.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

22.2. **If the sun has risen on him there is blood for him**. If the
theft was in the daytime---which is less frightening and when escape,
help, and witnesses are more possible---then one is legally responsible
if one kills the thief.

22:2. **He shall *pay***. If the thief is caught, not killed, he must
pay for his theft.

22:2. **If he does not have it, then he shall be sold for his theft**.
If the thief does not have the money to pay for his theft, then he is
sold into servitude.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 22:3*](#Contents.html_rExo22_3)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^3^If the theft will be *found* in his hand---from ox to ass to
sheep---alive, he shall pay two.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap22_03.jpg){height="67" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 22:4*](#Contents.html_rExo22_4)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^4^"If a man will have a field or a vineyard grazed, and he will let his
beast go and graze in another person's field, he shall pay the best of
his field and the best of his vineyard.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap22_04.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/f0245-01.jpg){height="26" width="84"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 22:5*](#Contents.html_rExo22_5)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^5^"If fire will break out and find thorns and stacked grain or standing
grain or a field is consumed, the one who set the blaze shall *pay*.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap22_05.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 22:6*](#Contents.html_rExo22_6)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^6^"If a man will give his neighbor money or items to watch, and it will
be stolen from the man's house, if the thief will be found he shall pay
two.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap22_06.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 22:7*](#Contents.html_rExo22_7)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^7^If the thief will not be found, then the owner of the house shall be
brought near to God: that he has not put out his hand into his
neighbor's property.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap22_07.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

22:7. **brought near to God: that he has not put out his hand**. The
wording (starting with *'im*) plus the notice of being brought "near to
God" indicate that he is to take an oath that he has not taken the
missing property for which he was responsible. The wording occurs again
in 22:10 with an explicit reference to an oath.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 22:8*](#Contents.html_rExo22_8)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^8^"Over every case of an offense, over an ox, over an ass, over a
sheep, over clothing, over every loss about which one will say that
'This is it,' the word of the two of them shall come to God. The one
whom God will implicate shall pay two to his neighbor.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap22_08.jpg){height="98" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

22:8. **implicate**. The verb here is plural. Some therefore take its
subject, *'![Image](images/e-image.jpg){height="12"
width="7"}l![Image](images/o.jpg){height="11" width="7"}hîm*, not to
refer to God here and in the preceding verse. (See Rashi and Ibn Ezra.)
Rather, it is understood to refer to the judges before whom the case is
brought.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 22:9*](#Contents.html_rExo22_9)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^9^"If a man will give an ass or an ox or a sheep or any animal to his
neighbor to watch, and it dies or is injured or is seized---no one
seeing---

![Image](images/Exo_Chap22_09.jpg){height="70" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 22:10*](#Contents.html_rExo22_10)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^10^an oath of YHWH shall be between the two of them: that he has not
put out his hand into his neighbor's property; and its owner shall take
it, and he shall not pay.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap22_10.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

22:10. **he shall not pay**. The person who was watching the animal does
not pay anything to the animal's owner.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 22:11*](#Contents.html_rExo22_11)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^11^And if it will be *stolen* from him, he shall pay its owner.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap22_11.jpg){height="22" width="280"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 22:12*](#Contents.html_rExo22_12)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^12^If it will be *torn*, he shall bring it in witness; he shall not pay
for the torn one.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap22_12.jpg){height="40" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

22:12. **witness**. Rashi understands this to mean that he brings
witnesses. Ibn Ezra suggests that it means that he brings a portion of
the torn animal as evidence. The latter is the understanding of most
contemporary translators.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 22:13*](#Contents.html_rExo22_13)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^13^And if a man will ask it of his neighbor, and it is injured or dies,
its owner not being with it, he shall *pay*.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap22_13.jpg){height="50" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

22:13. **ask**. That is, borrow.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 22:14*](#Contents.html_rExo22_14)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^14^If its owner is with it, he shall not pay. If it was hired, he has
its hire coming to him.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap22_14.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 22:15*](#Contents.html_rExo22_15)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^15^"And if a man will deceive a virgin who is not betrothed, and he
lies with her, he shall *espouse her by a bride-price* to him as a wife.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap22_15.jpg){height="68" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 22:16*](#Contents.html_rExo22_16)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^16^If her father will *refuse* to give her to him, he shall weigh out
money corresponding to the bride-price of virgins.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap22_16.jpg){height="46" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 22:17*](#Contents.html_rExo22_17)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^17^"You shall not let a witch live.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap22_17.jpg){height="24" width="171"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

22:17. **a witch**. Why does the text condemn only women and not male
sorcerers? Males are identified as practicing magic as well, as in the
case of the Egyptian magicians (Exod 7:11). The feminine form of this
word occurs only here out of the entire *Tanak*. And the Septuagint has
the masculine plural (which is used to include both men and women) here
instead of the Masoretic Text's feminine singular. This suggests that
the original text forbids the practice of magic by either men or women
(sorcerers or witches). Note also: the text does not deny that magic
*works*. (On the contrary, the Egyptian magicians can turn staffs into
snakes.) It forbids the *practice* of magic.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 22:18*](#Contents.html_rExo22_18)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^18^"Anyone who lies with an animal shall be *put to death*.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap22_18.jpg){height="22" width="253"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 22:19*](#Contents.html_rExo22_19)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^19^"One who sacrifices to gods shall be completely destroyed---except
to YHWH alone.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap22_19.jpg){height="22" width="286"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

22:19. **completely destroyed**. Hebrew
*![Image](images/hdot.jpg){height="15" width="7"}erem* refers to the
rule of destroying an individual or community along with all his family,
animals, and possessions and thus dedicating them to the deity rather
than profiting in any way from them.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 22:20*](#Contents.html_rExo22_20)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^20^"And you shall not persecute an alien, and you shall not oppress
him, because you were aliens in the land of Egypt.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap22_20.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 22:21*](#Contents.html_rExo22_21)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^21^"You shall not degrade any widow or orphan.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap22_21.jpg){height="23" width="220"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 22:22*](#Contents.html_rExo22_22)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^22^If you will *degrade* them, when they will *cry out* to me I'll
*hear* their cry,

![Image](images/Exo_Chap22_22.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 22:23*](#Contents.html_rExo22_23)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^23^and my anger will flare, and I'll kill you with a sword, and *your*
wives will be widows and *your* children orphans!

![Image](images/Exo_Chap22_23.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 22:24*](#Contents.html_rExo22_24)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^24^"If you will lend money to my people, to the poor who is with you,
you shall not be like a creditor to him; you shall not impose interest
on him.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap22_24.jpg){height="49" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 22:25*](#Contents.html_rExo22_25)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^25^If you *take* your neighbor's clothing as *security*, you shall give
it back to him by the sunset,

![Image](images/Exo_Chap22_25.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 22:26*](#Contents.html_rExo22_26)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^26^because it is his only apparel; it is his clothing for his skin. In
what will he sleep? And it will be, when he will cry out to me, that I
shall listen, because I am gracious.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap22_26.jpg){height="64" width="338"}

![Image](images/f0247-01.jpg){height="26" width="90"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 22:27*](#Contents.html_rExo22_27)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^27^"You shall not blaspheme God, and you shall not curse a chieftain
among your people.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap22_27.jpg){height="25" width="310"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

22:27. **chieftain**. Hebrew *n![Image](images/a-image.jpg){height="11"
width="8"}![Image](images/s1.jpg){height="11" width="7"}î'*. Elsewhere
in the Torah the text refers to the chieftain of a tribe (Num 2:3--29)
or of a priestly family (Num 3:24--35) or of a city (Gen 34:2). Here it
may allude to a king of Israel, "a chieftain among your people" (as in
1 Kings 11:34; Ezek 34:24), or it may mean any of these leaders.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 22:28*](#Contents.html_rExo22_28)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^28^"You shall not delay your fulfillment and your flowing.

"You shall give me the firstborn of your sons.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap22_28.jpg){height="22" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

22:28. **fulfillment and your flowing**. Everyone is unsure of what this
means.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 22:29*](#Contents.html_rExo22_29)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^29^"You shall do this to your ox and to your sheep: Seven days it will
be with its mother. On the eighth day you shall give it to me.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap22_29.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 22:30*](#Contents.html_rExo22_30)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^30^"And you shall be holy people to me.

"And you shall not eat meat in the field that is torn. You shall throw
it to the dog.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap22_30.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

22:30. **meat that is torn**. This refers to the meat of an animal that
was killed by another animal rather than having been killed in the
sacred manner prescribed by the law. The word for "torn" (Hebrew
*t![Image](images/e-image.jpg){height="12"
width="7"}r![Image](images/e1.jpg){height="14"
width="6"}p![Image](images/a-image.jpg){height="11" width="8"}h*) later
came to be used for any prohibited foods.

[]{#Exodus23.html}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 23:1*](#Contents.html_rExo23_1)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **23** {.h2a}

^1^"You shall not bring up a false report. Do not join your hand with a
wicked person to be a malevolent witness.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap23_01.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 23:2*](#Contents.html_rExo23_2)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^2^You shall not be following many to do bad. And you shall not testify
about a dispute to bend following many, to bend it.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap23_02.jpg){height="68" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

23:2. **not be following many to do bad**. Do not follow a group, a
crowd, a majority if what they are doing is wrong. Do not do it for
acceptance, for the secure feeling of being in a group, or for the
sadistic pleasure of being able to exclude someone. It is easy to be
hurtful in a group. And it is easy to keep silent when one's group does
harm---or when its leaders do harm from their position, which derives
its power from the group. All of this is forbidden. It is utterly
inconsistent with the Torah's conceptions of what a human should be and
how one should behave toward other human beings. This comment should be
unnecessary.

23:2. **to bend following many, to bend it**. When giving testimony or
when sitting in judgment, do not incline toward the majority of those
involved in the case or toward the majority of the public. To "bend"
toward them is to "bend" justice.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 23:3*](#Contents.html_rExo23_3)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^3^And you shall not favor a weak person in his dispute.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap23_03.jpg){height="22" width="196"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

23:3. **you shall not favor a weak person**. *Of course* one should not
favor the majority, or the rich, or the famous, or the powerful. *Of
course* we should not bend the law for bad motives. We are reminded that
we also must not bend the law or the truth even for good motives, such
as feelings of compassion for the poor. Compare Lev 19:15.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 23:4*](#Contents.html_rExo23_4)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^4^"If you will happen upon your enemy's ox or his ass straying, you
shall *bring it back* to him.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap23_04.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 23:5*](#Contents.html_rExo23_5)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^5^If you will see the ass of someone who hates you sagging under its
burden, and you would hold back from helping him: you shall *help* with
him.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap23_05.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

23:5. **you would hold back from helping him**. That is, you would hold
back from helping your adversary.

23:5. **help**. The Hebrew *'zb* (to leave) is difficult. One
possibility, suggested here, is to read the word as *'zr* (to help).
Alternatively, it may mean: "If you will see the ass of someone who
hates you sagging under its burden, then you shall hold back from
leaving it for him; you shall leave it *with* him." That is, do not
leave it for him to handle himself; do what is necessary to assist and
leave it back with him. By any reading, though, the main point is that
one must be of help, even to someone who bears one ill will---perhaps
*especially* to someone who bears ill will.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 23:6*](#Contents.html_rExo23_6)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^6^"You shall not bend the judgment of your poor in his dispute.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap23_06.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

23:6. **your poor**. It is natural to use the phrase "*your* enemy," as
in the preceding verses, but we would not readily expect "*your* poor"
linguistically. But the issue is not linguistic. It is moral. The poor
are not neutral as a group. They are *your* poor, your responsibility.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 23:7*](#Contents.html_rExo23_7)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^7^"You shall keep far from a word of a lie, and do not kill an innocent
and a virtuous person, because I shall not vindicate a wicked person.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap23_07.jpg){height="46" width="340"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 23:8*](#Contents.html_rExo23_8)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^8^"And you shall not take a bribe, because bribery will blind those who
can see and will undermine the words of the virtuous.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap23_08.jpg){height="47" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 23:9*](#Contents.html_rExo23_9)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^9^"And you shall not oppress an alien---since you know the alien's
*soul*, because you were aliens in the land of Egypt.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap23_09.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

23:9. **you know the alien's *soul***. This verse, commanding not to
oppress an alien residing among Israel, repeats a commandment that has
already been given just a little earlier, in 22:20. But it says it even
more eloquently. You have been aliens, so you *know* the alien. You know
how he or she *feels* oppression inside.

Few of the laws in this code have an extra line of explanation like
this. Notably, another law protecting aliens in the preceding chapter
also adds a line of explanation (22:20). Again, the treatment of a
non-Israelite who is within Israel's power is singled out in a special
way.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 23:10*](#Contents.html_rExo23_10)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^10^"And six years you shall sow your land and gather its produce;

![Image](images/Exo_Chap23_10.jpg){height="45" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 23:11*](#Contents.html_rExo23_11)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^11^and the seventh: you shall let it lie fallow and leave it, and your
people's indigent will eat, and what they leave the animal of the field
will eat. You shall do this to your vineyard, to your olives.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap23_11.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 23:12*](#Contents.html_rExo23_12)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^12^"Six days you shall do the things you do, and on the seventh day you
shall cease, so that your ox and your ass will rest, and your maid's son
and the alien will be refreshed.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap23_12.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 23:13*](#Contents.html_rExo23_13)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^13^"And you shall be watchful in everything that I have said to you.
And you shall not commemorate the name of other gods. Let it not be
heard on your mouth.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap23_13.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

23:13. **commemorate**. Other translations take this to mean "mention,"
i.e., that one cannot even say the name of another god---but compare
Exod 20:21, where this same word appears to refer to mentioning YHWH's
name *institutionally*. I therefore understand this verse to mean that
one is commanded not to say another god's name in a setting of worship.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 23:14*](#Contents.html_rExo23_14)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^14^"You shall celebrate three pilgrimages for me in the year:

![Image](images/Exo_Chap23_14.jpg){height="22" width="238"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 23:15*](#Contents.html_rExo23_15)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^15^"You shall observe the Festival of Unleavened Bread. Seven days you
shall eat unleavened bread, as I commanded you, at the appointed time of
the month of Abib, because you went out of Egypt in it. And none shall
appear before me empty.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap23_15.jpg){height="93" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

23:15. **empty**. Meaning: empty-handed. One must go to the designated
place and bring a sacrifice.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 23:16*](#Contents.html_rExo23_16)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^16^"And the Festival of Harvest, the first fruits of what you do, of
what you will sow in the field.

"And the Festival of Gathering, at the end of the year, when you gather
what you have done from the field.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap23_16.jpg){height="75" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 23:17*](#Contents.html_rExo23_17)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^17^"Three times in the year every male of yours shall appear in front
of the Lord YHWH.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap23_17.jpg){height="67" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 23:18*](#Contents.html_rExo23_18)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^18^"You shall not offer the blood of my sacrifice on leavened bread.
And the fat of my festival shall not remain until morning.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap23_18.jpg){height="48" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 23:19*](#Contents.html_rExo23_19)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^19^"You shall bring the first of the firstfruits of your land to the
house of YHWH, your God.

"You shall not cook a kid in its mother's milk.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap23_19.jpg){height="70" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

23:19. **the house of YHWH**. This is a reference to the Temple, the
building that will one day be the only sanctioned place of sacrifice and
the central place of worship. The first house of YHWH will be located at
Shiloh. The second and third will be located at Jerusalem.

23:19. **a kid in its mother's milk**. A Canaanite (Ugaritic) text may
picture the chief god, El, having kid cooked in milk. This biblical law
may thus be a prohibition against eating something that was regarded as
a food for a deity. This explanation is uncertain because the Canaanite
text does not specify *mother's* milk, and the word there that is read
as "kid" is partly effaced. Alternatively, the biblical law may be a
