moral one, consistent with other biblical law, which prohibits
sacrificing an ox or sheep on the same day as its offspring (Lev 22:28)
or taking a mother bird along with her eggs in the wild (Deut 22:6--7).

This law came to be taken in later Jewish practice to amount to a
complete separation of meat and dairy consumption. This practice is not
commanded in the Torah but is rather based on postbiblical rabbinic
rulings.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 23:20*](#Contents.html_rExo23_20)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^20^"Here, I'm sending an angel ahead of you to watch over you on the
way and to bring you to the place that I've prepared.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap23_20.jpg){height="46" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

23:20. **I'm sending an angel ahead of you**. This will be repeated
twice (32:34; 33:2). It is another recurrence of language from the
accounts of the patriarchs: Abraham told his servant that God will send
"His angel ahead of you," which would lead to the success of his mission
to get a wife for Isaac (Gen 24:7; see also 32:4). That is now a
reminder that the preceding angel on a journey is an assurance and
protection.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 23:21*](#Contents.html_rExo23_21)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^21^Be watchful in front of him and listen to his voice. Don't rebel
against him, because he will not bear your offense, because my name is
within him.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap23_21.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

23:21. **rebel**. The Hebrew, *tamm![Image](images/e1.jpg){height="14"
width="6"}r*, here in the Masoretic Text has the form of a *Hiphil* of
the root *mrr*, meaning "to make bitter." But these vowel points are
generally understood to be incorrect. The word should rather be read as
*temer*, a form of the root *mrh*, meaning "to rebel," which is how the
Greek reads. The theme of rebellion will figure strongly in the events
to come. Alternatively, one might argue that it is an allusion to the
story of the bitter waters at Marah, which was the people's first
rebellion after crossing the Red Sea.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 23:22*](#Contents.html_rExo23_22)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^22^But if you will *listen* to his voice and do everything that I
speak, then I'll be an enemy to your enemies and an opponent to your
opponents.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap23_22.jpg){height="72" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 23:23*](#Contents.html_rExo23_23)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^23^When my angel will go ahead of you and will bring you to the Amorite
and the Hittite and the Perizzite and the Canaanite, the Hivite, and the
Jebusite, and I obliterate them,

![Image](images/Exo_Chap23_23.jpg){height="76" width="368"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 23:24*](#Contents.html_rExo23_24)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^24^you shall not bow to their gods, and you shall not serve them, and
you shall not do like the things they do; but you shall *tear* them down
and *shatter* their pillars.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap23_24.jpg){height="76" width="366"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 23:25*](#Contents.html_rExo23_25)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^25^And you will serve YHWH, your God, and He will bless your bread and
your water, and I shall turn sickness away from within you.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap23_25.jpg){height="77" width="370"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 23:26*](#Contents.html_rExo23_26)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^26^There won't be a bereaved woman and an infertile woman in your land.
I shall fulfill the number of your days.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap23_26.jpg){height="50" width="366"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 23:27*](#Contents.html_rExo23_27)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^27^I shall have my terror go ahead of you, and I shall throw all the
people against whom you come into tumult, and I shall give all your
enemies to you by the back.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap23_27.jpg){height="81" width="366"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

23:27. **by the back**. The enemies will turn their backs and flee. This
image of Israel's enemies occurs in 2 Sam 22:41 and Ps 18:41. As a
terrifying lesson, it is reversed in a case of a serious violation by an
Israelite of a divine command, and then it is Israel that turns its back
to its enemies (Josh 7:8,12).

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 23:28*](#Contents.html_rExo23_28)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^28^And I shall send the hornet ahead of you, and it will drive out the
Hivite, the Canaanite, and the Hittite from in front of you.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap23_28.jpg){height="50" width="366"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 23:29*](#Contents.html_rExo23_29)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^29^I won't drive them out from in front of you in one year, or else the
land will be a devastation, and the animal of the field will be many at
you.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap23_29.jpg){height="77" width="366"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 23:30*](#Contents.html_rExo23_30)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^30^Little by little I shall drive them out from in front of you, until
you will be fruitful, and you will have a legacy of the land.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap23_30.jpg){height="50" width="366"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 23:31*](#Contents.html_rExo23_31)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^31^And I shall set your border from the Red Sea to the sea of the
Philistines and from the wilderness to the river, because I shall put
the residents of the land in your hand, and you'll drive them out from
in front of you.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap23_31.jpg){height="93" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

23:31. **the Red Sea**. This refers to the *eastern* arm of the Red Sea,
presently the site of Eilat. The promised territory of Israel is never
conceived of in the Torah as extending all the way across the Sinai
Peninsula to the western arm of the Red Sea. It stops at the "river of
Egypt," the Wadi el-'Arîsh, which is north of the eastern arm of the Red
Sea.

23:31. **the sea of the Philistines**. The Mediterranean Sea. The
Philistines came across the Mediterranean from the Greek islands. They
lived in cities near the Mediterranean coast (Ashdod, Ashkelon, Ekron,
Gath, and Gaza).

23:31. **the river**. This is commonly understood to refer to the
Euphrates.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 23:32*](#Contents.html_rExo23_32)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^32^You shall not make a covenant with them and with their gods.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap23_32.jpg){height="50" width="367"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 23:33*](#Contents.html_rExo23_33)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^33^They shall not live in your land, in case they will make you sin
against me when you'll serve their gods, because it will be a trap for
you."

![Image](images/Exo_Chap23_33.jpg){height="50" width="366"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

[]{#Exodus24.html}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 24:1*](#Contents.html_rExo24_1)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **24** {.h2a}

^1^And He said to Moses, "Come up to YHWH: you and Aaron, Nadab and
Abihu, and seventy of Israel's elders, and bow from a distance.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap24_1.jpg){height="70" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

24:1. **from a distance**. This account has a multitude of parallels to
the account of the binding of Isaac (Genesis 22): Moses says the same
words to the elders that Abraham says to the servant boys: "Sit here ...
we'll come back to you." The Exodus account has servant boys
(*n![Image](images/a1.jpg){.block height="12"
width="8"}'![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11"
width="8"}rîm*) as well. Both accounts use the term "from a distance"
(*m![Image](images/e1.jpg){.block height="14"
width="6"}r![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11"
width="8"}![Image](images/hdat.jpg){.block height="17"
width="14"}![Image](images/o.jpg){.block height="11" width="7"}q*). Both
use the term "to bow" (*hišta![Image](images/hdat.jpg){.block
height="17" width="14"}![Image](images/a1.jpg){.block height="12"
width="8"}wôt*). Both Moses and Abraham come up a mountain. Both have a
burnt offering (*ha'![Image](images/a1.jpg){.block height="12"
width="8"}lôt 'ôl![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11"
width="8"}h*). The two accounts share a chain of ten verbs: "and he
said," "and he took ... and he set," "and he got up early," "and he
built an altar," "and he put out his hand," "and he/it was," "and
he/they got up," "and he/they came," "and he/they saw." All these
parallels alert us to the fact that some important connection exists
here. The culminating parallels indicate what that connection is:
Abraham is rewarded at the end "because you did this thing"; and the
people in Exodus promise that "We'll do all the things." Abraham is
rewarded because "you listened to my voice"; and Exodus reports that
"they said with one voice," and they say, "we'll listen." So (1) this is
a reminder that the merit of Abraham remains the basis on which all of
this now happens. (2) This is a fulfillment of promises made following
Abraham's acts of extraordinary obedience. And (3) Abraham's obedience
is an example for the people to emulate.

The contrast is striking as well. Abraham's obedience is the centerpiece
of the first story. In this second story, the people will soon commit
their worst act of *dis*obedience, the golden calf event.

Note also: The first story is at "the mountain of YHWH." The second is
at "the mountain of God." And both *state* that they are about divine
appearances. The divine appearances mark the sacredness of the two
mountain locations. The "mountain of God" is Horeb/Sinai. The identity
of the "mountain of YHWH" is uncertain, although elsewhere it refers to
the location of the Temple in Jerusalem. The parallels of these two
stories connect the mountain where the greatest revelation takes place
to the mountain where the divine promise was confirmed in the past---and
where the Temple will stand in the future. (The location of the Temple
is identified with the place of the binding of Isaac---Moriah---in 2 Chr
3:1.) The two sacred mountains are connected; the patriarch and his
descendants are connected; past, present, and future are connected---all
in the embroidery of the Torah's wording.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 24:2*](#Contents.html_rExo24_2)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^2^And Moses will come over alone to YHWH, and they shall not come over,
and the people shall not come up with him."

![Image](images/Exo_Chap24_2.jpg){height="51" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 24:3*](#Contents.html_rExo24_3)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^3^And Moses came and told the people all of YHWH's words and all the
judgments. And all the people answered, one voice, and they said, "We'll
do all the things that YHWH has spoken."

![Image](images/Exo_Chap24_3.jpg){height="96" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 24:4*](#Contents.html_rExo24_4)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^4^And Moses wrote all of YHWH's words. And he got up early in the
morning and built an altar below the mountain and twelve pillars for
twelve tribes of Israel.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap24_4.jpg){height="95" width="340"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 24:5*](#Contents.html_rExo24_5)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^5^And he sent young men of the children of Israel, and they made burnt
offerings, and they made peace-offering sacrifices to YHWH: bulls.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap24_5.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 24:6*](#Contents.html_rExo24_6)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^6^And Moses took half of the blood and set it in basins and threw half
of the blood on the altar.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap24_6.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 24:7*](#Contents.html_rExo24_7)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^7^And he took the scroll of the covenant and read in the people's ears.
And they said, "We'll do everything that YHWH has spoken, and we'll
listen."

![Image](images/Exo_Chap24_7.jpg){height="70" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

24:7. **We'll do ... and we'll listen**. It has been noted that the word
order here seems backward: doesn't one have to listen to the command
before one can do it?! The promises to do and to listen come together in
the order of this sentence in the Hebrew, so it may be that they were a
known formula, constituting an oath of absolute obedience. But I
understand them to be separate: The people's promise *to do* the
commandments is a repetition of what they had said before in v. 3. They
repeat it formally now because Moses has just read them the written
document. The people's promise *to listen* is, as Rashbam wrote, a
further promise, a commitment to follow the things that their God will
say to them in the future. In Hebrew as in English, words for
"listening" also imply obeying, as in: "Listen to your mother!" "Do you
hear me?!" (As an alternative and more banal explanation of the
seemingly backward order: the Septuagint has the two words in reverse
order.)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 24:8*](#Contents.html_rExo24_8)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^8^And Moses took the blood and threw it on the people, and he said,
"Here is the blood of the covenant that YHWH has made with you regarding
all these things."

![Image](images/Exo_Chap24_8.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 24:9*](#Contents.html_rExo24_9)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^9^And Moses and Aaron, Nadab and Abihu, and seventy of Israel's elders
went up.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap24_9.jpg){height="46" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 24:10*](#Contents.html_rExo24_10)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^10^And they saw the God of Israel. And below His feet it was like a
structure of sapphire brick and like the essence of the skies for
clarity.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap24_10.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

24:10--11. **And they saw God. ... And they envisioned God**. Some have
taken this to mean that they literally, physically see God. But, as Ibn
Ezra properly observed, the verb for "they saw" (Hebrew *r'h*) is also
used to refer to seeing *in a vision*, as when Isaiah says, "I saw the
Lord sitting on a throne" (Isa 6:1). This meaning of "they saw" here is
confirmed by the parallel use in v. 11 of the word "envisioned" (Hebrew
*![Image](images/hdat.jpg){.block height="17" width="14"}zh*), which
regularly means to have a vision (as, for example in Isa 1:1). Also,
this is consistent with the idea that no one ever sees the actual form
of God except Moses on a single occasion. This idea is understood
throughout the *Tanak*, without exception. (On the distinction between
vision and regular experience, see *The Hidden Face of God*, pp. 17--18,
62--63.)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 24:11*](#Contents.html_rExo24_11)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^11^And He did not put out His hand to the chiefs of the children of
Israel. And they envisioned God. And they ate and drank.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap24_11.jpg){height="65" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

24:11. **He did not put out His hand**. Those who take the passage to
mean that this group actually sees God understand "He did not put out
His hand" to mean that God does not kill them for seeing God. But the
text says "He did not put out His hand *to* the chiefs ..." The phrase
"to put out one's hand" followed by the preposition "to" (Hebrew *'el*)
never refers to killing in the *Tanak*. (When referring to killing, the
phrase occurs with the preposition *b![Image](images/e1.jpg){.block
height="14" width="6"}*.) And, in any case, there are other cases of the
expression of God putting out a hand to humans that are meant
positively. In Ps 144:7, this refers to God *saving* someone. And there
is an even closer parallel in Jer 1:9, where it takes place in a
*vision* and refers to God reaching out to touch Jeremiah's mouth to
enable him to speak. Here in Exodus, perhaps it means that in their
vision they see but are not touched by God.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 24:12*](#Contents.html_rExo24_12)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^12^And YHWH said to Moses, "Come up to me, to the mountain, and be
there, and I'll give you stone tablets and the instruction and the
commandment that I've written to instruct them."

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

[*Exodus 24:13*](#Contents.html_rExo24_13)

[]{#Exodus25.html}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 25:1*](#Contents.html_rExo25_1)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## D[ONATION]{.smallcaps1} {.h201}

## **25** {.h2a}

^1^And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying,

![Image](images/Exo_Chap25.jpg){height="20" width="89"}

![Image](images/Exo_Chap25_1.jpg){height="22" width="230"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 25:2*](#Contents.html_rExo25_2)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^2^"Speak to the children of Israel that they shall take a donation for
me. You shall take my donation from every man whose heart will move him.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap25_2.jpg){height="70" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

25:2. **Speak to the children of Israel that they shall take a donation
for me. You shall take my donation from every man whose heart will move
him**. The command is to all the people---to take from individuals, who
are themselves part of the people. It is strange linguistically. The
whole people is not commanded to give. People only have to give if they
are individually moved to do so. But the whole people is commanded to
collect it. It is like when someone says that he gave a gift to his
congregation when he himself is a member of the congregation.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 25:3*](#Contents.html_rExo25_3)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^3^And this is the donation that you shall take from them: gold and
silver and bronze

![Image](images/Exo_Chap25_3.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 25:4*](#Contents.html_rExo25_4)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^4^and blue and purple and scarlet and linen and goats' hair

![Image](images/Exo_Chap25_4.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 25:5*](#Contents.html_rExo25_5)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^5^and rams' skins dyed red and leather skins and acacia wood,

![Image](images/Exo_Chap25_5.jpg){height="47" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

25:5. **leather**. Hebrew: skins that are
*t![Image](images/e-image.jpg){.block height="12"
width="7"}![Image](images/hdat.jpg){.block height="17"
width="14"}![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11"
width="8"}šîm*. No one knows what this means. It has been understood to
be dolphin skins, badger skins, goatskins, and skins of a particular
color. It is cognate to an Arabic word meaning dolphin; but, since this
word does not occur in the list of sea or land animals that are
forbidden or permitted for food in Leviticus 11, it may not refer to a
particular species of animal at all. It may just mean skins that have
been tanned; that is: leather.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 25:6*](#Contents.html_rExo25_6)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^6^oil for lighting, spices for the anointing oil and for the incense
fragrances,

![Image](images/Exo_Chap25_6.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 25:7*](#Contents.html_rExo25_7)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^7^onyx stones and stones to be set for the ephod and for the
breastplate.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap25_7.jpg){height="43" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 25:8*](#Contents.html_rExo25_8)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^8^And they shall make me a holy place, and I shall tent among them.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap25_8.jpg){height="46" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

25:8. **I shall tent among them**. Forms of this Hebrew verb
(*š![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11" width="8"}kantî*) are
frequently taken to mean "to dwell"; but when it refers to God, this
verb is a denominative from the noun
*mišk![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11" width="8"}n:*
Tabernacle. As God explains to David, "I haven't lived in a house from
the day I brought the children of Israel up from Egypt to this day, but
I've been going about in a tent and in a Tabernacle" (2 Sam 7:6). The
creator cannot be pictured as *residing* on the earth but rather is
understood to *tent* among humans, that is, to be present in association
with a non permanent, movable structure. Even after the Temple is built,
God is associated with the Tabernacle, which is housed there (1 Kings
8:4; Babylonian Talmud Tractate So![Image](images/tdat.jpg){.block
height="13" width="5"}a 9a) and represented by the space under the wings
of the cherubs in the Holy of Holies (Ps 61:5).

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 25:9*](#Contents.html_rExo25_9)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^9^According to everything that I show you: the design of the Tabernacle
and the design of all its equipment. And you shall do so.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap25_9.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

25:9. **Tabernacle**. There is more about the Tabernacle and its
contents than any other subject in the Torah. There are long chapters
dealing with the building of the Tabernacle, filled with details of
materials and measurements in cubits. Tedious reading though it may be
for most readers, it conveys the significance of the Tabernacle as the
channel to YHWH. Exodus notes explicitly that the function of the
Tabernacle is: "I shall meet there with the children of Israel ... "
(29:43; 25:22). The book of Exodus culminates in the Tabernacle's
consecration (Exodus 40).

------------------------------------------------------------------------

half cubits and its width a cubit and a half and its height a cubit and

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

^22^And I shall meet with you there and speak with you from above the

![Image](images/Exo_Chap25_22.jpg){height="93" width="338"}

25:22. **meet**. The term denotes meeting at an appointed place or at an
appointed time. Thus the Tabernacle itself is known as the Tent of
Meeting.

[]{#Exodus26.html}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 26:1*](#Contents.html_rExo26_1)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **26** {.h2a}

^1^"And you shall make the Tabernacle with ten curtains of woven linen
and blue and purple and scarlet, with cherubs---you shall make them
designer's work.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap26_1.jpg){height="68" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

26:1. **make the Tabernacle**. No one has ever figured out how the
Tabernacle is put together. The components are given here, but the text
does not say how they are put together. Twenty frames are used for each
side wall and six for the rear wall (plus two more for supporting the
rear corners). The frames are ten cubits tall and "a cubit and a half
cubit" wide. People have therefore assumed that the Tabernacle is thirty
cubits long by ten cubits wide, and ten cubits high. Curtains fit over
these frames, and the curtains are made of two giant fabrics. Each
fabric is made of five pieces sewn together; the pieces are four cubits
by twenty-eight cubits wide. So the two fabrics are each twenty by
twenty-eight. They are attached together with gold clasps, making a
double fabric that is forty by twenty-eight. When this forty-cubit piece
is placed over the thirty-cubit-long structure of frames, it covers the
top and has ten cubits left over to cover the ten-cubit-high rear wall.
In support of this, people have noted that Solomon's Temple is sixty
cubits long by twenty wide, so the Temple and Tabernacle are in
two-to-one proportion.

But there are many things wrong with this. (1) The Temple is thirty
cubits high, so the proportion there is three-to-one, not two-to-one.
(2) The ten-cubit width is wrong, because six frames of a cubit and a
half each make only a nine-cubit width. (3) The twenty-eight-cubits-wide
curtains do not fit this structure. Its two sidewalls are each ten
cubits high, and its ceiling is ten cubits across, which requires a
cover that is thirty cubits wide. The twenty-eight-cubits-wide curtain
leaves a whole cubit (eighteen inches) uncovered on each side. (4) A
second set of curtains is made into two large fabrics like the first
set, but this second set (made of goats' hair) is made of curtains that
are four cubits by thirty, instead of four by twenty-eight, and there is
an extra, eleventh curtain at one end. The extra curtain has no purpose,
and the thirty-cubits-length hangs down past the bottom of the inner
fabric. (5) The gold clasps cannot be seen in this arrangement.

Also, this arrangement leaves questions: (6) Why would the frames be a
cubit and a half wide instead of just one cubit or two cubits? And why
does it say "a cubit and a half cubit" instead of the normal wording, "a
cubit and a half"? (7) Why are the curtains in groups of five, which are
each four cubits wide? What is significant about four cubits?

All of these problems and questions point to a different way that the
Tabernacle is put together. I propose that the reason that the frames
are "a cubit and a half cubit" is because they overlap each other by the
extra half-cubit. This is more stable and better for ventilation than if
they stood side by side (Figures 1 and 2). The twenty-frame length by
six-frame width means a structure that is twenty cubits by six cubits on
the inside. If the frames are a half-cubit wide, then it is twenty by
eight on the outside (Figure 3). Now, if we take the two large fabrics
that are twenty by twenty-eight cubits, and we connect them with the
clasps, and then we fold it in half along the clasps, it fits this
structure. The gold clasps now are visible: they surround the entrance
to the Tabernacle. The twenty-cubit width of the fabric matches the
twenty-cubit length of the Tabernacle. And the twenty-eight-cubit length
of the fabric goes up one side of the Tabernacle (ten cubits high),
across the ceiling (eight cubits), and down the other side (ten cubits).

![Image](images/f0259-01.jpg){height="456" width="565"}

A temple has been excavated in Israel at Arad. Its measurements are
twenty cubits by six cubits, the same as the (inside dimensions of the)
Tabernacle.

On the significance of these dimensions, see the comment on 26:30.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 26:2*](#Contents.html_rExo26_2)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^2^The length of one curtain shall be twenty-eight in cubits, and the
width of one curtain four in cubits: one size to all the curtains.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap26_2.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 26:3*](#Contents.html_rExo26_3)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^3^Five curtains will be connected, each to its sister-piece, and five
curtains connected, each to its sister-piece.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap26_3.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 26:4*](#Contents.html_rExo26_4)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^4^And you shall make loops of blue on the side of the one curtain at
the end of the connected group, and you shall do so in the side of the
end curtain in the second connected group.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap26_4.jpg){height="70" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 26:5*](#Contents.html_rExo26_5)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^5^You shall make fifty loops in the end of the curtain that is in the
second connected group, with the loops parallel: each to its
sister-piece.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap26_5.jpg){height="93" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 26:6*](#Contents.html_rExo26_6)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^6^And you shall make fifty clasps of gold and connect the curtains,
each to its sister-piece, with the clasps. And the Tabernacle will be
one.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap26_6.jpg){height="70" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

26:6. **the Tabernacle will be one**. In the simplest sense, this means
that once they sew all the curtains together and connect all the loops
and clasps, the Tabernacle will be one whole piece. But the
wording---"the Tabernacle will be one"---also fits with the centrality
of the Tabernacle to Israel's monotheism. One God, one Tabernacle, one
altar, only one place of worship. Later it will be confirmed by divine
commandment that Israel may have only one place of sacrifice, and that
this one place is in front of the Tabernacle (Leviticus 17). There
cannot be more than one place to worship God, because that might suggest
that there is more than one God. Or, to put it more essentially: it is
inconceivable in the first place in this new religion of a single deity
that there could possibly be more than a single sanctuary. Those who
claim that Israel's religion was not a real monotheism until a very late
stage (Second Temple) have not appreciated the significance of the
commandment of centralization of worship at a single location.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 26:7*](#Contents.html_rExo26_7)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^7^"And you shall make curtains of goats' hair for a tent over the
Tabernacle. You shall make them eleven curtains.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap26_7.jpg){height="71" width="340"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 26:8*](#Contents.html_rExo26_8)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^8^The length of one curtain shall be thirty in cubits, and the width of
one curtain four in cubits: one size for eleven curtains.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap26_8.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 26:9*](#Contents.html_rExo26_9)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^9^And you shall connect five of the curtains by themselves and six of
the curtains by themselves. And you shall double-fold the sixth curtain
opposite the front of the tent.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap26_9.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

26:9. **double-fold the sixth curtain**. The extra, eleventh curtain of
the tent is four cubits wide. When folded, each half of it covers half
of the eight-cubits-wide back of the Tabernacle (Figure 4). This also
answers the question of why the curtains are four cubits wide. And it
further confirms that the Tabernacle is eight cubits wide.

26:9. **opposite the front of the tent**. Meaning: it falls along the
*back* wall, *facing* the front. Thus v. 12 refers to "the hanging one
that is left over among the tent's curtains" and instructs that "you
shall hang half of the leftover curtain on the back parts of the
Tabernacle."

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 26:10*](#Contents.html_rExo26_10)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^10^And you shall make fifty loops on the side of the one end curtain in
the connected group and fifty loops on the side of the curtain of the
second connected group.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap26_10.jpg){height="70" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 26:11*](#Contents.html_rExo26_11)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^11^And you shall make fifty clasps of bronze and bring the clasps into
the loops and connect the tent. And it will be one.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap26_11.jpg){height="69" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 26:12*](#Contents.html_rExo26_12)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^12^And the hanging one that is left over among the tent's curtains: you
shall hang half of the leftover curtain on the back parts of the
Tabernacle.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap26_12.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 26:13*](#Contents.html_rExo26_13)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^13^And the cubit on this side and the cubit on that side in the
leftover in the length of the tent's curtains shall be hung on the sides
of the Tabernacle, on this side and on that side, to cover it.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap26_13.jpg){height="70" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

26:13. **the cubit on this side and the cubit on that side in the
leftover in the length**. This second set of curtains is thirty cubits
long. When it is spread over the inside set of curtains, which is only
twenty-eight cubits, there is thus a cubit left over on each side. It
"shall be hung on the sides of the Tabernacle, on this side and on that
side, to cover it." That is, it is folded under the inside linen fabric
so as to protect it from touching the ground.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 26:14*](#Contents.html_rExo26_14)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^14^And you shall make a covering for the tent of rams' skins dyed red
and a covering of leather skins above.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap26_14.jpg){height="46" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 26:15*](#Contents.html_rExo26_15)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^15^"And you shall make the frames for the Tabernacle of acacia wood,
standing.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap26_15.jpg){height="45" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

26:15. **frames**. Trellises---not solid boards or planks. If they were
solid, then the linen curtains would be sandwiched between the boards on
the inside and the goats' hair and leather coverings on the outside and
could never be seen.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 26:16*](#Contents.html_rExo26_16)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^16^The frame's length shall be ten cubits, and the width of one frame a
cubit and a half cubit.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap26_16.jpg){height="47" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 26:17*](#Contents.html_rExo26_17)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^17^One frame has two projections, each aligned with its sister-piece:
so you shall make for all the Tabernacle's frames.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap26_17.jpg){height="71" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 26:18*](#Contents.html_rExo26_18)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^18^And you shall make the frames for the Tabernacle: twenty frames for
the south side---to the south.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap26_18.jpg){height="46" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 26:19*](#Contents.html_rExo26_19)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

![Image](images/Exo_Chap26_19.jpg){height="95" width="340"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

![Image](images/f0261-01.jpg){height="243" width="193"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 26:20*](#Contents.html_rExo26_20)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^20^And for the Tabernacle's second side, for the north side: twenty
frames

![Image](images/Exo_Chap26_20.jpg){height="46" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 26:21*](#Contents.html_rExo26_21)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^21^and their forty bases of silver, two bases under one frame and two
bases under each other frame.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap26_21.jpg){height="46" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 26:22*](#Contents.html_rExo26_22)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^22^And for the rear of the Tabernacle, to the west, you shall make six
frames.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap26_22.jpg){height="22" width="337"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 26:23*](#Contents.html_rExo26_23)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^23^And you shall make two frames for the Tabernacle's corners in the
rear,

![Image](images/Exo_Chap26_23.jpg){height="45" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 26:24*](#Contents.html_rExo26_24)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^24^and they shall be doubles from below, and together they shall be
integrated on its top to one ring. It shall be so for the two of them;
they will be for the two corners.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap26_24.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 26:25*](#Contents.html_rExo26_25)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^25^And they shall be eight frames and their bases of silver, sixteen
bases, two bases under one frame and two bases under each other frame.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap26_25.jpg){height="96" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 26:26*](#Contents.html_rExo26_26)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^26^And you shall make bars of acacia wood: five for the frames of one
side of the Tabernacle

![Image](images/Exo_Chap26_26.jpg){height="46" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 26:27*](#Contents.html_rExo26_27)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^27^and five bars for the frames of the second side of the Tabernacle
and five bars for the frames of the side of the Tabernacle at the rear,
to the west,

![Image](images/Exo_Chap26_27.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 26:28*](#Contents.html_rExo26_28)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^28^and the middle bar through the frames extending from end to end.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap26_28.jpg){height="67" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 26:29*](#Contents.html_rExo26_29)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^29^And you shall plate the frames with gold, and you shall make their
rings gold, housings for the bars, and you shall plate the bars with
gold.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap26_29.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 26:30*](#Contents.html_rExo26_30)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^30^And you shall set up the Tabernacle according to the model of it
that you were shown in the mountain.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap26_30.jpg){height="41" width="390"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

26:30. **set up the Tabernacle**. What difference do these cubits make?
Why does it matter whether the Tabernacle is eight by twenty cubits or
ten by thirty? Because eight by twenty cubits, and ten cubits high, is
*the size of the space under the wings of the cherubs inside the Holy of
Holies in the Temple in Jerusalem*. Either the Tabernacle really stood
in that space, or the space represented it symbolically while the
Tabernacle itself was stored away somewhere inside the building. The
*Tanak* reports that the Tabernacle is in fact brought to the Temple on
the day of its dedication (1 Kings 8:4; 2 Chr 5:5). The Talmud reports
that it was in fact thus stored beneath the Temple
(So![Image](images/tdat.jpg){.block height="13" width="5"}a 9a). The
ancient historian Josephus says that the effect of the cherubs' wings in
the Temple was to appear like a tent (*Ant*. 8.103). A psalm says: "I'll
reside in your tent forever, I'll conceal myself in the hidden place of
your wings" (Ps 61:5). The book of 1 Chronicles (9:23) refers to the
Temple as "the House of the Tent" (*bêt
h![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11" width="8"}
'![Image](images/o.jpg){.block height="11" width="7"}hel*). It also
speaks of the "Tabernacle of the House of God" (6:33; see also 1 Chr
23:22; 2 Chr 29:6--7; 24:6). What this means is that the Temple was not
just the *successor* to the Tabernacle. The Tabernacle, revered as the
ancient channel to God, was actually located *inside* the Temple. So all
the laws in the Torah that command that something be done at the
Tabernacle applied as long as the first Temple stood in Jerusalem. By
the time of the Temple's destruction (587 [B.C.E.]{.smallcaps}), the
Tabernacle and its contents---the ark, the tablets---disappear from the
Bible. The rabbinic tradition is that the presence of God (Shechinah;
which, by the way, has the same root as the word for the Tabernacle:
*mišk![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11" width="8"}n*) was
in the first Temple but not in the second (Yoma 21b; Rashi on Gen 9:27).
This contributes to an impression of growing distance from the original
feeling of a direct connection with God in the Hebrew Bible.

This construction of the Tabernacle also goes against the dominant view
of biblical scholars for the past century: that the Tabernacle was not
real, that it was simply a fiction, invented to stand for the second
Temple in Jerusalem. That view was based on the two-to-one
correspondence of the measurements of the Tabernacle and the Temple. In
the first place, we have seen that the correspondence is not correct.
(See point number one in the comment on 26:1 above.) Second, those
measurements were based on the *first* Temple, not the second, in any
case. And, third, this chapter is part of a text that is written in
Classical Hebrew, which comes from a period long before the second
Temple.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 26:31*](#Contents.html_rExo26_31)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^31^"And you shall make a pavilion of blue and purple and scarlet and
woven linen; he shall make them designer's work with cherubs.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap26_31.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

26:31. **pavilion**. Hebrew *p![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block
height="11" width="8"}r![Image](images/o.jpg){.block height="11"
width="7"}ket*. In the second Temple this came to be a curtain, veiling
the Holy of Holies. Subsequently in synagogues it likewise has been a
curtain, veiling the ark. But in the Tabernacle it is a pavilion, a tent
within the tent, not a veil. It hangs on four posts, it provides a cover
over the ark *(w![Image](images/e-image.jpg){.block height="12"
width="7"}sakk![Image](images/o.jpg){.block height="11"
width="7"}t![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11" width="8"}
'al h![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11"
width="8"}'![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11"
width="8"}r![Image](images/o.jpg){.block height="11" width="7"}n;* Exod
40:3), it is *over* the Testimony (Exod 27:21), and it is referred to as
"the covering pavilion," *p![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block
height="11" width="8"}r![Image](images/o.jpg){.block height="11"
width="7"}ket hamm![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11"
width="8"}s![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11" width="8"}k*
(Exod 40:21; Num 4:5). (It is the same root word that is used earlier to
describe how the cherubs' wings "cover over" the atonement dais; 25:20.)
Such a *sukk![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11" width="8"}h*
is referred to in Ps 27:5 and in Lam 2:6 as well. Made of fabric
identical to that of the Tabernacle, it provides the Tabernacle's
necessary rear fabric wall. In the Talmudic age, when the
*p![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11"
width="8"}r![Image](images/o.jpg){.block height="11" width="7"}ket* had
come to be a curtain, the sages assumed that it had always been a
curtain, and so they were puzzled that in the Torah it is pictured as a
*sukk![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11" width="8"}h* and
that it is described as being "over" the ark (Sukkah 7b; cf.
So![Image](images/tdat.jpg){.block height="13" width="5"}a 37a; Menahot
62a, 98a).

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 26:32*](#Contents.html_rExo26_32)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^32^And you shall place it on four acacia columns plated with gold,
their hooks of gold, on four bases of silver.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap26_32.jpg){height="71" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 26:33*](#Contents.html_rExo26_33)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^33^And you shall place the pavilion under the clasps. And you shall
bring the Ark of the Testimony there, inside the pavilion, and the
pavilion will distinguish for you between the Holy and the Holy of
Holies.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap26_33.jpg){height="95" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

26:33. **under the clasps**. The Greek text says "upon the frames,"
meaning up against them, or near them. In Hebrew, the frames are
*q![Image](images/e-image.jpg){.block height="12"
width="7"}r![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11"
width="8"}šîm*, and the clasps are *q![Image](images/e-image.jpg){.block
height="12" width="7"}r![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11"
width="8"}![Image](images/s.jpg){.block height="13" width="8"}îm*. The
similarity of the two words led to the scribal confusion here. The Greek
fits with the understanding that the
*p![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11"
width="8"}r![Image](images/o.jpg){.block height="11" width="7"}ket* is a
pavilion, not a veil. The Masoretic Text makes little sense if it is a
pavilion. (If it were a veil, and if the Tabernacle were thirty cubits
long, then the Masoretic Text would mean that the veil hangs down under
the clasps, which come at the point of separation between the Holy place
and the Holy of Holies.)

26:33. **bring the Ark of the Testimony there, inside the pavilion**.
The ark is surrounded by a series of layers: first the pavilion (the
*p![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11"
width="8"}r![Image](images/o.jpg){.block height="11" width="7"}ket*),
then the Tabernacle (the linen tent), then the tent (the goats' hair
tent), then the covering (the tent of rams' skins dyed red), then the
covering of leather skins, then a courtyard surrounded by linen
hangings. The Israelites are thus given to understand that there is a
sequence of holiness, ever increasing, as one comes closer to the sacred
object in the center. Anyone who pursues holiness must be aware that he
or she must pass through a progression of levels, with increasing awe
and increasing danger as one comes closer to the sacred.

26:33. **the Holy**. The space from the entrance up to the
*p![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11"
width="8"}r![Image](images/o.jpg){.block height="11" width="7"}ket* is
referred to as the Holy.

26:33. **the Holy of Holies**. The space inside the
*p![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11"
width="8"}r![Image](images/o.jpg){.block height="11" width="7"}ket* is
the Holy of Holies, meaning the holiest of all holy places.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

------------------------------------------------------------------------

in the Holy of Holies.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap26_34.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

[*Exodus 26:36*](#Contents.html_rExo26_36)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^36^"And you shall make a cover for the entrance of the tent: blue and

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

[]{#Exodus27.html}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 27:1*](#Contents.html_rExo27_1)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **27** {.h2a}

^1^"And you shall make the altar of acacia wood. Five cubits long and
five cubits wide, the altar shall be square---and its height three
cubits.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap27_1.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

27:1. **And you shall make**. The quantity of detail in these chapters
is an indication that these are authentic descriptions of the Tabernacle
and its accouterments. What motive would there be to make all of this
up? The dominant view in critical biblical scholarship for over a
hundred years has been that the Tabernacle is a fiction. The character
of the text, however, argues against that view as much as the content
does.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 27:20*](#Contents.html_rExo27_20)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## C[OMMAND]{.smallcaps1} {.h201}

^20^"And you: you shall command the children of Israel that they shall
bring clear pressed olive oil to you for the light, to keep up a lamp
always.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap27.jpg){height="19" width="56"}

![Image](images/Exo_Chap27_20.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 27:21*](#Contents.html_rExo27_21)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^21^In the Tent of Meeting, outside the pavilion that is over the
Testimony, Aaron and his sons shall arrange it from evening until
morning in front of YHWH, an eternal law through their generations from
the children of Israel.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap27_21.jpg){height="95" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

[]{#Exodus28.html}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 28:1*](#Contents.html_rExo28_1)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **28** {.h2a}

^1^"And you, bring Aaron, your brother, forward to you, and his sons
with him, from among the children of Israel, for him to function as a
priest for me: Aaron, Nadab and Abihu, Eleazar and Ithamar, Aaron's
sons.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap28_1.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 28:2*](#Contents.html_rExo28_2)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^2^And you shall make holy clothes for Aaron, your brother, for glory
and for beauty.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap28_2.jpg){height="43" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

28:2. **for glory and for beauty**. Beauty inspires. Building beautiful
places for the practice of religion is a valuable thing. *Of course*
this does not mean building great edifices at the expense of the
starving masses, nor does it mean focusing on the outer trappings and
missing the content and spirit that they serve. There must be
balance---wisdom. But we must recognize the value of art and beauty: the
building, the priests' clothing, the music, the smells, the tastes.
Religion is not the enemy of the senses.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 28:3*](#Contents.html_rExo28_3)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^3^And you shall speak to all the wise of heart whom I have filled with
a spirit of wisdom, that they shall make Aaron's clothes, to sanctify
him for him to function as a priest for me.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap28_3.jpg){height="69" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 28:4*](#Contents.html_rExo28_4)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^4^And these are the clothes that they shall make: breastplate and ephod
and robe and patterned coat, a headdress and a sash. And they shall make
holy clothes for Aaron, your brother, and for his sons, for him to
function as a priest for me.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap28_4.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 28:5*](#Contents.html_rExo28_5)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^5^And *they* shall take the gold and the blue and the purple and the
scarlet and the linen.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap28_5.jpg){height="71" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 28:6*](#Contents.html_rExo28_6)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^6^"And they shall make the ephod: gold, blue, and purple, scarlet, and
woven linen, designer's work.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap28_6.jpg){height="50" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

[*Exodus 28:7*](#Contents.html_rExo28_7)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

will be connected.

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

[*Exodus 28:8*](#Contents.html_rExo28_8)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^8^And the ephod's designed belt that is on it shall be like its work
and be a part of it: gold, blue, and purple, and scarlet and woven
linen.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap28_8.jpg){height="46" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 28:9*](#Contents.html_rExo28_9)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^9^And you shall take two onyx stones and inscribe the names of the
children of Israel on them:

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}
