![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 37:5*](#Contents.html_rExo37_5)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^5^and brought the poles through the rings on the ark's sides, in order
to carry the ark.

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

[*Exodus 37:6*](#Contents.html_rExo37_6)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 37:7*](#Contents.html_rExo37_7)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 37:8*](#Contents.html_rExo37_8)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

cherubs from the atonement dais at its two sides.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap37_8.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/f0302-01.jpg){height="26" width="94"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 37:9*](#Contents.html_rExo37_9)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^9^And the two cherubs were spreading wings above, covering over the
atonement dais with their wings, and their faces each toward its
brother: the cherubs' faces were toward the atonement dais.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap37_9.jpg){height="94" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 37:10*](#Contents.html_rExo37_10)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^10^And he made the table of acacia wood, its length two cubits and its
width a cubit and its height a cubit and a half.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap37_10.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 37:11*](#Contents.html_rExo37_11)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^11^And he plated it with pure gold and made a border of gold all around
for it

![Image](images/Exo_Chap37_11.jpg){height="22" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 37:12*](#Contents.html_rExo37_12)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^12^and made a rim of a handbreadth for it all around and made a border
of gold for its rim all around.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap37_12.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 37:13*](#Contents.html_rExo37_13)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^13^And he cast four rings of gold for it and put the rings on the four
corners that its four legs had.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap37_13.jpg){height="70" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 37:14*](#Contents.html_rExo37_14)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^14^The rings were in juxtaposition to the rim, housings for the poles
in order to carry the table.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap37_14.jpg){height="46" width="340"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 37:15*](#Contents.html_rExo37_15)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^15^And he made the poles of acacia wood and plated them with gold in
order to carry the table.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap37_15.jpg){height="46" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 37:16*](#Contents.html_rExo37_16)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^16^And he made the items that were on the table---its dishes and its
pans and its bowls and its jars with which libations would be
poured---of pure gold.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap37_16.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 37:17*](#Contents.html_rExo37_17)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^17^And he made the menorah of pure gold. He made the menorah of
hammered work---its shaft and its branch, its cups, its ornaments, and
its flowers were part of it---

![Image](images/Exo_Chap37_17.jpg){height="69" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 37:18*](#Contents.html_rExo37_18)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^18^and six branches coming out from its sides, three of the menorah's
branches from its one side and three of the menorah's branches from its
second side,

![Image](images/Exo_Chap37_18.jpg){height="69" width="340"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 37:19*](#Contents.html_rExo37_19)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^19^three almond-shaped cups in the one branch, with ornament and
flower, and three almond-shaped cups in the other branch, with ornament
and flower. So it was for the six branches that came out from the
menorah,

![Image](images/Exo_Chap37_19.jpg){height="98" width="345"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 37:20*](#Contents.html_rExo37_20)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^20^and four almond-shaped cups within the menorah, with its ornaments
and its flowers,

![Image](images/Exo_Chap37_20.jpg){height="44" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 37:21*](#Contents.html_rExo37_21)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^21^and an ornament under the two branches from it, and an ornament
under the two branches from it, and an ornament under the two branches
from it, for the six branches that came out from it.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap37_21.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 37:22*](#Contents.html_rExo37_22)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^22^Their ornaments and their branches were part of it, all of it one
hammered work of pure gold.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap37_22.jpg){height="42" width="416"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 37:23*](#Contents.html_rExo37_23)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^23^And he made its lamps, seven, and its tongs and its fire-holders of
pure gold.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap37_23.jpg){height="44" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 37:24*](#Contents.html_rExo37_24)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^24^He made it and all of its equipment out of a talent of pure gold.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap37_24.jpg){height="22" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 37:25*](#Contents.html_rExo37_25)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^25^And he made the incense altar of acacia wood: its length a cubit and
its width a cubit, square, and its height two cubits; its horns were a
part of it.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap37_25.jpg){height="70" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 37:26*](#Contents.html_rExo37_26)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^26^And he plated it with pure gold, its roof and its walls all around
and its horns. And he made a border of gold for it all around.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap37_26.jpg){height="70" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 37:27*](#Contents.html_rExo37_27)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^27^And he made two rings of gold for it below its rim on its two sides,
on its two sidewalls, for housings for poles with which to carry it.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap37_27.jpg){height="71" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 37:28*](#Contents.html_rExo37_28)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^28^And he made the poles of acacia wood and plated them with gold.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap37_28.jpg){height="50" width="340"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 37:29*](#Contents.html_rExo37_29)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^29^And he made the holy anointing oil and the pure incense fragrances,
the work of an ointment-maker.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap37_29.jpg){height="46" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

[]{#Exodus38.html}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 38:1*](#Contents.html_rExo38_1)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **38** {.h2a}

^1^And he made the altar of burnt offering of acacia wood, its length
five cubits and its width five cubits, square, and its height three
cubits.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap38_1.jpg){height="70" width="340"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 38:2*](#Contents.html_rExo38_2)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^2^And he made its horns on its four corners. Its horns were part of it.
And he plated it with bronze.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap38_2.jpg){height="47" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 38:3*](#Contents.html_rExo38_3)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^3^And he made all the altar's equipment: the pots, the shovels and the
basins, the forks and the fire-holders. He made all of its equipment
bronze.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap38_3.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 38:4*](#Contents.html_rExo38_4)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^4^And he made a grate for the altar, a network of bronze, under its
ledge up to the middle of it.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap38_4.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 38:5*](#Contents.html_rExo38_5)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^5^And he cast four rings in the bronze grate's four ends: housings for
the poles.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap38_5.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 38:6*](#Contents.html_rExo38_6)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^6^And he made the poles of acacia wood and plated them with bronze

![Image](images/Exo_Chap38_6.jpg){height="20" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 38:7*](#Contents.html_rExo38_7)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^7^and brought the poles through the rings on the altar's sides in order
to carry it with them. He made it hollow of boards.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap38_7.jpg){height="46" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 38:8*](#Contents.html_rExo38_8)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^8^And he made the basin of bronze and its stand of bronze with the
mirrors of the women who served at the entrance of the Tent of Meeting.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap38_8.jpg){height="71" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

38:8. **women who served at the entrance of the Tent of Meeting**. This
is the first mention of the female workers at the Tabernacle. They are
mentioned one time outside the Torah: a few generations after Moses, the
priest Eli's sons have illicit sex with them at Shiloh. The temptation
of males in power to take advantage of their high position in the eyes
of women who work under them has persisted for centuries. It is
criticized in the *Tanak*. In the case of the Shiloh priests, it is one
of the acts that leads to the downfall of that priestly house and its
replacement by Samuel (1 Sam 2:22--26).

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 38:9*](#Contents.html_rExo38_9)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^9^And he made the courtyard: for the south side---to the south, the
courtyard's hangings of woven linen, a hundred in cubits,

![Image](images/Exo_Chap38_9.jpg){height="60" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 38:10*](#Contents.html_rExo38_10)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^10^their columns twenty, and their bases twenty of bronze, and the
columns' hooks and their rods of silver.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap38_10.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 38:11*](#Contents.html_rExo38_11)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^11^And for the north side: a hundred in cubits, their columns twenty,
and their bases twenty of bronze, and the columns' hooks and their bands
of silver.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap38_11.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 38:12*](#Contents.html_rExo38_12)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^12^And for the west side: hangings of fifty in cubits, their columns
ten, and their bases ten, the columns' hooks and their bands of silver.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap38_12.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 38:13*](#Contents.html_rExo38_13)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^13^And the courtyard's width for the east side---to the east---fifty
cubits.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap38_13.jpg){height="22" width="286"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 38:14*](#Contents.html_rExo38_14)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^14^Fifteen cubits of hangings to the panel, their columns three and
their bases three;

![Image](images/Exo_Chap38_14.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 38:15*](#Contents.html_rExo38_15)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^15^and the second panel---on this side and that side of the courtyard's
gate---hangings of fifteen cubits, their columns three and their bases
three.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap38_15.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 38:16*](#Contents.html_rExo38_16)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^16^All of the courtyard's hangings, all around, were woven linen.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap38_16.jpg){height="46" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 38:17*](#Contents.html_rExo38_17)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^17^And the column's bases were bronze, the columns' hooks and their
bands were silver, and the plating of their tops was silver, and they
were banded with silver: all of the courtyard's columns.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap38_17.jpg){height="94" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 38:18*](#Contents.html_rExo38_18)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^18^And the cover of the courtyard's gate was embroiderer's work, blue
and purple and scarlet and woven linen, and twenty cubits in length, and
five cubits in height in the width, in juxtaposition to the courtyard's
hangings.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap38_18.jpg){height="95" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

38:18. **five cubits in height in the width**. I understand the cover to
be a canopy above the entrance (as opposed to a screen or curtain
hanging). The Hebrew (*m![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11"
width="8"}s![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11" width="8"}k*)
denotes a thing that covers *over* something else (Exod 40:3; 2 Sam
17:19; Ps 105:39). So the phrase in this verse means that the entire
width of the courtyard's cover, from front to back, is suspended five
cubits above the ground, or that both its width and its height above the
ground are five cubits.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 38:19*](#Contents.html_rExo38_19)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^19^And their columns were four and their bases four of bronze, their
hooks silver, and the plating of their tops and their bands silver.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap38_19.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 38:20*](#Contents.html_rExo38_20)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^20^And all of the pegs of the Tabernacle and the courtyard all around
were bronze.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap38_20.jpg){height="45" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 38:21*](#Contents.html_rExo38_21)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## A[CCOUNTS]{.smallcaps1} {.h201}

^21^These are the accounts of the Tabernacle, the Tabernacle of the
Testimony, that were made by Moses' word, the work of the Levites by the
hand of Ithamar, son of Aaron, the priest.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap38.jpg){height="28" width="66"}

![Image](images/Exo_Chap38_21.jpg){height="70" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

38:21. **by the hand of Ithamar**. Meaning: under the direction of
Ithamar.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 38:22*](#Contents.html_rExo38_22)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^22^And Bezalel, son of Uri, son of Hur, of the tribe of Judah, made
everything that YHWH had commanded Moses.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap38_22.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 38:23*](#Contents.html_rExo38_23)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^23^And with him was Oholiab, son of Ahisamach, of the tribe of Dan, an
engraver and designer and embroiderer in blue and in purple and in
scarlet and in linen.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap38_23.jpg){height="70" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 38:24*](#Contents.html_rExo38_24)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^24^All the gold that was used for the work, in all the work of the
Holy: and the gold of the elevation offering was twenty-nine talents and
seven hundred thirty shekels by the shekel of the Holy.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap38_24.jpg){height="71" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 38:25*](#Contents.html_rExo38_25)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^25^And the silver of the congregation's accounts was a hundred talents
and a thousand seven hundred seventy-five shekels by the shekel of the
Holy:

![Image](images/Exo_Chap38_25.jpg){height="45" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 38:26*](#Contents.html_rExo38_26)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^26^a beqa per head, half of a shekel by the shekel of the Holy, for
everyone who passed through the counts, from twenty years old and up,
for six hundred thousand and three thousand five hundred fifty.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap38_26.jpg){height="96" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 38:27*](#Contents.html_rExo38_27)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^27^And the hundred talents of silver were for casting the bases of the
Holy and the bases of the pavilion, a hundred bases to the hundred
talents, a talent per base.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap38_27.jpg){height="71" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 38:28*](#Contents.html_rExo38_28)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^28^And he made the thousand seven hundred seventy-five into hooks for
the columns, and he plated their tops and banded them.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap38_28.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 38:29*](#Contents.html_rExo38_29)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^29^And the bronze of the elevation offering was seventy talents and two
thousand four hundred shekels.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap38_29.jpg){height="44" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 38:30*](#Contents.html_rExo38_30)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^30^And with it he made the bases of the entrance of the Tent of Meeting
and the bronze altar and the bronze network that it had and all of the
altar's equipment

![Image](images/Exo_Chap38_30.jpg){height="91" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 38:31*](#Contents.html_rExo38_31)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^31^and the bases of the courtyard all around and the bases of the
courtyard's gate and all of the Tabernacle's pegs and all of the
courtyard's pegs all around.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap38_31.jpg){height="71" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

[]{#Exodus39.html}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 39:1*](#Contents.html_rExo39_1)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **39** {.h2a}

^1^And from the blue and the purple and the scarlet they made fabric
clothes for ministering in the Holy, and they made the holy clothes that
were Aaron's, as YHWH had commanded Moses.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap39_1.jpg){height="71" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 39:2*](#Contents.html_rExo39_2)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^2^And he made the ephod: gold, blue, and purple and scarlet and woven
linen.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap39_2.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 39:3*](#Contents.html_rExo39_3)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^3^And they hammered out foil sheets of the gold, and he cut threads to
use among the blue and among the purple and among the scarlet and among
the linen---designer's work.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap39_3.jpg){height="74" width="342"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 39:4*](#Contents.html_rExo39_4)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^4^They made shoulder-pieces for it, connected: on its two ends it was
connected.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap39_4.jpg){height="44" width="338"}

![Image](images/f0306-01.jpg){height="26" width="90"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 39:5*](#Contents.html_rExo39_5)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^5^And its ephod designed belt that was on it was a part of it, like its
work, gold, blue, and purple and scarlet and woven linen, as YHWH had
commanded Moses.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap39_5.jpg){height="95" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 39:6*](#Contents.html_rExo39_6)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^6^And they made the onyx stones surrounded by settings of gold,
inscribed with signet inscriptions with the names of the children of
Israel.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap39_6.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 39:7*](#Contents.html_rExo39_7)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^7^And he set them on the ephod's shoulder-pieces, commemorative stones
for the children of Israel, as YHWH had commanded Moses.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap39_7.jpg){height="46" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 39:8*](#Contents.html_rExo39_8)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^8^And he made the breastplate, designer's work, like the work of the
ephod: gold, blue, and purple and scarlet and woven linen.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap39_8.jpg){height="68" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 39:9*](#Contents.html_rExo39_9)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^9^It was square. They made the breastplate doubled, its length a span
and its width a span, doubled.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap39_9.jpg){height="67" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 39:10*](#Contents.html_rExo39_10)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^10^And they mounted four rows of stones in it: a row of a carnelian, a
topaz, and an emerald: the one row;

![Image](images/Exo_Chap39_10.jpg){height="46" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 39:11*](#Contents.html_rExo39_11)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^11^and the second row: a ruby, a sapphire, and a diamond;

![Image](images/Exo_Chap39_11.jpg){height="45" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 39:12*](#Contents.html_rExo39_12)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^12^and the third row: a *jacinth*, an agate, and an amethyst;

![Image](images/Exo_Chap39_12.jpg){height="45" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 39:13*](#Contents.html_rExo39_13)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^13^and the fourth row: a beryl, an onyx, and a jasper---set in gold in
their mountings.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap39_13.jpg){height="47" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 39:14*](#Contents.html_rExo39_14)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^14^And the stones: they were with the names of the children of Israel,
twelve with their names, signet engravings, each with its name, for
twelve tribes.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap39_14.jpg){height="70" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 39:15*](#Contents.html_rExo39_15)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^15^And they made twisted chains, rope work, of pure gold, on the
breastplate.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap39_15.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 39:16*](#Contents.html_rExo39_16)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^16^And they made two settings of gold and two rings of gold and put the
two rings on the two ends of the breastplate

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

[*Exodus 39:17*](#Contents.html_rExo39_17)

breastplate.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap39_17.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 39:18*](#Contents.html_rExo39_18)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^18^And they put the two ends of the two ropes on the two settings and
put them on the ephod's shoulder-pieces opposite the front of it.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap39_18.jpg){height="72" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 39:19*](#Contents.html_rExo39_19)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^19^And they made two rings of gold and put them on the breastplate's
two ends on its side that is adjacent to the ephod, inward.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap39_19.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 39:20*](#Contents.html_rExo39_20)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^20^And they made two rings of gold and put them on the ephod's two
shoulder-pieces below, opposite the front of it, by its connection,
above the ephod's designed belt.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap39_20.jpg){height="71" width="342"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 39:21*](#Contents.html_rExo39_21)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^21^And they attached the breastplate from its rings to the ephod's
rings with a blue string so it would be on the ephod's designed belt and
the breastplate would not be detached from the ephod, as YHWH had
commanded Moses.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap39_21.jpg){height="92" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

39:21. **as YHWH had commanded Moses**. A major sentence is missing from
the Masoretic Text (and the Septuagint) here. A Dead Sea scroll and the
Samaritan text add it: "And they made the Urim and Tummim, as YHWH had
commanded Moses." Presumably it was omitted from the MT because the
scribe's eye jumped from the first occurrence of the phrase "as YHWH had
commanded Moses" to the second. This is another case of the scribal
phenomenon known as haplography, and it shows that even important
matters can be lost by scribal errors of copying. (For other
implications of scribal changes, see the comment on Exod 1:5.)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 39:32*](#Contents.html_rExo39_32)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^32^And all of the construction of the Tabernacle of the Tent of Meeting
was finished. And the children of Israel did according to everything
that YHWH had commanded Moses. They did so.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap39_32.jpg){height="70" width="340"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

39:32,43; 40:9. **finished, saw, work, did, blessed, made holy**. The
conclusion of the account of the people's construction of the Tabernacle
mirrors the language of the conclusion of the account of God's creation
of the universe (Gen 1:31--2:3). This further reminds us of the role of
the Tabernacle as the channel through which divine and human
communicate. And it indicates that, after the first revelation at Sinai,
such communication requires action by humans in order to receive it.

[]{#Exodus40.html}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 40:1*](#Contents.html_rExo40_1)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **40** {.h2a}

^1^And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying,

![Image](images/Exo_Chap40_1.jpg){height="22" width="253"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 40:2*](#Contents.html_rExo40_2)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^2^"On the day of the first month, on the first of the month, you shall
set up the Tabernacle of the Tent of Meeting.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap40_2.jpg){height="73" width="339"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 40:3*](#Contents.html_rExo40_3)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^3^And you shall set the Ark of the Testimony there and have the
pavilion cover over the ark.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap40_3.jpg){height="47" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

40:3. **have the pavilion cover over the ark**. The
*p![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11"
width="8"}r![Image](images/o.jpg){.block height="11" width="7"}ket* is
clearly a pavilion, not a curtain. It is mounted so as to *cover over*
the ark. The word is *sakk![Image](images/o.jpg){.block height="11"
width="7"}t![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11" width="8"}*,
meaning to make a covering: a sukkah. Thus a psalm about the Tent and
the Temple says: "He'll conceal me in His *sukkah* in a day of trouble;
He'll hide me in His tent's hidden place" (Ps 27:5; cf. Lam 2:6). The
*p![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11"
width="8"}r![Image](images/o.jpg){.block height="11" width="7"}ket*
later became a curtain (presumably in the second Temple) when there was
no longer an ark to place under it, and then people mistakenly
understood it retroactively to have been a curtain in the Tabernacle and
first Temple as well. (See the comment on 26:31.)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 40:4*](#Contents.html_rExo40_4)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^4^And you shall bring the table and make its arrangement, and bring the
menorah and put up its lamps.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap40_4.jpg){height="70" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 40:5*](#Contents.html_rExo40_5)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

the Testimony, and you shall set the cover of the entrance of the

![Image](images/Exo_Chap40_5.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^11^And you shall anoint the basin and its stand and make it holy.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap40_11.jpg){height="44" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 40:12*](#Contents.html_rExo40_12)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^12^"And you shall bring Aaron and his sons forward to the entrance of
the Tent of Meeting and wash them with water.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap40_12.jpg){height="70" width="340"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 40:13*](#Contents.html_rExo40_13)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^13^And you shall dress Aaron with the holy clothes and anoint him and
make him holy, and he shall function as a priest for me.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap40_13.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 40:14*](#Contents.html_rExo40_14)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^14^And you shall bring his sons forward and dress them with coats

![Image](images/Exo_Chap40_14.jpg){height="44" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 40:15*](#Contents.html_rExo40_15)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^15^and anoint them as you anointed their father, and they shall
function as priests for me. And it will be for their anointing to be
theirs as an eternal priesthood through their generations."

![Image](images/Exo_Chap40_15.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Exodus 40:16*](#Contents.html_rExo40_16)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^16^And Moses did it. According to everything that YHWH commanded him,
he did so.

![Image](images/Exo_Chap40_16.jpg){height="47" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

40:16. **And Moses did it. According to everything that YHWH commanded
him, he did so**. These are the exact words that are used to describe
Noah's obedience (except for the mention of the name of God here). There
are several parallels between Noah and Moses. They are the two biblical
persons associated with floating in arks (Gen 6:14; Exod 2:3)! But the
most explicit, verbatim, parallel is focused on their obedience to
divine commands. Especially for those who hear the divine commands
directly, precise obedience is the primary concern.

40:16. **He did so**. Exodus concludes with silence on Moses' part. In
the final chapter of the book, Moses does not speak. (He is last said to
have spoken in the last verse of Exodus 39.) He sets up the ark and
Tabernacle as commanded. He anoints Aaron and his sons as priests. The
cloud and glory fill the Tabernacle so that Moses cannot enter. And it
is reported that the people's travels are to depend on the movements of
the cloud and glory. Thus the book of Exodus ends as it began, with the
attention on the people---and their relationship with their God. The
person of Moses is the focus of the story in the intervening chapters,
but the nature of the opening and concluding chapters would suggest that
he is just that: a tangible focus of a larger dynamic, between God and a
human community.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[]{#Lev_Title.html}

![Image](images/Lev_Title.jpg){height="302" width="527"}

[]{#Lev_Front.html}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[COMMENTARY ON THE TORAH](#Contents.html_rLev_Front)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[A]{.dropcap}fter the rush of forces in Exodus, Leviticus is a rather
tranquil book. Following the mighty *acts* of God in Exodus, Leviticus
is disproportionately the *words* of God. There is very little
narrative---barely three chapters out of twenty-seven in the book---and
no poetry (with the possible exception of a few isolated verses). More
than the other four of the Five Books of Moses, Leviticus pictures the
deity speaking. Consistent with this is the absence of movement in
Leviticus. Unlike Genesis and Exodus, the entire book of Leviticus is
set in one place: at the foot of Mount Sinai. In Exodus, the
universe---the land, waters, and sky---is in disarray. Following that,
Leviticus is concerned with orderliness, arrangement.

Leviticus pictures the beginning of the solidification---the formation
of an identity---of a people through law. The law is significant as a
subject of study in itself and in context of the study of history as
well. But, also in narrative terms, it is important to see the place of
the law in the development of the story of the people: laws of how to
behave toward other human beings, laws that bring identity (what to eat,
religious ceremonies, holidays). The laws are not merely listed. They
are narrated as words of YHWH to Moses (and Aaron) and thus are
presented as an integrated component of the account.

In Leviticus the knowledge of God, which is an explicit, continuing
component in Exodus, likewise resides in the background. The term "to
know" (Hebrew *yd*') never occurs in Leviticus in the sense of knowledge
of God. YHWH now *is* known to Israel. This is assumed.

Impressively, the story stops being about Moses in Leviticus. The laws
are conveyed through him, certainly, but the book is *about* the laws
themselves and the fact of their being revealed to the people. The only
stories are primarily about Aaron and his sons. More broadly, Leviticus
does not develop personalities. It is about institutions, not
individuals. And this, too, contributes to the reduction of tension in
Leviticus following the great confrontations of Exodus.

All of this contributes to the change of feeling of this book. Leviticus
is both integral to the context of the books that precede and follow it
and, at the same time, anomalous in character.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*Leviticus*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Unlike in Exodus, there are few miracles portrayed in Leviticus, yet the
book, rich in background like the two books that precede it, is pervaded
with the presence of the divine. The continuing scene has already been
set in Exodus: A column of cloud or fire is visible at all times. A Tent
of Meeting, the Tabernacle, stands outside the camp. Moses goes in and
out of the tent, his face veiled and then unveiled, bringing laws from
God. The book begins with God speaking from the Tabernacle, thus
establishing the Tabernacle from the outset as the visible, present
entity among the people through which the divine word enters the world.
The cosmic/miraculous thus looms, and a channel between it and the
earthly operates.

[]{#Lev_Chapter01.html}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 1:1*](#Contents.html_rlev1_1)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## A[ND]{.smallcaps1} H[E]{.smallcaps1} C[ALLED]{.smallcaps1} {.h201}

## **1** {.h2a}

^1^And He called to Moses, and YHWH spoke to him from the Tent of
Meeting, saying,

![Image](images/Lev_Chap1.jpg){height="28" width="66"}

![Image](images/Lev_Chap1_01.jpg){height="47" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 1:2*](#Contents.html_rlev1_2)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^2^"Speak to the children of Israel. And you shall say to them: A human
from you who will make an offering to YHWH---you shall make your
offering from the domestic animals: from the herd and from the flock.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap1_02.jpg){height="95" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

1:2. **A human**. The laws of sacrifice begin by referring to the
offerer by the species term "a human" (Hebrew
'*![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11"
width="8"}d![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11"
width="8"}m*). Rashi gives a midrashic explanation for this. But we
should note that the text next refers to the offerer as "a person"
(Hebrew *nepe![Image](images/s-image.jpg){.block height="11"
width="7"},* Lev 2:1) and then as "a man" (Hebrew
'*î![Image](images/s-image.jpg){.block height="11" width="7"},* Lev
7:8), and this is the same order in which these terms are used to refer
to human beings in the book of Genesis
('*![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11"
width="8"}d![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11" width="8"}m*
in Gen 1:26; *nepe![Image](images/s-image.jpg){.block height="11"
width="7"}* in 2:7; '*î![Image](images/s-image.jpg){.block height="11"
width="7"}* in 2:23). This makes a link between the books of the Torah,
and it makes a link between God's creation of humans on the one hand and
humans' sacrifices to their creator on the other. Creation and sacrifice
are linked. Creation produces life. Sacrifice takes life. And so the
word order here subtly reminds us that, when we take animals' lives in
order to support our own, we should remember that both their lives and
ours are part of a common creation, and that both are treated in the
Torah as sacred.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 1:3*](#Contents.html_rlev1_3)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^3^"If his offering is a burnt offering from the herd, he shall make it
an unblemished male. He shall bring it forward to the entrance of the
Tent of Meeting for his acceptance in front of YHWH.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap1_03.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

1:3. **to the entrance of the Tent of Meeting**. From this time on,
there is only one place on earth where an Israelite may perform a
sacrifice: at the altar at the entrance of the Tent of Meeting. This
applies here to a burnt offering. Later in this section we learn that it
also applies to the peace offering (3:2), the sin offering (4:3), the
guilt offering (7:2), and the grain offering (6:7--9). This applies to
the period of Israel's travels through the wilderness. Then the tent is
set up at Shiloh (Josh 18:1), then at Gibeah (2 Chr 1:3), and then
finally it is brought to the Temple that King Solomon builds in
Jerusalem (1 Kings 8:4; 1 Chr 5:5). One exception: the prophet Elijah
makes a sacrifice at Mount Carmel in a duel with the priests of Baal;
but it is presented as an extraordinary occurrence, led by a prophet,
involving a miracle, the last miracle in the *Tanak* that is said to be
performed in front of the entire people of Israel (1 Kings 18). All
other sacrifice is to take place at the Tent of Meeting. This
*centralization* of worship in Israel's religion is crucially important.
It is a powerful corollary of Israelite monotheism: one God, one altar.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 1:4*](#Contents.html_rlev1_4)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^4^And he shall lay his hand on the head of the burnt offering, and it
will be accepted for him, to atone for him.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap1_04.jpg){height="47" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 1:5*](#Contents.html_rlev1_5)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^5^And he shall slaughter the herd animal in front of YHWH, and the sons
of Aaron, the priests, shall bring the blood forward and fling the blood
on the altar, all around, which is at the entrance of the Tent of
Meeting.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap1_05.jpg){height="71" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

1:5. **he shall slaughter ... and the priests shall bring the blood
forward**. The *individual* slaughters the animal; the *priests* handle
the blood. This is consistent with the conception found elsewhere in the
Torah that blood is sacred. It can contaminate, it can protect, and it
"is the life."

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 1:6*](#Contents.html_rlev1_6)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^6^And he shall flay the burnt offering and cut it into its parts.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap1_06.jpg){height="22" width="288"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 1:7*](#Contents.html_rlev1_7)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^7^And the sons of Aaron, the priest, shall put fire on the altar and
arrange wood on the fire.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap1_07.jpg){height="69" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

1:7. **the sons of Aaron, the priest**. Leviticus's laws, as well as its
narrative, are fundamentally concerned with priesthood, and thus with
leadership, authority---human authority, but sanctioned by
God---transforming the Israelites from a ragtag mass of ex-slaves into
an organized society. The priests are installed and anointed, their
sacrificial function is institutionalized, and they are commissioned to
teach (10:10--11), a commission that implies authority. The installation
of the priesthood---Aaron and his sons---is divinely stamped by the only
appearance of the divine glory (the
*k![Image](images/e-image.jpg){.block height="12" width="7"}bôd YHWH*)
in Leviticus, at the end of eight days of inaugural ceremonies of the
priesthood and the sacrificial system. And it is emphasized that the
priestly laws applying to Aaron and his sons are eternal (10:9,15). The
consecration of the priesthood had been announced in summary in Exodus
40. Now it is narrated. The priests' ritual and ethical activity is tied
to sacrifice, and so the narrative's first concern is the new
priesthood's first sacrificial offering. But sacrifice cannot be
performed in any way one wishes. One must perform it in a prescribed
manner. And so first there are seven chapters of prescription. This is
consistent with a theme of prescription that pervades the book. There is
a way to behave: in morality, in ethics, and in ritual.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 1:8*](#Contents.html_rlev1_8)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^8^And the sons of Aaron, the priests, shall arrange the parts, the
head, and the suet on the wood that is on the fire that is on the altar.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap1_08.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 1:9*](#Contents.html_rlev1_9)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^9^And he shall wash its innards and its legs with water. And the priest
shall burn it all to smoke at the altar, a burnt offering, an offering
by fire of a pleasant smell to YHWH.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap1_09.jpg){height="69" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 1:10*](#Contents.html_rlev1_10)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^10^"And if his offering is from the flock---from the sheep or from the
goats---for a burnt offering, he shall make it an unblemished male.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap1_10.jpg){height="71" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 1:11*](#Contents.html_rlev1_11)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^11^And he shall slaughter it on the northward side of the altar in
front of YHWH. And the sons of Aaron, the priests, shall fling its blood
on the altar, all around.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap1_11.jpg){height="74" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 1:12*](#Contents.html_rlev1_12)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^12^And he shall cut it into its parts and its head and its suet. And
the priest shall arrange them on the wood that is on the fire that is on
the altar.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap1_12.jpg){height="73" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 1:13*](#Contents.html_rlev1_13)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^13^And he shall wash the innards and the legs with water. And the
priest shall bring it all forward and burn it to smoke at the altar. It
is a burnt offering, an offering by fire of a pleasant smell to YHWH.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap1_13.jpg){height="68" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 1:14*](#Contents.html_rlev1_14)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^14^"And if his offering to YHWH is a burnt offering from birds, then he
shall make his offering from turtledoves or from pigeons.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap1_14.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 1:15*](#Contents.html_rlev1_15)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^15^And the priest shall bring it forward to the altar and wring its
head and burn it to smoke at the altar, and its blood shall be drained
on the wall of the altar.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap1_15.jpg){height="69" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 1:16*](#Contents.html_rlev1_16)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^16^And he shall take away its crop with its feathers and throw it
beside the altar eastward to the place of the ashes.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap1_16.jpg){height="68" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 1:17*](#Contents.html_rlev1_17)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^17^And he shall tear it open by its wings---he shall not divide
it---and the priest shall burn it to smoke at the altar on the wood that
is on the fire. It is a burnt offering, an offering by fire of a
pleasant smell to YHWH.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap1_17.jpg){height="89" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

1:17. **he shall not divide it.** One cuts up herd and flock animals
during a sacrifice, but one does not divide birds this way. This summons
to mind the original sacrifice that sealed the covenant between God and
Abraham, in which Abraham cuts all the animals in half but leaves the
birds whole (Gen 15:10), and a fire representing God's presence passes
between the parts of the animals. Thus Israel's manner of sacrifice now
memorializes the fact that God bound Himself to Israel through a
ceremony of animal sacrifice.

[]{#Lev_Chapter02.html}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 2:1*](#Contents.html_rlev2_1)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **2** {.h2a}

^1^"And a person who will make an offering of a grain offering to YHWH,
his offering shall be of fine flour, and he shall pour oil on it and put
frankincense on it.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap2_01.jpg){height="46" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 2:2*](#Contents.html_rlev2_2)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^2^And he shall bring it to the sons of Aaron, the priests. And he shall
take the fill of his fist from there: from its fine flour and from its
oil in addition to all of its frankincense. And the priest shall burn a
representative portion of it to smoke at the altar, an offering by fire
of a pleasant smell to YHWH.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap2_02.jpg){height="95" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 2:3*](#Contents.html_rlev2_3)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^3^And the remainder from the grain offering is Aaron's and his sons',
the holy of holies from YHWH's offerings by fire.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap2_03.jpg){height="46" width="336"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 2:4*](#Contents.html_rlev2_4)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^4^"And if he will make an offering of an oven-baked grain offering:
fine flour, unleavened cakes mixed with oil and unleavened wafers with
oil poured on them.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap2_04.jpg){height="69" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 2:5*](#Contents.html_rlev2_5)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^5^"And if your offering is a grain offering on a griddle, it shall be
fine flour, mixed with oil, unleavened.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap2_05.jpg){height="47" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 2:6*](#Contents.html_rlev2_6)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^6^Break it into pieces and pour oil on it. It is a grain offering.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap2_06.jpg){height="41" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 2:7*](#Contents.html_rlev2_7)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^7^"And if your offering is a grain offering from a pan, it shall be
made of fine flour in oil.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap2_07.jpg){height="45" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 2:8*](#Contents.html_rlev2_8)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^8^"And you shall bring the grain offering that one will make from these
things to YHWH, and one shall bring it forward to the priest, and he
shall bring it over to the altar.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap2_08.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 2:9*](#Contents.html_rlev2_9)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^9^And the priest shall lift from the grain offering a representative
portion of it and burn it to smoke at the altar, an offering by fire of
a pleasant smell to YHWH.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap2_09.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 2:10*](#Contents.html_rlev2_10)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^10^And the remainder from the grain offering is Aaron's and his sons',
the holy of holies from YHWH's offerings by fire.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap2_10.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 2:11*](#Contents.html_rlev2_11)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^11^Every grain offering that you will bring forward to YHWH shall not
be made with leavening, because all leaven and all honey: you shall not
burn any of it to smoke as an offering by fire to YHWH.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap2_11.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

2:11. **Every grain offering ... shall not be made with leavening**.
That is, *no* grain offering shall be made with leaven. See the comment
on Exod 29:2.

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 2:12*](#Contents.html_rlev2_12)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^12^You shall bring them forward to YHWH as an offering of a first
thing, but they shall not go up to the altar as a pleasant smell.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap2_12.jpg){height="45" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 2:13*](#Contents.html_rlev2_13)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^13^And you shall sprinkle every offering of a grain offering with salt.
And you shall not let the salt of your God's covenant cease from on your
grain offering; you shall bring salt on all your offerings.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap2_13.jpg){height="98" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

2:13. **sprinkle every offering of a grain offering with salt**. The
requirement to use salt with these offerings is now memorialized by the
custom of putting salt on bread when one says the blessing over bread
(*hamm![Image](images/o.jpg){.block height="11"
width="7"}![Image](images/sdot.jpg){.block height="11" width="6"}î'*) at
the beginning of a meal.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 2:14*](#Contents.html_rlev2_14)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^14^"And if you will bring a grain offering of firstfruits to YHWH, you
shall bring the grain offering of your firstfruits *ripe,* parched with
fire, groats of fresh grain.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap2_14.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 2:15*](#Contents.html_rlev2_15)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^15^And you shall put oil on it and set frankincense on it. It is a
grain offering.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap2_15.jpg){height="46" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 2:16*](#Contents.html_rlev2_16)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^16^And the priest shall burn a representative portion of it to smoke,
from its groats and from its oil, in addition to all of its
frankincense, an offering by fire to YHWH.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap2_16.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

![Image](images/Lev_Chap3_01.jpg){height="71" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

![Image](images/Lev_Chap3_02.jpg){height="73" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[]{#Lev_Chapter04.html}

[*Leviticus 4:1*](#Contents.html_rlev4_1)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **4** {.h2a}

^1^And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying,

![Image](images/Lev_Chap4_01.jpg){height="25" width="330"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 4:2*](#Contents.html_rlev4_2)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^2^"Speak to the children of Israel, saying: A person who sins by
mistake---of any of YHWH's commandments that are not to be done---and
does any one of them:

![Image](images/Lev_Chap4_02.jpg){height="73" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

4:2. **A person who sins**. At the time that I am writing this, it has
been almost two thousand years since the second Jerusalem Temple was
destroyed and sacrifices ended in Judaism. The two largest religions,
Christianity and Islam, also do not perform sacrifices, and so it has
been so long since we have seen or done this practice that all but a few
people are unclear about what sacrifice was. Frequently they imagine
that it meant that people regularly destroyed much of their livestock
for no reason. This is wrong.

The purpose of sacrifice was to recognize that an animal's life was
sacred.

The order of the book of Leviticus itself may have contributed to the
misunderstanding of sacrifice. It deals with the sacrifices that go to
the priests first (in Leviticus 1--7) and does not get to the "food"
sacrifices until Leviticus 17.

Most sacrificed animals are eaten by the persons who bring the animals
to the altar. There is also a second group of sacrifices, which involve
the ritual slaughter of an animal that is not for consumption by the
person who brings the sacrifice. Rather, the meat is eaten by the
priests or is entirely burnt. Such sacrifices relate to guilt and
expiation. The introduction of law means the introduction of violations
of the law. Besides addressing matters of punishment (compensation,
execution), the system requires some means of dealing with individuals'
feelings of guilt---and with public condemnation. This is achieved
through the sacrificial system, including sacrifices for unwitting
violations, by individuals or the community (Leviticus 4 and 5), and
sacrifices for various other feelings on the part of the offerer
(Leviticus 1--3). The system that provides grounds for guilt also
provides a mechanism for expiation and forgiveness.

Sacrifice is the only mechanism for forgiveness in the book of
Leviticus. There is no suggestion in Leviticus that repentance alone can
bring forgiveness for violations of the laws, no indication that one can
appeal to YHWH's mercy, His grace, or His kindness for atonement.
Indeed, the words "repentance" (*![Image](images/s-image.jpg){.block
height="11" width="7"}ûb*), "mercy" (*ra![Image](images/hdot.jpg){.block
height="15" width="7"}![Image](images/a1.jpg){.block height="12"
width="8"}mîm*), "grace" (*![Image](images/hdot.jpg){.block height="15"
width="7"}![Image](images/e1.jpg){.block height="14" width="6"}n*), and
"kindness" (*![Image](images/hdot.jpg){.block height="15"
width="7"}esed*) do not occur in Leviticus. Thus the psychological and
spiritual state of the community is linked powerfully to this visible,
tangible act. And this psychological and spiritual focus, together with
the very physical focus of the consumption of meat, places sacrifice in
a critical and pervasive role in the community's life. And it places the
priests who alone can perform sacrifice in a correspondingly critical
role. And, no less significantly, it concentrates the community's
attention on the single place where sacrifice can be performed: the
Tabernacle, or Tent of Meeting.

4:2. **by mistake**. This begins a lengthy section on what to do when
people sin by mistake: a priest, the entire community, a leader, or an
individual. Humans make mistakes. But even a sin that is committed by
mistake requires some act of atonement. People still feel guilty when
they do harm, even if they meant no harm, and so this provides a
mechanism for purging the guilt and putting the act in the past. Now, in
the absence of sacrifice, other means of atonement have risen in
importance. Notably, the Day of Atonement (Yom Kippur) has become the
most sacred and widely observed holiday; whereas, in the Torah, Passover
stands out as the first and foremost of the holidays.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 4:3*](#Contents.html_rlev4_3)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^3^"If the anointed priest will sin, causing the people's guilt, then he
shall bring forward for his sin that he committed: a bull of the cattle,
unblemished, for YHWH as a sin offering.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap4_03.jpg){height="70" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

4:3. **the anointed priest**. Hebrew *hakk![Image](images/o.jpg){.block
height="11" width="7"}h![Image](images/e1.jpg){.block height="14"
width="6"}n hamm![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11"
width="8"}![Image](images/s-image.jpg){.block height="11"
width="7"}îa![Image](images/hdot.jpg){.block height="15" width="7"}.*
This is the first occurrence of the word
*m![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11"
width="8"}![Image](images/s-image.jpg){.block height="11"
width="7"}îa![Image](images/hdot.jpg){.block height="15" width="7"},*
meaning "anointed" and commonly translated elsewhere in the *Tanak* as
"Messiah." In the Torah *m![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block
height="11" width="8"}![Image](images/s-image.jpg){.block height="11"
width="7"}îa![Image](images/hdot.jpg){.block height="15" width="7"}*
always refers to the high priest and not, as it later came to mean, the
king. The relationship between priest and king was always a complex one
in the *Tanak* and in the postbiblical world as well. The judge Gideon
declines to be made Israel's first king, but he acts as a priest, which
may well have involved greater power, distinction, and income (Judg
8:22--27). David makes some of his sons priests
(2![Image](images/s-image.jpg){.block height="11" width="7"}Sam 8:18).
The Hasmoneans (Maccabees) were initially high priests but later were
simultaneously priests and kings.

4:6. **the pavilion of the Holy**. The pavilion
(*p![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11"
width="8"}r![Image](images/o.jpg){.block height="11" width="7"}ket*) is
over the Holy of Holies, the inner part of the Tabernacle, not over the
Holy, the outer part. So why is it called "the pavilion of the Holy"
here![Image](images/s-image.jpg){.block height="11" width="7"} As in Lev
16:2, "the Holy" can refer generally to the holiness that is inside the
Holiest of Holies.

^22^"When a chieftain will sin and do one of any of the commandments of

4:22. **chieftain**. Hebrew *n![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block
height="11" width="8"}![Image](images/s1.jpg){.block height="11"
width="7"}î'.* Elsewhere in the Torah the word refers to the chieftain
of a tribe (Num 2:3--29) or of a priestly family (Num 3:24--35) or of a
city (Gen 34:2). Here it may allude to a king of Israel, "a chieftain
among your people" (as in 1 Kings 11:34; Ezek 34:24), or it may mean any
of these leaders.

[]{#Lev_Chapter05.html}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 5:1*](#Contents.html_rlev5_1)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **5** {.h2a}

^1^"And a person who will sin in that he has heard a pronouncement of an
oath, and he was a witness---whether he saw or he knew: if he will not
tell, then he shall bear his crime.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap5_01.jpg){height="50" width="340"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

5:1. **bear his crime**. This expression means that the person is not
punished by a court. It is between the individual and God. After all, in
this case, it may be that no one but the person himself is even aware
that he did the sin. The reverse expression occurs in the great formula
of God's compassion in Exod 34:7, where it says that *God* bears crime
(*n![Image](images/o.jpg){.block height="11"
width="7"}s![Image](images/e1.jpg){.block height="14" width="6"}'
'![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11"
width="8"}w![Image](images/o.jpg){.block height="11" width="7"}n*) for
humans. That very emphasis on the usual divine mercy makes cases like
the current one so frightening: here it is the human who must bear the
crime himself. This expression will be used in a number of cases in
Leviticus and Numbers. By saying that a case is beyond the jurisdiction
of any court or ruler, the Torah recognizes that some things are beyond
humans' ability and humans' right to judge or punish.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 5:2*](#Contents.html_rlev5_2)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^2^"Or a person who will touch any impure thing or the carcass of an
impure wild animal or the carcass of an impure domestic animal or the
carcass of an impure swarming creature, and it was hidden from him, so
he had become impure and had become guilty;

![Image](images/Lev_Chap5_02.jpg){height="98" width="344"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 5:3*](#Contents.html_rlev5_3)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^3^or when he will touch a human's impurity---for any impurity of his
through which he will become impure---and it was hidden from him, and
then he had come to know and had become guilty;

![Image](images/Lev_Chap5_03.jpg){height="73" width="349"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 5:4*](#Contents.html_rlev5_4)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^4^or a person who will swear so as to let out of his lips to do bad or
to do good---for anything that a human would let out in an oath---and it
was hidden from him, and then he had come to know and had become guilty
by one of these:

![Image](images/Lev_Chap5_04.jpg){height="96" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

confess that he has sinned over it,

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

has committed: a female from the flock, a sheep or a goat, as a sin
offering, and the priest shall make atonement over him from his sin.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap5_06.jpg){height="104" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

[*Leviticus 5:7*](#Contents.html_rlev5_7)

^7^"And if his hand will not attain enough for a sheep, then he shall
bring as his guilt offering for having sinned two turtledoves or two
pigeons to YHWH: one for a sin offering and one for a burnt offering.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap5_07.jpg){height="77" width="343"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

5:7. **if his hand will not attain enough**. Meaning: if he will not be
able to afford it. A person is not to be prevented from getting
atonement because of lack of money.

[*Leviticus 5:12*](#Contents.html_rlev5_12)

![Image](images/Lev_Chap5_12.jpg){height="71" width="340"}
