the number of years. It shall be with him like the days of an employee:

![Image](images/Lev_Chap25_50.jpg){height="70" width="339"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 25:51*](#Contents.html_rlev25_51)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^51^If there are still a great number of years, he shall pay back his
redemption according to them from his purchase price.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap25_51.jpg){height="47" width="340"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 25:52*](#Contents.html_rlev25_52)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^52^And if a small amount of years remain until the jubilee year, then
he shall figure it for him. He shall pay back his redemption according
to his years.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap25_52.jpg){height="74" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 25:53*](#Contents.html_rlev25_53)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^53^He shall be with him like a year-by-year employee. He shall not
dominate him with harshness before your eyes.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap25_53.jpg){height="46" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 25:54*](#Contents.html_rlev25_54)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^54^And if he will not be redeemed by these, then he shall go out in the
jubilee year, he and his children with him.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap25_54.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 25:55*](#Contents.html_rlev25_55)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^55^Because the children of Israel are servants to *me.* They are *my*
servants, whom I brought out from the land of Egypt. I am YHWH, your
God.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap25_55.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

[]{#Lev_Chapter26.html}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:1*](#Contents.html_rlev26_1)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **26** {.h2a}

^1^"You shall not make idols, and you shall not set up an image or a
pillar, and you shall not put a carved stone in your land to bow at it,
because I am YHWH, your God.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_01.jpg){height="74" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:2*](#Contents.html_rlev26_2)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^2^"You shall observe my Sabbaths and fear my sanctuary. I am YHWH.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_02.jpg){height="43" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

26:2. **observe my Sabbaths and fear my sanctuary**. What is the reason
for mentioning these two here together: Sabbaths and sanctuary? This
pronouncement brings together the sanctification of time and the
sanctification of space. The Sabbath is the most sacred time. The
sanctuary (meaning first the Tabernacle and later the Temple) is the
most sacred space. This dual command thus embraces everything.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:3*](#Contents.html_rlev26_3)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## B[Y MY LAWS]{.smallcaps1} {.h201}

^3^"If you will go by my laws, and if you will observe my commandments,
and you will do them:

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26.jpg){height="28" width="73"}

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_03.jpg){height="45" width="311"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

26:3. **If you will go by my laws**. The key to the relationship between
Leviticus's laws and the Bible's story is the list of blessings and
curses here in Leviticus 26. The list comes at the conclusion and is
arguably the culmination of the book. It indicates that all of this law
has implications. Leviticus has definite connections to what has come
before it. The blessings-and-curses list is the connection to what will
follow. It says, plainly enough: the people's fate will depend on
whether they follow these laws or not. The blessings include rain,
produce, satiety, security, peace, victory, population, divine presence,
and divine relationship. The curses include illness, defeat, wild
animals, pestilence, famine, divine rejection, destruction, dispersion,
and exile. The blessings and curses thus provide for the eventuality of
a variety of essential matters in the realms of nature, human relations
(especially political relations), and divine-human relationship. All
future stories of the fortunes of the people of Israel in these realms
are to be understood, according to this text, as being the results of
whether "you will go by my laws, and if you will observe my
commandments, and you will do them" (26:3) or "if you will reject my
laws, and if your souls will scorn my judgments so as not to do all my
commandments, so that you break my covenant" (26:15). Underscoring the
literary force of these promises and threats is not only their placement
at the conclusion of the book but the fact that the list of curses is
the most artfully written section in the book. I call it the most
artfully written on the basis of its wording as much as its content,
including words of irony ("you will flee though no one is pursuing you,"
26:17), words of horror ("you will eat your sons' flesh, and your
daughters' flesh you will eat," 26:29), and words of pathos ("the sound
of a driven leaf will chase them," 26:36). The commandments thus are not
presented as a loosely relevant list. They are woven into the fabric of
the narrative as essential to the life of the community.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:4*](#Contents.html_rlev26_4)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^4^ then I shall give your rains in their time, and the earth will give
its crop, and the tree of the field will give its fruit,

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_04.jpg){height="44" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:5*](#Contents.html_rlev26_5)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^5^ and threshing will extend to vintage for you, and vintage will
extend to seeding, and you will eat your bread to the full, and you will
live in security in your land,

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_05.jpg){height="71" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:6*](#Contents.html_rlev26_6)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^6^ and I shall give peace in the land, and you will lie down with no
one making you afraid, and I shall make wild animals cease from the
land, and a sword will not pass through your land,

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_06.jpg){height="67" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:7*](#Contents.html_rlev26_7)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^7^ and you will chase your enemies, and they will fall in front of you
by the sword,

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_07.jpg){height="22" width="336"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:8*](#Contents.html_rlev26_8)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^8^ and five of you will chase a hundred, and a hundred of you will
chase ten thousand, and your enemies will fall in front of you by the
sword,

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_08.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:9*](#Contents.html_rlev26_9)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^9^ and I shall turn to you, and I shall make you fruitful and make you
multiply, and I shall establish my covenant with you,

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_09.jpg){height="74" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:10*](#Contents.html_rlev26_10)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^10^ and you will eat the oldest of the old, and you will take out the
old because of the new,

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_10.jpg){height="47" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

26:10. **the oldest of the old**. Old, stored produce will still make
good food.

26:10. **take out the old because of the new**. One will never run out
of food. There will be good stored produce right up to the time that the
new crops come in, and the old will have to be taken out in order to
make room for the new.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:11*](#Contents.html_rlev26_11)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^11^ and I shall put my Tabernacle among you, and my soul will not scorn
you,

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_11.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

26:11. **my soul**. Does God have a soul? This is the only place in the
Torah that refers to the deity's *nepeš* (although a related verbal
form, *wayyinn![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11"
width="8"}paš,* occurs in Exod 31:17). Elsewhere, the word refers to the
living quality in humans and animals and is associated with breath. It
is usually understood to mean soul, person, being, and life. It might
possibly help us to understand what is meant by creation in the image of
God, but that seems unlikely since animals are said to have a *nepeš*
there as well, but they are not said to be in the divine image (Gen
1:24--27). We must be cautious in using the word's occurrences here to
conclude anything about the Torah's conception of God, because both of
these occurrences are in the phrase "my soul will scorn." This phrase
may simply have been a known expression. It also occurs here with humans
as the subject (26:15); and it occurs in Jeremiah as well (14:19). It
may refer to the personal aspect of God, or it may rather refer to God
in no specific way. Rashi (following Siphra) takes the phrase to refer
to the departure of the divine presence (Shechinah). Here, too, we must
be cautious, first, because that is also the understanding of other
metaphorical phrases in the *Tanak.* Thus the Targum takes the phrase
"God hides His face" to mean the departure of the Shechinah as well.
And, second, the word *Shechinah* never occurs in the Torah or anywhere
in the Hebrew Bible.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:12*](#Contents.html_rlev26_12)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^12^ and I shall walk among you, and I shall be God to you, and you will
be a people to me.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_12.jpg){height="47" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

26:12. **walk among you**. This same term is used to describe God's
walking among humans in the garden of Eden (the Hebrew root
![Image](images/f409-01.jpg){height="21" width="30"} in the *Hitpael;*
Gen 3:8). This blessing thus hints at a return of the closeness to the
divine presence that existed prior to the initial divine-human conflict
in Eden.

26:12. **I shall be God to you, and you will be a people to me**. The
last blessing of the list echoes what is thought to be the wording of
the marriage ceremony in ancient Israel: "I shall be a husband to you,
and you will be a wife to me"---as well as the adoption ceremony: "I
shall be a parent to you, and you will be a son/daughter to me" (see,
e.g., 2 Sam 7:14).

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:13*](#Contents.html_rlev26_13)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^13^I am YHWH, your God, who brought you out from the land of Egypt,
from being slaves to them, and I broke the beams of your yoke and had
you go standing tall.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_13.jpg){height="101" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:14*](#Contents.html_rlev26_14)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^14^"But if you will not listen to me and not do all these commandments,

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_14.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:15*](#Contents.html_rlev26_15)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^15^and if you will reject my laws, and if your souls will scorn my
judgments so as not to do all my commandments, so that you break my
covenant,

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_15.jpg){height="69" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:16*](#Contents.html_rlev26_16)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^16^I, too, I shall do this to you: Then I shall appoint terror over you
with consumption and with fever, exhausting eyes and grieving the soul,
and you will sow your seed in vain, for your enemies will eat it,

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_16.jpg){height="92" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:17*](#Contents.html_rlev26_17)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^17^ and I shall set my face against you, and you will be struck before
your enemies, and ones who hate you will dominate you, and you will flee
though no one is pursuing you.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_17.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:18*](#Contents.html_rlev26_18)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^18^And if, even after these, you will not listen to me, then I shall
add seven times more to discipline you for your sins:

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_18.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:19*](#Contents.html_rlev26_19)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^19^ and I shall break your strong pride, and I shall make your skies
like iron and your land like bronze,

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_19.jpg){height="45" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:20*](#Contents.html_rlev26_20)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^20^ and your power will be used up in vain, and your land will not give
its crop, and the tree of the land will not give its fruit.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_20.jpg){height="50" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:21*](#Contents.html_rlev26_21)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^21^And if you will go on with me in defiance and will not be willing to
listen to me, then I shall add seven times more striking on you,
corresponding to your sins:

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_21.jpg){height="70" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:22*](#Contents.html_rlev26_22)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^22^ and I shall let loose the wild animal among you, and it will
bereave you, and it will cut off your cattle, and it will diminish you,
and your roads will be desolated.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_22.jpg){height="71" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:23*](#Contents.html_rlev26_23)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^23^And if you will not be disciplined for me by these, and you will go
on with me in defiance,

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_23.jpg){height="48" width="336"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:24*](#Contents.html_rlev26_24)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^24^then I shall go on with you, I also, in defiance. And I shall strike
you, I too, seven times more for your sins:

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_24.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:25*](#Contents.html_rlev26_25)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^25^ and I shall bring a sword over you, requiting covenant retribution,
and you will be gathered to your cities, and I shall let an epidemic go
among you, and you will be put in your enemy's hand.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_25.jpg){height="74" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:26*](#Contents.html_rlev26_26)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^26^ When I break the staff of bread for you, then ten women will bake
your bread in one oven, and they will give back your bread by weight,
and you will eat and not be full.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_26.jpg){height="70" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:27*](#Contents.html_rlev26_27)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^27^And if, through this, you will not listen to me, and you will go on
with me in defiance,

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_27.jpg){height="45" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:28*](#Contents.html_rlev26_28)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^28^then I shall go on with you in a fury of defiance, and I shall
discipline you, I also, seven times more for your sins:

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_28.jpg){height="68" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:29*](#Contents.html_rlev26_29)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^29^ and you will eat your sons' flesh, and your daughters' flesh you
will eat,

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_29.jpg){height="47" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

26:29. **you will eat your sons' flesh and your daughters' flesh**. The
blessings and curses are not rewards and punishments. They are a formal
part of the biblical covenants (Leviticus 26; Deuteronomy 28)---with
exact parallels in legal contracts of the ancient Near East. They
express the *outcomes* of fulfillment or nonfulfillment of the
covenant's terms. A people who is faithful to its God and keeps the
Sabbath and honors its parents and does not steal will be prosperous and
enduring in its land. A people who loses sight of its commitments and
values will suffer. (It should not be hard to think of contemporary
parallels.) The frightful curse of eating one's own children comes true
centuries later in one of the most horrifying stories in the Bible (2
Kings 6:24--30). It is not presented there as a punishment. It rather
conveys the terrible state of things in Israel in the wake of the
heretical reigns of Kings Ahab and Jehoram. There is a big difference
between a punishment and a curse, between a threat and a warning.

This explanation is still distressing, but it does not so easily picture
the deity as malicious, as people have sometimes taken these curses to
mean. The God of the Hebrew Bible is not the "Old Testament God of
Wrath," but rather a deity who is torn between mercy and justice,
between affection for humankind and regret over the continuous conflict
with them. The curses are a sad outcome of a certain kind of human
behavior. But, then, the blessings are the outcome of the other kind.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:30*](#Contents.html_rlev26_30)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^30^ and I shall destroy your high places, and I shall cut off your
incense altars, and I shall put your carcasses on your idols' carcasses,
and my soul will scorn you,

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_30.jpg){height="69" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

26:30. **high places**. These were large stone platforms where
sacrifices were performed. Some were used for worship of YHWH, and
others were used for worship of pagan gods; but both kinds are regularly
forbidden in the *Tanak.* High places to the God of Israel are regarded
as violations of the laws of centralization of worship at a single site
(see comments on Lev 1:3 and 17:4).

26:30. **incense altars**. These were stands, usually designed, on which
incense was burned. Both high places and incense altars have been
excavated in Israel.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:31*](#Contents.html_rlev26_31)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^31^ and I shall make your cities a ruin, and I shall devastate your
holy places, and I shall not smell your pleasant smell,

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_31.jpg){height="70" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

26:31. **not smell your pleasant smell**. The "pleasant smell" has been
referred to many times as the product of the burning incense that
accompanies the sacrifices. Starting with Noah's sacrifice after the
flood, the smell of sacrifices is meant to be attractive to the deity,
but the curse here is that God will refuse to smell it.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:32*](#Contents.html_rlev26_32)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^32^ and I, *I,* shall devastate the land, so your enemies who live in
it will be astonished,

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_32.jpg){height="47" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:33*](#Contents.html_rlev26_33)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^33^ and you: I shall scatter among the nations, and I shall unsheathe
my sword after you, and your land will be a devastation, and your cities
will be a ruin.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_33.jpg){height="70" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

26:33. **scatter among the nations**. Among the worst curses was the
curse of exile: for a people to be driven off their land and scattered
around the world. This possibility was a reality in the ancient Near
East, where exile was a horrifying fate. This curse would come to pass
for Israel later in the *Tanak,* when they would come to know exile at
the hands of the Assyrians; and it would come to pass for Judah still
later at the hands of the Babylonians. But they would also come to know
the blessing of return.

26:33. **unsheathe my sword**. To appreciate the horror of the metaphor
of God saying He will go after Israel unsheathing a sword, one should
recall that this is exactly what the Egyptians say that they will do to
Israel in the Song of the Sea (Exod 15:9). That is, God, who is Israel's
protector, threatens a curse in which God would become like Israel's
enemies. It is one's worst nightmare: that one's loving, protecting
parents become a horrible threat.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:34*](#Contents.html_rlev26_34)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^34^Then the land will accept its Sabbaths: all the days of devastation,
while you are in your enemies' land. Then the land will cease and accept
its Sabbaths.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_34.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:35*](#Contents.html_rlev26_35)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^35^All the days of the desolation it will cease, the amount that it did
not cease on your Sabbaths when you lived on it.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_35.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:36*](#Contents.html_rlev26_36)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^36^And those of you who remain: I shall bring faintness in their hearts
in their enemies' lands, and the sound of a driven leaf will chase them,
and they will flee like the flight from a sword, and they will fall when
there is no one pursuing,

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_36.jpg){height="71" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:37*](#Contents.html_rlev26_37)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^37^ and they will stumble, each over his brother, as in front of a
sword, when there is no one pursuing, and you will not have a footing in
front of your enemies,

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_37.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:38*](#Contents.html_rlev26_38)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^38^ and you will perish among the nations, and your enemies' land will
eat you up,

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_38.jpg){height="44" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:39*](#Contents.html_rlev26_39)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^39^ and those of you who remain will rot for their crime in your
enemies' lands, and also for their fathers' crimes with them they will
rot.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_39.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:40*](#Contents.html_rlev26_40)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^40^"When they will confess their crime and their fathers' crime for
their breach that they made against me, and also that they went on with
me in defiance,

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_40.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

26:40. **their crime and their fathers' crime**. On one hand, this is
yet another reminder that it takes *generations* before the God of
Israel will take such actions. On the other hand, it means that the
Israelites must recognize that it is not only their own offenses that
have led to these things. They must come to terms with their parents'
offenses as well. The ultimate revelation of the divine at Sinai
includes the fact that the deity "reckons fathers' crime on children and
on children's children, on third generations and on fourth generations."
Now the perspective is turned to those children, generations later; and
we learn from the Torah, no less than from Freud, that humans must look
back not only at the good that their parents and grandparents did but
also at their errors and faults. It is an essential part of
understanding how we came to be what we are.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:41*](#Contents.html_rlev26_41)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^41^so I, I also, would go on with them in defiance, and I brought them
into their enemies' land; or if then their uncircumcised heart will be
humbled and then they will accept their crime,

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_41.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:42*](#Contents.html_rlev26_42)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^42^then I shall remember my covenant of Jacob, and I shall remember
also my covenant of Isaac and also my covenant of Abraham, and I shall
remember the land.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_42.jpg){height="73" width="340"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:43*](#Contents.html_rlev26_43)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^43^ And the land will be left from them, and it will accept its
Sabbaths while it is devastated from them, and they will accept their
crime, because and *only* because they rejected my judgments and their
soul scorned my laws.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_43.jpg){height="77" width="368"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:44*](#Contents.html_rlev26_44)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^44^"And even despite this, when they were in their enemies' land I did
not reject them and did not scorn them, to finish them, to break my
covenant with them, because I am YHWH, their God!

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_44.jpg){height="70" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:45*](#Contents.html_rlev26_45)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^45^So I shall remember for them the covenant of the first ones, whom I
brought out from the land of Egypt before the eyes of the nations to be
God to them. I am YHWH."

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_45.jpg){height="73" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 26:46*](#Contents.html_rlev26_46)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^46^These are the laws and the judgments and the instructions that YHWH
gave between Him and the children of Israel in Mount Sinai by Moses'
hand.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap26_46.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

26:46. **These are the laws and the judgments and the instructions**.
The present age plainly appears to value the ethical more than the
ritual in religion. Some even feel the need to justify ritual by
attempting to connect each ritual act to some ethical value: "We keep
kosher to remind us to care about animals; we wear fringes to remind us
to be kind\...." This is misleading. Certainly ritual acts can have
consequences in the ethical realm, but that is not their reason for
being. If we are to understand Leviticus, we must have an appreciation
of what ritual meant in its society intrinsically. Ritual can have
aesthetic, psychological, and inspirational value. It has the potential
of generating feelings of closeness to the sacred as well as feelings of
identity, stability, and security. For some, it means, in a world of
uncertainty, to be told what the "right" things are, what acts one can
perform to feel that one is acting in accordance with nothing less than
the will of the creator of the universe. In the matter of purity, for
example, one can believe that, when one has had contact with any of a
group of things that intimidate us but that are physiological facts of
life (blood and other bodily fluids, bodily sores, death), one can do
things that remove the condition that this contact has produced---and
thus remove the fear. In the matter of sacrifice, as we have observed,
the ritual has an impact on one's feeling of guilt. In the observance of
the holidays, the ritual contributes to feelings of festivity and
communal celebration---and the sanctification of time. And in the
variety of elaborate ceremonies performed at the Tent of Meeting,
officiated by the specially garbed priests, accompanied by the burning
of incense, the ritual contributes an aesthetic component to religion as
well. All of this is known---consciously or intuitively---to those who
have found meaning and even enjoyment in the practice of the rituals of
their own religion. The ritual and the ethical are two components of
religion---and of Leviticus---that do not justify each other, but rather
unite and produce mutual support. Indeed, it is instructive that
Leviticus, a book that is so fundamentally concerned with distinction,
does not make any explicit distinction between its ethical and its
ritual laws. Sometimes they are mixed together, but they are never
identified as two distinct categories of law.

We do a disservice when we interpret ritual matters in terms of ethical
justifications. We move ourselves even farther from the appreciation of
ritual and the awe before the holy. Similarly, when we say that the
Hebrew word *q*![Image](images/a-image.jpg){.block height="11"
width="8"}dōš means a neutral kind of "separate" rather than meaning
"holy, sacred," we further lose our feeling for holiness. And perhaps we
err similarly when we say that Hebrew
![Image](images/hdot.jpg){height="15"
width="7"}![Image](images/a-image.jpg){height="11"
width="8"}*t*![Image](images/a-image.jpg){height="11" width="8"} means
"to miss the mark" rather than "to sin," to do something wrong that
places us in a state that needs to be remedied through atonement and/or
sacrifice and/or compensation.

[]{#Lev_Chapter27.html}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 27:1*](#Contents.html_rlev27_1)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **27** {.h2a}

^1^And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying,

![Image](images/Lev_Chap27_1.jpg){height="22" width="230"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 27:2*](#Contents.html_rlev27_2)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^2^"Speak to the children of Israel. And you shall say to them: When a
man will express a vow by your appraisal of persons for YHWH,

![Image](images/Lev_Chap27_2.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

[*Leviticus 27:3*](#Contents.html_rlev27_3)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^3^then your appraisal shall be:

"A male from twenty years old up to sixty years old: then your appraisal
shall be fifty shekels of silver by the shekel of the Holy.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap27_3.jpg){height="72" width="340"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 27:4*](#Contents.html_rlev27_4)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^4^And if it is a female, then your appraisal shall be thirty shekels.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap27_4.jpg){height="46" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 27:5*](#Contents.html_rlev27_5)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^5^And if one is from five years old up to twenty years old, then your

shekels.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap27_5.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 27:6*](#Contents.html_rlev27_6)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^6^And if one is from a month old up to five years old, then your

for a female three shekels.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap27_6.jpg){height="94" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 27:7*](#Contents.html_rlev27_7)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

![Image](images/Lev_Chap27_7.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

[*Leviticus 27:8*](#Contents.html_rlev27_8)

front of the priest, and the priest shall appraise him. The priest shall

attain.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap27_8.jpg){height="74" width="339"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

![Image](images/Lev_Chap27_9.jpg){height="74" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

27:9. **an animal from which people would bring an offering**. Meaning:
an animal that is permissible for sacrifice, as opposed to an impure
animal (see v. 11).

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 27:10*](#Contents.html_rlev27_10)

it and the one traded for it shall be a holy thing.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap27_10.jpg){height="71" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 27:14*](#Contents.html_rlev27_14)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

27:21. **like a devoted field**. Anything that one formally devotes to

[*Leviticus 27:33*](#Contents.html_rlev27_33)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^33^He shall not inspect it either for good or for bad. And he shall not
trade it. And if he *will* trade it, then both it and the one traded for

![Image](images/Lev_Chap27_33.jpg){height="77" width="366"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Leviticus 27:34*](#Contents.html_rlev27_34)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^34^These are the commandments that YHWH commanded Moses for the
children of Israel in Mount Sinai.

![Image](images/Lev_Chap27_34.jpg){height="77" width="367"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

27:34. **These are the commandments that YHWH commanded Moses for the
children of Israel in Mount Sinai**. Literary studies of Leviticus are
likely to begin by raising the question of whether this book that
contains so much law and so little narrative belongs in a literary study
at all. But at bottom we do not have to argue that Leviticus is great
literature in order to justify its inclusion in a literary analysis. It
is not literature, not in the way in which almost anyone ever means that
term. But the literary study of the Hebrew Bible has to come to terms
with Leviticus, for it is embedded in the biblical narrative. Indeed,
the fact that no law code from ancient Israel survived independently,
that law codes survived only in contexts of narrative, is notable in
itself. Leviticus's laws are integral---and assumed thereafter. The book
begins with the notation that YHWH speaks these things to Moses from the
Tabernacle (1:1), and it ends here with the reminder that "These are the
commandments that YHWH commanded Moses for the children of Israel at
Mount Sinai." Some of the laws presume the preceding narrative, e.g.,
"You shall not do like what is done in the land of Egypt, in which you
lived ..." (18:3), which presumes the bondage in Egypt. Legal authority
is vested in the priests (the power to teach and enforce, 10:10--11),
which is an essential element in narratives in subsequent books. As new
political figures, especially kings, will rise, the compatibility or
conflict between them and the priests will be critical to the narrative,
as it was critical in history.

Leviticus thus is a design for an organized society of people who help
one another, who do not intentionally injure one another, who respect
one another's property and relationships, who regularly assemble to
celebrate together, who acknowledge their errors and atone for them, who
regard life---in humans and in animals---as sacred, who pursue purity in
various forms, who respect law, and who are utterly loyal to one God. It
is against the background of this design that the book of Numbers must
be understood.

[]{#Num_Title.html}

![Image](images/Num_title.jpg){height="302" width="527"}

[]{#Num_Front.html}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[COMMENTARY ON THE TORAH](#Contents.html_rnum_Front)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[T]{.dropcap}he book of Numbers is the story of a journey. Against the
backdrop of the book of Leviticus, with its absence of movement, Numbers
is entirely about movement. The journey as a literary theme has been a
recurring component of world literature from the epic of *Gilgamesh,*
the oldest known book, and the *Odyssey* to *Alice's Adventures in
Wonderland* and *The Wonderful Wizard of Oz.* It reflects real
experience, it is a ready metaphor for human lives, and it is a
connector between the world of literature and the world of dreams, in
which experiences of journeys are common. The journey in literature can
be the experience of an individual or a group. In the book of Numbers it
is both. It is the people of Israel's journey from Sinai to the border
of Canaan, from the site of the establishment of the covenant to the
place where it is to be fulfilled. And it is Moses' journey from Mount
Sinai to Mount Abarim, from the place where he has seen God
[]{#Num_Front.html_page_2}to the place of his death.

One must read the story in Numbers with a sense of what is pictured: a
great mass of people (603,550 adult males, plus the junior and elderly
males and the females of all ages), an entire nation, in movement---with
a visible miracle (the column of cloud and fire) and miraculous feeding
(manna) happening in their presence at all times over a span of nearly
forty years. It is the only age in the Bible in which miracle is a daily
fact of life. There is an organization for travel, a hierarchy for
community life (Moses, judges, priests), and formal rituals of time
(Sabbath, holidays, and new moons) and of space (sacrifice, incense,
vestments, the Tabernacle, fringes on clothing). There is a destination
anticipated in a future in which some of them will participate and some
will not. They are in an interim condition, conscious of the life in
Egypt behind them and the promised life in front of them. Their
mentality is a mentality of slaves. There are various groups and
individuals among them with an interest in positions of leadership. On
the evidence of the episodes of the story, they are to be pictured as
deeply afraid.

Against this setting, the deity is pictured frequently as speaking and
acting in response to human events rather than by divine initiation.
Divine communications are fewer and briefer than in the preceding books.
Leviticus is primarily concerned with God's giving the rules to the
people. Now Numbers is concerned with their first experiences living
under these rules and with God's responses to the courses of action they
take. Against the backdrop of the commandments of Leviticus, Numbers is
the story of a people coming to terms with having a constitution of
laws, and coming to terms with their relationship of holiness with the
deity. And in the middle of this stands Moses. The story is about the
people and God, but it is focused more than ever on Moses, his
personality and especially his conflicts.

[]{#Numbers01.html}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 1:1*](#Contents.html_rnum1_01)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## I[N THE WILDERNESS]{.smallcaps1} {.h201}

## **1** {.h2a}

^1^And YHWH spoke to Moses in the wilderness of Sinai in the Tent of
Meeting on the first day of the second month in the second year of their
exodus from the land of Egypt, saying,

![image](images/Num_Chap01_01.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

1:1. **wilderness**. Wilderness emerges through the narrative not only
as a setting but also as a theme of considerable significance. The
Hebrew title of the book, *b![Image](images/e-image.jpg){height="12"
width="7"}midbar,* "In the Wilderness," which derives from the opening
verse ("And YHWH spoke to Moses in the wilderness of Sinai
*\[b![Image](images/e-image.jpg){height="12" width="7"}midbar sînay\]*
... "), is a better indicator of the book's contents than the Greek
title, *arithmoi* (from which comes the English "Numbers"). It also
better captures the pervasive feeling of the book. The wilderness
depiction conveys two quite different qualities. On the one hand, the
wilderness years constitute a kind of ideal. The people's life is
orderly, protected, close to God. It is a period of incubation, of
nurturing. All is provided: food, water, direction. The miraculous is
the norm. At the same time, though, the wilderness is depicted as
terrible. Conditions are bad. The environment is hostile. There is
rebellion from within and fighting with peoples whom they encounter on
the way. There are power struggles and fear. And this is pictured as
having been almost entirely avoidable, a fate that has come upon the
people for having rejected the opportunity to enter the land. Numbers
thus expresses pervasively a notion that is only begun in Leviticus,
namely that closeness to the divine is both glorious and dangerous. This
dual picture of the ideal and the awesome sides of the wilderness years
is perhaps the most striking and singular aspect of the book of Numbers.
Numbers alone among the books of the Hebrew Bible recounts the story of
a single generation of the nation, involving the entire nation, in a
condition unlike that of any other time. In the earthly events and in
the cosmic aspect of both continuous and situational miracles, the
wilderness generation is like no other, and the book that tells its
story is correspondingly unique.

[*Numbers 1:49*](#Contents.html_rnum1_49)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^49^"Except: you shall not count the tribe of Levi, and you shall not
add up their heads among the children of Israel.

![image](images/Num_Chap01_49.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 1:50*](#Contents.html_rnum1_50)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^50^And you: appoint the Levites over the Tabernacle of the Testimony
and over all its equipment and over everything it has. They shall carry
the Tabernacle and all its equipment, and they shall attend to it, and
they shall camp around the Tabernacle.

![image](images/Num_Chap01_50.jpg){height="71" width="340"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 1:51*](#Contents.html_rnum1_51)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^51^And when the Tabernacle travels, the Levites shall take it down; and
when the Tabernacle camps, the Levites shall set it up. And an outsider
who comes close shall be put to death.

![image](images/Num_Chap01_51.jpg){height="46" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 1:52*](#Contents.html_rnum1_52)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^52^And the children of Israel shall camp, each at his camp, each at his
flag, by their army units,

![image](images/Num_Chap01_52.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

1:52. **flag**. Hebrew *degel* refers to some visible sign of the group
to which each Israelite belongs: a standard, banner, or flag of some
sort.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 1:53*](#Contents.html_rnum1_53)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^53^and the Levites shall camp all around the Tabernacle of the
Testimony, so there will not be a rage on the congregation of the
children of Israel, and the Levites shall keep the charge of the
Tabernacle of the Testimony."

![image](images/Num_Chap01_53.jpg){height="71" width="340"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

1:53. **a rage**. The Levites are to form a protective barrier to
prevent anyone from violating the Tabernacle, its equipment, and its
functions. When such a violation occurs in the Korah rebellion and its
aftermath, such a rage occurs, producing a plague that Aaron prevents
from spreading (Num 17:11); and then the Levites are again commanded to
perform this protective function (18:2--5).

commanded Moses. They did so.

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

[*Numbers 2:31*](#Contents.html_rnum2_31)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^31^All the counts of Dan's camp are one hundred fifty-seven thousand

![image](images/Num_Chap02_31.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 2:32*](#Contents.html_rnum2_32)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^32^These are the counts of the children of Israel by their fathers'
house, all the counts of the camps by their armies: six hundred three
thousand five hundred fifty.

![image](images/Num_Chap02_32.jpg){height="71" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

2:32. **six hundred three thousand five hundred fifty**. Add to this the
underage and elderly men, the women of all ages, and the Levites, and
the total number of Israelites must approach two million. It has been
calcu[]{#Numbers02.html_page_8}lated that by these numbers, marching
eight across, when the first Israelites reach Mount Sinai, half of them
would still be in Egypt! The extraordinary size of this population is a
famous old problem in traditional and critical biblical scholarship. The
numbers appear far too high; but they do not appear to be entirely
invented either, because what would be the motive for contriving them in
all of this tribe-by-tribe detail for the first four chapters of
Numbers? Some suggest that the word for "thousand" here means rather a
"clan," but that is not correct (see the comment on Num 3:43). One
possibility is that these are the numbers from the census that is
attributed to King David (2 Samuel 24; see the comment on Exod 30:12).
(There are just three censuses in the *Tanak,* the two censuses of Moses
and the census of David.) Coming centuries later, in a period in which
Israel is settled in the land, the numbers are more understandable in
the Davidic era (though they are questionably high even for that
period). In this scenario, the records here would have come from old
documents among the archives which would have come to be mixed in with
documents that were used as sources for the Torah.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 2:33*](#Contents.html_rnum2_33)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

![image](images/Num_Chap02_33.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

[*Numbers 3:1*](#Contents.html_rnum3_1)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **3** {.h2a}

^1^These are the records of Aaron and Moses in the day YHWH spoke to
Moses in Mount Sinai.

![image](images/Num_Chap03_01.jpg){height="49" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 3:2*](#Contents.html_rnum3_2)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^2^And these are the names of Aaron's sons: the firstborn Nadab, and
Abihu and Eleazar and Ithamar.

![image](images/Num_Chap03_02.jpg){height="48" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 3:3*](#Contents.html_rnum3_3)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^3^These are the names of Aaron's sons, the anointed priests, who filled
their hand to function as priests.

![image](images/Num_Chap03_03.jpg){height="72" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

3:3. **filled their hand to function as priests**. See comment on Exod
29:29.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 3:4*](#Contents.html_rnum3_4)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^4^And Nadab and Abihu died in front of YHWH when they brought forward
unfitting fire in front of YHWH in the wilderness of Sinai, and they had
no sons; and Eleazar and Ithamar functioned as priests in front of
Aaron, their father.

![image](images/Num_Chap03_04.jpg){height="98" width="340"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 3:5*](#Contents.html_rnum3_5)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^5^And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying,

![image](images/Num_Chap03_05.jpg){height="22" width="223"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 3:6*](#Contents.html_rnum3_6)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^6^"Bring forward the tribe of Levi and stand it in front of Aaron, the
priest, so they will attend to him.

![image](images/Num_Chap03_06.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 3:7*](#Contents.html_rnum3_7)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^7^And they shall keep his charge and the charge of all the congregation
in front of the Tent of Meeting, to do the work of the Tabernacle.

![image](images/Num_Chap03_07.jpg){height="71" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 3:8*](#Contents.html_rnum3_8)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^8^And they shall keep all the equipment of the Tent of Meeting and the
charge of the children of Israel, to do the work of the Tabernacle.

![image](images/Num_Chap03_08.jpg){height="72" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 3:9*](#Contents.html_rnum3_9)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^9^And you shall give the Levites to Aaron and to his sons. They are
given---*given*---to him from the children of Israel.

![image](images/Num_Chap03_09.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 3:10*](#Contents.html_rnum3_10)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^10^And you shall appoint Aaron and his sons, that they shall keep their
priesthood, and an outsider who comes close shall be put to death."

![image](images/Num_Chap03_10.jpg){height="73" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 3:11*](#Contents.html_rnum3_11)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^11^And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying,

![image](images/Num_Chap03_11.jpg){height="23" width="214"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 3:12*](#Contents.html_rnum3_12)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^12^"And I, here, I have taken the Levites from among the children of
Israel in place of every firstborn, the first birth of the womb, from
the children of Israel. And the Levites shall be mine.

![image](images/Num_Chap03_12.jpg){height="96" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 3:27*](#Contents.html_rnum3_27)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^27^And Kohath had the Amramite family and the Izharite family and the
Hebronite family and the Uzzielite family. These are the Kohathite
families.

![image](images/Num_Chap03_27.jpg){height="75" width="340"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 3:28*](#Contents.html_rnum3_28)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^28^By the number of every male from a month old and up: eight thousand
six hundred keeping the charge of the Holy.

![image](images/Num_Chap03_28.jpg){height="75" width="340"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 3:29*](#Contents.html_rnum3_29)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^29^The families of the children of Kohath were to camp to the south.

![image](images/Num_Chap03_29.jpg){height="49" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 3:30*](#Contents.html_rnum3_30)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^30^And the chieftain of the father's house for the Kohathite families
was Elizaphan son of Uzziel.

![image](images/Num_Chap03_30.jpg){height="50" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 3:31*](#Contents.html_rnum3_31)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^31^And their charge was the ark and the table and the menorah and the
altars and the equipment of the Holy with which they minister and the
cover and all of its service.

![image](images/Num_Chap03_31.jpg){height="75" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

3:31; **menorah**. The lampstand has been compared in appearance---and
as a symbol---to a tree, and especially to the tree of life. It is
therefore notable that it is mentioned here as being the responsibility
of the Kohathites. And then just a few verses later the Kohathites are
identified as the Levite group who must be careful that their closeness
to the holy not cause them to die (see the comment on 4:15). The
menorah, the sacred object, which became a symbol of Israel (more
properly so than the six-pointed star that became a symbol some two
thousand years after this text), is also a reminder of the essential
human relationship to life and death that begins the Torah.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 3:32*](#Contents.html_rnum3_32)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^32^And the chieftain of chieftains of the Levites was Eleazar son of
Aaron, the priest, with responsibility for those who keep the charge of
the Holy.

![image](images/Num_Chap03_32.jpg){height="73" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

[*Numbers 3:33*](#Contents.html_rnum3_33)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 3:34*](#Contents.html_rnum3_34)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 3:38*](#Contents.html_rnum3_38)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^38^And those who camped in front of the Tabernacle, on the east, in

death.

![image](images/Num_Chap03_38.jpg){height="98" width="342"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 3:39*](#Contents.html_rnum3_39)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^39^All the counts of the Levites that Moses and Aaron made by YHWH's
word, by their families, every male from a month old and up were
twenty-two thousand.

![image](images/Num_Chap03_39.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 3:40*](#Contents.html_rnum3_40)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^40^And YHWH said to Moses, "Count every male firstborn of the children
of Israel from a month old and up, and add up the number of their names.

![image](images/Num_Chap03_40.jpg){height="72" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 3:41*](#Contents.html_rnum3_41)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^41^And you shall take the Levites for me---I am YHWH---in place of
every firstborn in the children of Israel, and the Levites' animals in
place of every firstborn in the animals of the children of Israel."

![image](images/Num_Chap03_41.jpg){height="100" width="339"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 3:42*](#Contents.html_rnum3_42)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^42^And Moses counted, as YHWH commanded him, every firstborn in the
children of Israel.

![image](images/Num_Chap03_42.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 3:43*](#Contents.html_rnum3_43)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^43^And every male firstborn, by the number of names, from a month old
and up, by their counts were twenty-two thousand two hundred
seventy-three.

![image](images/Num_Chap03_43.jpg){height="94" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

3:43. **twenty-two thousand**. The number of firstborn Israelite males
is given here as 22,273. This indicates that those who take the word for
"thousand" (Hebrew *'elep*) to mean "clan" are mistaken, since firstborn
sons as a group do not constitute clans. Those who try to understand
this term as "clan" do this because they are troubled by the high
numbers of Israelites in the census in Numbers. But we cannot escape the
problem by redefining a term. Whatever we believe to have been the
historical case, the fact is that the text depicts a vast population in
the wilderness. And my concern is not to debate the numbers but rather
to recognize the significance of the numbers to the Torah and to its
story. Historically, only a portion of the Israelites may have had the
experience of slavery in Egypt, but all of Israel came to see the
experience as their own. For the Torah, it is important that the entire
people of Israel be seen as present at Mount Sinai.

The complication of this number of 22,273 firstborn males of all ages is
that it is utterly out of proportion to the number of over 600,000 adult
males. I have seen no satisfactory solution to this problem.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 4:1*](#Contents.html_rnum4_1)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **4** {.h2a}

^1^And YHWH spoke to Moses and to Aaron, saying,

![image](images/Num_Chap04_01.jpg){height="22" width="310"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

of Levi by their families, by their fathers' house,

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 4:4*](#Contents.html_rnum4_4)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

![image](images/Num_Chap04_04.jpg){height="51" width="339"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 4:5*](#Contents.html_rnum4_5)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^5^"And Aaron and his sons shall come when the camp is to travel, and
they shall take down the covering pavilion and cover the ark of the

![image](images/Num_Chap04_05.jpg){height="74" width="339"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 4:6*](#Contents.html_rnum4_6)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

![image](images/Num_Chap04_06.jpg){height="73" width="342"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 4:7*](#Contents.html_rnum4_7)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

they shall put on it the dishes and the pans and the bowls and the
libation jars; and the continual bread shall be on it.

![image](images/Num_Chap04_07.jpg){height="75" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 4:8*](#Contents.html_rnum4_8)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^8^And they shall spread a scarlet fabric over them and cover it with a

![image](images/Num_Chap04_08.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 4:9*](#Contents.html_rnum4_9)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

[*Numbers 4:10*](#Contents.html_rnum4_10)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

![image](images/Num_Chap04_10.jpg){height="47" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 4:11*](#Contents.html_rnum4_11)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^11^And on the gold altar they shall spread a blue fabric, and they
shall cover it with a leather covering and set its poles.

![image](images/Num_Chap04_11.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 4:12*](#Contents.html_rnum4_12)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^12^And they shall take all the equipment for ministering with which
they minister in the Holy and put them into a blue fabric and cover them
with a leather covering and put them on a beam.

![image](images/Num_Chap04_12.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 4:13*](#Contents.html_rnum4_13)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^13^And they shall clear the ashes from the altar and spread a purple
fabric over it.

![image](images/Num_Chap04_13.jpg){height="22" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 4:14*](#Contents.html_rnum4_14)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^14^And they shall put on it all its equipment with which they minister
at it---the fire-holders, the forks and the shovels and the basins---all
the altar's equipment, and they shall spread a leather covering on it
and set its poles.

![image](images/Num_Chap04_14.jpg){height="96" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 4:15*](#Contents.html_rnum4_15)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^15^And Aaron and his sons will finish covering the Holy and all the
equipment of the Holy when the camp is to travel, and after that the
children of Kohath shall come to carry, so they shall not touch the holy
and die. These are what the children of Kohath are to carry in the Tent
of Meeting.

![image](images/Num_Chap04_15.jpg){height="97" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

4:15. **so they shall not touch the holy and die**. This is followed by:
"they shall not come to see as the Holy is covered up and die" (4:20).
Thus, twice more the text reminds us that closeness to the divine is
dangerous. The death of Aaron's sons stands in the background of these
warnings. (In fact, it is mentioned in the preceding chapter; 3:4.) And
these warnings also remind us of the distinction between the ritual and
moral realms. Whatever their intentions, whatever their moral condition,
if these Kohathites touch what they are not supposed to touch or see
what they are not supposed to see, they will die.

[*Numbers 5:1*](#Contents.html_rnum5_1)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **5** {.h2a}

^1^And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying,

![image](images/Num_Chap05_01.jpg){height="22" width="223"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 5:2*](#Contents.html_rnum5_2)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^2^"Command the children of Israel that they shall have every leper and
everyone who has an emission and everyone who is impure by a person go
from the camp.

![image](images/Num_Chap05_02.jpg){height="67" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

5:2. **impure by a person**. Meaning: something that has become impure
because of contact with the dead body of a person.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 5:3*](#Contents.html_rnum5_3)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^3^Male or female, you shall have them go; you shall have them go
outside the camp so they will not make their camps, among which I tent,
impure."

![image](images/Num_Chap05_03.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 5:4*](#Contents.html_rnum5_4)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^4^And the children of Israel did so, and they had them go outside the
camp. As YHWH spoke to Moses, so the children of Israel did.

![image](images/Num_Chap05_04.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 5:5*](#Contents.html_rnum5_5)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^5^And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying,

![image](images/Num_Chap05_05.jpg){height="46" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 5:6*](#Contents.html_rnum5_6)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^6^"Speak to the children of Israel: A man or a woman---when they will
do any of the sins of humans, to make a breach against YHWH---so that

![image](images/Num_Chap05_06.jpg){height="75" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

![image](images/Num_Chap05_07.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

[*Numbers 5:8*](#Contents.html_rnum5_8)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

![image](images/Num_Chap05_08.jpg){height="71" width="341"}
