^8^And YHWH said to Moses, "Make a fiery one and set it on a pole, and
it will be that everyone who is bitten and sees it will live."

![image](images/Num_Chap21_08.jpg){height="46" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 21:9*](#Contents.html_rnum21_9)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^9^And Moses made a bronze snake and set it on a pole, and it was, if a
snake bit a man, then he would look at the bronze snake and live.

![image](images/Num_Chap21_09.jpg){height="70" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

21:9. **a bronze snake**. When someone is bitten by a snake, the victim
can look at the bronze snake of Moses and be healed. This story is the
only case of what appears to be sympathetic magic in these books. It is
also the only depiction of a sacred object that is not associated with
the Tent of Meeting and its contents. Related to this fact, and perhaps
more significant, it is the only sacred object that is not connected in
any way with Aaron and the priests of his family. Rather, it is
personally made and mounted by Moses himself. This fits with the fact
that it was *Moses'* staff that miraculously became a snake at the
burning bush and in Egypt (Exod 4:2--3); Aaron's staff became a
different kind of serpent (Exod 7:8--10; see the comment on 7:9). The
text is unclear as to whether the snake is used only on the occasion of
this crisis or if it continues to be mounted in some visible location.
On the evidence of an event reported in the book of 2 Kings (18:4), it
seems likely that we should understand it as continuing to be in public
view even after the crisis is past. In that account, we are told that
Moses' snake was called Nehushtan and that King Hezekiah destroyed it
because the Israelites had been burning incense to it. (Concerning the
reason why a Judean king would have dared to destroy something that was
believed to have been made by Moses himself, see my *Who Wrote the
Bible?* pp. 126, 210--211.)

Another point: The two signs that God gives Moses at the burning bush
are that his staff becomes a snake and his hand becomes leprous like
snow (Exod 4:1--8). These now are seen to foreshadow events in Numbers:
the snake on a pole (Num 21:5--9) and Miriam's becoming leprous like
snow (Numbers 12).

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 21:10*](#Contents.html_rnum21_10)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^10^And the children of Israel traveled, and they camped in Oboth.

![image](images/Num_Chap21_10.jpg){height="22" width="240"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 21:11*](#Contents.html_rnum21_11)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^11^And they traveled from Oboth, and they camped in Iye-abarim, in the
wilderness that is toward Moab, toward the sun's rising.

![image](images/Num_Chap21_11.jpg){height="68" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 21:12*](#Contents.html_rnum21_12)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^12^From there they traveled, and they camped in the Wadi Zered.

![image](images/Num_Chap21_12.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 21:13*](#Contents.html_rnum21_13)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^13^From there they traveled, and they camped across the Arnon, which is
in the wilderness that extends from the border of the Amorite, because
Arnon is Moab's border, between Moab and the Amorite.

![image](images/Num_Chap21_13.jpg){height="72" width="342"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 21:14*](#Contents.html_rnum21_14)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^14^On account of this it is said in the Scroll of the Wars of YHWH:

Waheb in Suphah and the wadis of Arnon,

![image](images/Num_Chap21_14.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

[]{#Numbers21.html_page_74}21:14. **the Scroll of the Wars of YHWH**.
The Torah here indicates that there are known older, written sources,
which it may sometimes use or cite.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 21:15*](#Contents.html_rnum21_15)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^15^and the slope of the wadis that reached to the settlement of Ar, and
pressed to Moab's border.

![image](images/Num_Chap21_15.jpg){height="46" width="285"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 21:16*](#Contents.html_rnum21_16)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^16^And from there to Beer: that is the well of which YHWH said to
Moses, "Gather the people, so I may give them water."

![image](images/Num_Chap21_16.jpg){height="47" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 21:17*](#Contents.html_rnum21_17)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^17^Then Israel sang this song:

Spring up, well. Sing to it.

![image](images/Num_Chap21_17.jpg){height="68" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

21:17. **Then Israel sang**. The poems come relatively late in the book
(with the possible exception of Num 12:6--8), and their effect is likely
to be heightened for many readers on account of this. After
twenty-and-a-half chapters of rebellion episodes, lists, and laws, we
read that "Then Israel sang" (21:17). It is the first report of song
since the account of the crossing of the Red Sea, which culminated with
the Song of the Sea (introduced with comparable words: "Then Moses and
the children of Israel sang," Exod 15:1).

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 21:18*](#Contents.html_rnum21_18)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^18^The commanders hewed the well, the people's nobles dug it,

with a scepter, with their staffs.

And from the wilderness to Mattanah,

![image](images/Num_Chap21_18.jpg){height="47" width="268"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 21:19*](#Contents.html_rnum21_19)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^19^and from Mattanah to Nahaliel, and from Nahaliel to Bamoth,

![image](images/Num_Chap21_19.jpg){height="22" width="261"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 21:20*](#Contents.html_rnum21_20)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^20^and from Bamoth in the valley, that is in the field of Moab, to the
top of Pisgah, which looks toward Jeshimon.

![image](images/Num_Chap21_20.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 21:21*](#Contents.html_rnum21_21)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^21^And Israel sent messengers to Sihon, king of the Amorites, saying,

![image](images/Num_Chap21_21.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 21:22*](#Contents.html_rnum21_22)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^22^"Let me pass through your land. We won't turn in at a field and at a
vineyard and won't drink well waters. We'll go by the king's road until
we pass your border."

![image](images/Num_Chap21_22.jpg){height="71" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 21:23*](#Contents.html_rnum21_23)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^23^And Sihon did not allow Israel to pass within his border. And Sihon
gathered all his people and went out to Israel at the wilderness and
came to Jahaz and fought against Israel.

![image](images/Num_Chap21_23.jpg){height="96" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 21:24*](#Contents.html_rnum21_24)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^24^And Israel struck him by the sword and took possession of his land
from Arnon to Jabbok, to the children of Ammon, because the border of
the children of Ammon was strong.

![image](images/Num_Chap21_24.jpg){height="70" width="340"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 21:25*](#Contents.html_rnum21_25)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^25^And Israel took all of these cities, and Israel lived in all the
Amorite cities in Heshbon and all of its environs,

![image](images/Num_Chap21_25.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 21:26*](#Contents.html_rnum21_26)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^26^because Heshbon was the city of Sihon, the king of the Amorite, and
he fought against the former king of Moab and took all of his land from
his hand as far as Arnon.

![image](images/Num_Chap21_26.jpg){height="71" width="340"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 21:27*](#Contents.html_rnum21_27)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^27^On account of this they say proverbially:

Come to Heshbon.

Built and founded is Sihon's city.

![image](images/Num_Chap21_27.jpg){height="47" width="277"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 21:28*](#Contents.html_rnum21_28)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^28^For fire went out of Heshbon,

flame from Sihon's town;

it consumed Ar of Moab,

the lords of the high places of Arnon.

![image](images/Num_Chap21_28.jpg){height="47" width="301"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

21:28. **high places**. High places are large flat platforms where
sacrificial worship takes place. They are used among the Canaanites; and
later the Israelites use them, sometimes for worship of YHWH and
sometimes for worship of pagan gods. High places are forbidden to
Israelites, whether for YHWH or for pagan gods. Israelites are allowed
to sacrifice in only one place: at the Tent of Meeting, "the place where
YHWH tents His name" (Lev 17:5; Deut 12:5--6).

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 21:29*](#Contents.html_rnum21_29)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^29^Woe unto you, Moab:

you've perished, Chemosh's people.

He's made his sons refugees

and his daughters captive

to the king of the Amorite, Sihon.

![image](images/Num_Chap21_29.jpg){height="71" width="225"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 21:30*](#Contents.html_rnum21_30)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^30^And their fiefdom perished,

Heshbon to Dibon,

and we devastated up to Nophah

which reaches to Medeba.

![image](images/Num_Chap21_30.jpg){height="44" width="223"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 21:31*](#Contents.html_rnum21_31)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^31^And Israel lived in the Amorite's land.

![image](images/Num_Chap21_31.jpg){height="25" width="225"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 21:32*](#Contents.html_rnum21_32)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^32^And Moses sent to spy on Jazer, and they captured its environs and
dispossessed the Amorite who was there.

![image](images/Num_Chap21_32.jpg){height="70" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 21:33*](#Contents.html_rnum21_33)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^33^And they turned and went up the road of Bashan, and Og, the king of
Bashan, went out to them, he and all his people, to war at Edrei.

![image](images/Num_Chap21_33.jpg){height="70" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 21:34*](#Contents.html_rnum21_34)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^34^And YHWH said to Moses, "Don't fear him, because I've delivered him
and all his people and his land into your hand, and you'll do to him as
you did to Sihon, king of the Amorite, who lived in Heshbon."

![image](images/Num_Chap21_34.jpg){height="96" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 21:35*](#Contents.html_rnum21_35)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^35^And they struck him and his sons and all his people until he did not
have a remnant left, and they took possession of his land.

![image](images/Num_Chap21_35.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

21:35. **they took possession of his land**. The Israelites' eastern
route next takes them to the territory of the Amorite kings Sihon and
Og, and Israel makes the same request for peaceful passage that it has
made to Edom. Both Sihon and Og attack Israel militarily, and both are
utterly defeated. Thus Israel comes to acquire territory on the eastern
side of the Jordan River, outside the borders of the promised land (Num
21:21--35).

[]{#Numbers22.html}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:1*](#Contents.html_rnum22_1)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## B[ALAK]{.smallcaps1} {.h201}

## **22** {.h2a}

^1^And the children of Israel traveled, and they camped in the plains of
Moab, across the Jordan from Jericho.

![image](images/Num_Chap22.jpg){height="34" width="49"}

![image](images/Num_Chap22_01.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:2*](#Contents.html_rnum22_2)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^2^And Balak, son of Zippor, saw everything that Israel had done to the
Amorite.

![image](images/Num_Chap22_02.jpg){height="47" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:3*](#Contents.html_rnum22_3)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^3^And Moab was very fearful because of the people because it was
numerous, and Moab felt a disgust at the children of Israel.

![image](images/Num_Chap22_03.jpg){height="45" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

22:3. **Moab**. The descendants of Lot and his daughter (Gen 19:36--37).

22:3. **it was numerous, and Moab felt a disgust**. History repeats
itself. Moab's fears are expressly like those of Egypt at the time of
the oppression of the Israelites. A cluster of parallel wordings conveys
this: Israel []{#Numbers22.html_page_75}was "numerous" (cf. Exod 1:9).
Moab "felt a disgust at the children of Israel"; these exact words are
used with regard to the Egyptians (Exod 1:12). Israel is "more powerful
than I" (Num 22:6; cf. Exod 1:9). Israel "has covered the eye of the
land"; these words are used to describe the locust plague (Exod
10:5,15). Egypt set out to control the too-numerous Israelites; Moab
sets out to drive them away. But the irony in both cases is that they
*perceive* Israel to be a threat because it is so big, but Israel is in
fact no threat at all. Israel is not said to have any hostile intentions
at all. It is the actions that Egypt and Moab take on the basis of their
mistaken perceptions that bring catastrophes on them. It is a strange
human characteristic, taking the very existence of someone who is
different and strong to be a threat.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:4*](#Contents.html_rnum22_4)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^4^And Moab said to the elders of Midian, "Now the community will lick
up all of our surroundings the way an ox licks up the plants of a
field!"

And Balak, son of Zippor, was king of Moab at that time.

![image](images/Num_Chap22_04.jpg){height="96" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

22:4. **Moab ... Midian**. The Moabites, who are descendants of Lot,
ally with the Midianites, who are direct descendants of Abraham himself
(Gen 25:2). The forces that caused the ancestors to separate from one
another now escalate into hostility among the descendants. And the
kindness and family fidelity that Abraham showed when he rescued Lot
(Genesis 14) are no longer factors in the minds of Lot's descendants.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:5*](#Contents.html_rnum22_5)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^5^And he sent messengers to Balaam, son of Beor, at Pethor, which is on
the river---the land of the children of his people---to call him,
saying, "Here, a people has come out from Egypt. Here, it has covered
the eye of the land. And it's sitting across from me!

![image](images/Num_Chap22_05.jpg){height="100" width="344"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

22:5. **Balaam, son of Beor**. Balaam, son of Beor, is described in a
plaster inscription from Deir 'Alla that was discovered in 1967. He is
the earliest person mentioned in the Bible who is also mentioned in an
archaeological source.

22:5 **covered the eye of the land**. Meaning: the Israelites are so
many that they make the ground invisible. This is the same expression
that is used to describe the plague of the locust swarm in Egypt.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:6*](#Contents.html_rnum22_6)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^6^And now, go, curse this people for me, because it's more powerful
than I---maybe I'll be able: we'll strike it, and I'll drive it out from
the land---because I know that whoever you'll bless will be blessed, and
whoever you'll curse will be cursed."

![image](images/Num_Chap22_06.jpg){height="100" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:7*](#Contents.html_rnum22_7)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^7^And Moab's elders and Midian's elders went, and divination implements
were in their hand, and they came to Balaam and spoke Balak's words to
him.

![image](images/Num_Chap22_07.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:8*](#Contents.html_rnum22_8)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^8^And he said to them, "Spend the night here tonight, and I'll bring
back word to you as YHWH will speak to me." And the chiefs of Moab
stayed with Balaam.

![image](images/Num_Chap22_08.jpg){height="70" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:9*](#Contents.html_rnum22_9)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^9^And God came to Balaam and said, "Who are these people with you?"

![image](images/Num_Chap22_09.jpg){height="46" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

22:9. **God came to Balaam**. It is surely significant that the Torah, a
work that comes from Israel, pictures the creator as communicating with
a non-Israelite prophet as well. It is a reminder that the Torah begins
with the story of the connection between God and all of the earth. The
narrowing of focus to Abraham and to his descendants is said to be so
that they will be a blessing to all the families of the earth. Indeed
the connection between Balaam and that promise given in God's first
words to Abraham is explicit. The full wording to Abraham is: "And I'll
bless those who bless you, and those who affront you I'll curse. And all
the families of the earth will be blessed through you" (Gen 12:3). And
soon Balaam will bless Israel with the parallel words: "Those who bless
you: he's blessed. And those who curse you: he's cursed" (Num 24:9).

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:10*](#Contents.html_rnum22_10)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^10^And Balaam said to God, "Balak, son of Zippor, king of Moab, sent to
me:

![image](images/Num_Chap22_10.jpg){height="50" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:11*](#Contents.html_rnum22_11)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^11^'Here is a people who came out from Egypt and has covered the eye of
the land. Now go, execrate it for me. Maybe I'll be able to fight
against it, and I'll drive it out.'"

![image](images/Num_Chap22_11.jpg){height="70" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:12*](#Contents.html_rnum22_12)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^12^And God said to Balaam, "You shall not go with them. You shall not
curse the people, because it is blessed."

![image](images/Num_Chap22_12.jpg){height="46" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:13*](#Contents.html_rnum22_13)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^13^And Balaam got up in the morning and said to Balak's chiefs, "Go to
your land, because YHWH refused to allow me to go with you."

![image](images/Num_Chap22_13.jpg){height="67" width="339"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:14*](#Contents.html_rnum22_14)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^14^And the chiefs of Moab got up and came to Balak and said, "Balaam
refused to go with us."

![image](images/Num_Chap22_14.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

22:14. **Balaam refused**. Like the Egyptians toward Joseph, and like
the Israelites toward Moses, the Moabites focus on the human rather than
on God. What Balaam said was, "*YHWH* refused to allow me to go with
you." But the Moabite emissaries report, "*Balaam* refused to go with
us." Relating to the prophet rather than to the God who communicates
through the prophet is presented as a fundamental human characteristic.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:15*](#Contents.html_rnum22_15)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^15^And Balak went on again to send more numerous and prestigious chiefs
than these.

![image](images/Num_Chap22_15.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:16*](#Contents.html_rnum22_16)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^16^And they came to Balaam and said to him, "Balak, son of Zippor, said
this: 'Don't be held back from coming to me,

![image](images/Num_Chap22_16.jpg){height="69" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:17*](#Contents.html_rnum22_17)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^17^because I'll *honor* you very much, and I'll do everything that
you'll say to me. And go, *execrate* this people for me.'"

![image](images/Num_Chap22_17.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:18*](#Contents.html_rnum22_18)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^18^And Balaam answered, and he said to Balak's servants, "If Balak
would give me a houseful of silver and gold of his I wouldn't be able to
go against the mouth of YHWH, my God, to do something small or big.

![image](images/Num_Chap22_18.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:19*](#Contents.html_rnum22_19)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^19^And now, stay here, you as well, tonight, so I may know what YHWH
will add to speak with me."

![image](images/Num_Chap22_19.jpg){height="75" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:20*](#Contents.html_rnum22_20)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^20^And God came to Balaam at night and said to him, "If these people
have come to call you, get up, go with them. And just the thing that I
shall speak to you---you shall do *that.*"

![image](images/Num_Chap22_20.jpg){height="94" width="342"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:21*](#Contents.html_rnum22_21)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^21^And Balaam got up in the morning and harnessed his ass and went with
the chiefs of Moab.

![image](images/Num_Chap22_21.jpg){height="50" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:22*](#Contents.html_rnum22_22)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^22^And God's anger flared because he was going. And an angel of YHWH
stood up in the road as an adversary to him. And he was riding on his
ass, and his two boys were with him.

![image](images/Num_Chap22_22.jpg){height="71" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

22:22. **God's anger flared because he was going**. But did God not just
tell him to go (v. 20)?! The answer may lie in v. 32; see below.

22:22. **an angel ... his ass ... his two boys**. Note the parallels to
the story of the binding of Isaac: he takes an ass and two servant boys,
and he encounters an angel. And a life is spared. And altars are built.
And a ram is sacrificed as a burnt offering. And both stories involve
the doubled, emphatic form of the verb "to bless" (Gen 22:17; Num
24:10). And both involve the number three. And both end with the same
three verbs: got up, went, returned. This may thus be another signal
that the merit of the patriarchs protects their descendants, the
children of Israel. On the basis of Abraham's obedience at Moriah, God
promises (Gen 22:17) to make his descendants numerous, which is the
thing that now distresses Balak (22:3); and God promises to bless his
descendants, which He now does through Balaam (24:10).

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:23*](#Contents.html_rnum22_23)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^23^And the ass saw the angel of YHWH standing up in the road and his
sword drawn in his hand, and the ass turned from the road and went into
the field. And Balaam hit the ass to turn her back to the road.

![image](images/Num_Chap22_23.jpg){height="90" width="343"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:24*](#Contents.html_rnum22_24)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^24^And the angel of YHWH stood in the pathway of the vineyards, a fence
on this side and a fence on that side.

![image](images/Num_Chap22_24.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:25*](#Contents.html_rnum22_25)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^25^And the ass saw the angel of YHWH, and she was pressed against the
wall, and she pressed Balaam's foot against the wall, and he continued
hitting her.

![image](images/Num_Chap22_25.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:26*](#Contents.html_rnum22_26)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^26^And the angel continued passing on and stood in a narrow place where
there was no way to turn right or left.

![image](images/Num_Chap22_26.jpg){height="69" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:27*](#Contents.html_rnum22_27)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^27^And the ass saw the angel of YHWH, and she lay down under Balaam.
And Balaam's anger flared, and he struck the ass with a stick.

![image](images/Num_Chap22_27.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:28*](#Contents.html_rnum22_28)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^28^And YHWH opened the ass's mouth, and she said to Balaam, "What have
I done to you that you've struck me these three times?!"

![image](images/Num_Chap22_28.jpg){height="68" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:29*](#Contents.html_rnum22_29)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^29^And Balaam said to the ass, "Because you abused me! If there had
been a sword in my hand I would have killed you by now!"

![image](images/Num_Chap22_29.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

22:29. **Balaam said to the ass**. As usual, the text does not provide
details about emotions, and so we do not know if Balaam's answer is
expressed with amazement at hearing his ass talk or if he is to be
pictured as just answering matter-of-factly without yet realizing that
something extraordinary is happening. This heightens the comic effect.
Some people resist seeing humor in the Bible, as if being funny is
contrary to the book's dignity. Their error is not that they value the
Bible too much but rather that they value humor too little. Humor is
such an essential part of human life that the Bible would be strange and
incomplete without it.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:30*](#Contents.html_rnum22_30)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^[]{#Numbers22.html_page_76}30^And the ass said to Balaam, "Aren't I
your ass, on whom you've ridden? From your start until this day, have I
*been accustomed* to do like this to you?"

And he said, "No."

![image](images/Num_Chap22_30.jpg){height="70" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:31*](#Contents.html_rnum22_31)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^31^And YHWH uncovered Balaam's eyes, and he saw the angel of YHWH
standing up in the road, and his sword drawn in his hand. And he knelt
and bowed to his nose.

![image](images/Num_Chap22_31.jpg){height="74" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:32*](#Contents.html_rnum22_32)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^32^And the angel of YHWH said to him, "For what have you struck your
ass these three times? Here, I came out as an adversary because the way
was precipitous with regard to me.

![image](images/Num_Chap22_32.jpg){height="96" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

22:32. **the way was precipitous with regard to me**. This verse may
provide the answer to the question of why God was angry at Balaam for
going. The problem is that the meaning of the key word here (Hebrew
*yãra![image](images/tdot.jpg){height="16"
style="vertical-align:middle;" width="5"}*) is uncertain. This is its
only occurrence in the Torah. (Its only other occurrence in the *Tanak*
is in Job 16:11.) It may mean that Balaam was too quick to go with the
Moabite chiefs. Still, this remains one of the classic unsolved enigmas
of the Torah. Balaam appears to be doing just what he was told by God to
do, yet God is angry.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:33*](#Contents.html_rnum22_33)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^33^And the ass saw me and turned in front of me these three times. If
she hadn't turned from in front of me, I would have killed you, too, by
now and kept her alive!"

![image](images/Num_Chap22_33.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:34*](#Contents.html_rnum22_34)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^34^And Balaam said to the angel of YHWH, "I've sinned, because I didn't
know that you were standing up toward me in the road. And now, if it's
bad in your eyes, let me go back."

![image](images/Num_Chap22_34.jpg){height="94" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:35*](#Contents.html_rnum22_35)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^35^And the angel of YHWH said to Balaam, "Go with the people. And
nothing but the thing that I shall speak to you: that is what you shall
speak." And Balaam went with Balak's chiefs.

![image](images/Num_Chap22_35.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:36*](#Contents.html_rnum22_36)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^36^And Balak heard that Balaam was coming, and he went out to him, to a
city of Moab that was on the Arnon border, that was at the edge of the
border.

![image](images/Num_Chap22_36.jpg){height="71" width="340"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:37*](#Contents.html_rnum22_37)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^37^And Balak said to Balaam, "Didn't I *send* to you, to call you? Why
didn't you come to me? Am I indeed not able to honor you?!"

![image](images/Num_Chap22_37.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:38*](#Contents.html_rnum22_38)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^38^And Balaam said to Balak, "Here, I've come to you. Now: will I be
*able* to speak anything? The thing that God will set in my mouth: that
is what I'll speak."

![image](images/Num_Chap22_38.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:39*](#Contents.html_rnum22_39)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^39^And Balaam went with Balak, and they came to Kiriath Huzoth.

![image](images/Num_Chap22_39.jpg){height="45" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:40*](#Contents.html_rnum22_40)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^40^And Balak sacrificed oxen and sheep, and he let them go to Balaam
and to the chiefs who were with them.

![image](images/Num_Chap22_40.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

22:40. **he let them go**. He gave the sacrificed animals to Balaam and
the chiefs as a great meal prior to the intended cursing of Israel.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 22:41*](#Contents.html_rnum22_41)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^41^And it was in the morning, and Balak took Balaam and brought him up
to the []{#Numbers22.html_page_77}High Places of Baal, and he saw the
outer edge of the people from there.

![image](images/Num_Chap22_41.jpg){height="74" width="339"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

22:41. **High Places**. See the comment on Num 21:28.

[]{#Numbers23.html}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 23:1*](#Contents.html_rnum23_1)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **23** {.h2a}

^1^And Balaam said to Balak, "Build seven altars for me here, and
prepare seven bulls and seven rams for me here."

![image](images/Num_Chap23_01.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 23:2*](#Contents.html_rnum23_2)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^2^And Balak did as Balaam spoke, and Balak and Balaam offered a bull
and a ram on each altar.

![image](images/Num_Chap23_02.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 23:3*](#Contents.html_rnum23_3)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^3^And Balaam said to Balak, "Stand up by your burnt offering, and let
me go. Maybe YHWH will communicate to me. And whatever He'll show me,
I'll tell you." And he went to a viewpoint.

![image](images/Num_Chap23_03.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 23:4*](#Contents.html_rnum23_4)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^4^And God communicated to Balaam. And he said to Him, "I've arranged
the seven altars and offered a bull and a ram on each altar."

![image](images/Num_Chap23_04.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 23:5*](#Contents.html_rnum23_5)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^5^And YHWH put a word in Balaam's mouth, and He said, "Go back to
Balak, and speak like this."

![image](images/Num_Chap23_05.jpg){height="50" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 23:6*](#Contents.html_rnum23_6)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^6^And he went back to him, and here he was, standing up by his burnt
offering, he and all the chiefs of Moab.

![image](images/Num_Chap23_06.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 23:7*](#Contents.html_rnum23_7)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^7^And he took up his pronouncement, and he said:

Balak led me from Aram,

Moab's king from mountains of the East.

"Come, curse Jacob for me!

Come, denounce Israel!"

![image](images/Num_Chap23_07.jpg){height="92" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

23:7. **pronouncement**. There is no adequate way of conveying the sense
of Hebrew *m![Image](images/a-image.jpg){height="11"
width="8"}š![Image](images/a-image.jpg){height="11" width="8"}l,* a
pronouncement reflecting wisdom, inspiration, and, in some instances,
revelation, usually in poetry. It can refer to a proverb or parable, but
that does not fit here.

23:7. **Balak led me from Aram, Moab's king from mountains of the
East**. We should particularly note the beauty of the poems of the
Balaam episode. The poetry is embroidered more intimately in its prose
context than most biblical poetry that is housed in the narrative books.
Neither the narrative nor the poetry is complete without the other. The
narrative context sets the scene and creates the ironic character of the
poetry: the Moabite king has hired Balaam to curse Israel, but Balaam
blesses them instead. The poems themselves refer back to the narrative
context and are referred to in turn in the course of the narrative:
Balak hears the poems and complains about them. The Balaam episode is a
splendid example of the merging of poetry and prose in the Hebrew Bible.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 23:8*](#Contents.html_rnum23_8)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^8^How shall I execrate whom God hasn't execrated?

And how shall I denounce whom YHWH hasn't denounced?

![image](images/Num_Chap23_08.jpg){height="47" width="175"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 23:9*](#Contents.html_rnum23_9)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^9^For from the top of rocks I see it,

and from hills I view it.

Here: a people dwelling separate

and not reckoned among the nations.

![image](images/Num_Chap23_09.jpg){height="47" width="283"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

23:9. **rocks**. The word "rock" refers not to a small stone but to a
crag of the mountain. See Exodus 17 text and comments.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 23:10*](#Contents.html_rnum23_10)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^10^Who has counted the dust of Jacob

and the number of a fourth of Israel?

May my soul die the death of the righteous,

and may my future be like his!

![image](images/Num_Chap23_10.jpg){height="71" width="300"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 23:11*](#Contents.html_rnum23_11)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^11^And Balak said to Balaam, "What have you done to me?! I took you to
execrate my enemies. And, here, you've *blessed* them!"

![image](images/Num_Chap23_11.jpg){height="46" width="340"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 23:12*](#Contents.html_rnum23_12)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^12^And he answered and said, "Isn't it that what ever YHWH sets in my
mouth, *that* is what I'll watch out to say?!"

![image](images/Num_Chap23_12.jpg){height="49" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 23:13*](#Contents.html_rnum23_13)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^13^And Balak said to him, "Come on with me to another place from which
you'll see it. You'll see nothing but its edge, and you won't see all of
it. And execrate it for me from there."

![image](images/Num_Chap23_13.jpg){height="71" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 23:14*](#Contents.html_rnum23_14)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^14^And he took him to the field of Zophim, to the top of Pisgah, and he
built seven altars and offered a bull and a ram on each altar.

![image](images/Num_Chap23_14.jpg){height="70" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

23:14. **Zophim**. Meaning "lookouts"---a place with a view.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 23:15*](#Contents.html_rnum23_15)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^15^And he said to Balak, "Stand up here by your burnt offering, and
I'll be communicated with here."

![image](images/Num_Chap23_15.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 23:16*](#Contents.html_rnum23_16)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^16^And YHWH was communicated to Balaam, and He set a word in his mouth
and said, "Go back to Balak, and you []{#Numbers23.html_page_79}shall
speak this."

![image](images/Num_Chap23_16.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 23:17*](#Contents.html_rnum23_17)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^17^And he came to him, and here he was, standing up by his burnt
offering, and the chiefs of Moab were with him. And Balak said to him,
"What did YHWH speak?"

![image](images/Num_Chap23_17.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 23:18*](#Contents.html_rnum23_18)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^18^And he took up his pronouncement, and he said:

Get up, Balak, and listen.

Hear me, son of Zippor.

![image](images/Num_Chap23_18.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 23:19*](#Contents.html_rnum23_19)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^19^God is not a man, that He would lie,

or a human being, that He would regret.

Has He said and will not do

or spoken and will not bring it about?

![image](images/Num_Chap23_19.jpg){height="46" width="289"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 23:20*](#Contents.html_rnum23_20)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^20^Here, I was taken to bless,

and He blessed, and I won't take it back.

![image](images/Num_Chap23_20.jpg){height="22" width="274"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 23:21*](#Contents.html_rnum23_21)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^21^He didn't find harm in Jacob,

and He didn't see trouble in Israel.

YHWH, its God, is with it,

and a shout for a king is in it.

![image](images/Num_Chap23_21.jpg){height="72" width="239"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 23:22*](#Contents.html_rnum23_22)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^22^God who brought them out from Egypt is like a wild ox's horns for
it.

![image](images/Num_Chap23_22.jpg){height="22" width="286"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 23:23*](#Contents.html_rnum23_23)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^23^For there's no divination against Jacob

and no enchantment against Israel.

At this time it will be said to Jacob

and to Israel: "What has God done?"

![image](images/Num_Chap23_23.jpg){height="46" width="297"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 23:24*](#Contents.html_rnum23_24)

^24^Here, a people will get up like a feline

and like a lion will raise itself.

It won't lie down until it has eaten prey

and until it has drunk blood of carcasses.

![image](images/Num_Chap23_24.jpg){height="46" width="310"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 23:25*](#Contents.html_rnum23_25)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^25^And Balak said to Balaam, "Don't do either: *execrate* them or
*bless* them!"

![image](images/Num_Chap23_25.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 23:26*](#Contents.html_rnum23_26)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^26^And Balaam answered, and he said to Balak, "Didn't I speak to you,
saying, 'Everything that YHWH will speak, that is what I'll do'?"

![image](images/Num_Chap23_26.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 23:27*](#Contents.html_rnum23_27)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^27^And Balak said to Balaam, "Come on. I'll take you to another place.
Maybe it will be right in God's eyes, and you'll execrate it for me from
there."

![image](images/Num_Chap23_27.jpg){height="70" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 23:28*](#Contents.html_rnum23_28)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^28^And Balak took Balaam to the top of Peor, which looks out over
Jeshimon.

![image](images/Num_Chap23_28.jpg){height="47" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 23:29*](#Contents.html_rnum23_29)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^29^And Balaam said to Balak, "Build seven altars for me here and
prepare seven bulls and seven rams for me here."

![image](images/Num_Chap23_29.jpg){height="70" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

[]{#Numbers24.html}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 24:1*](#Contents.html_rnum24_1)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **24** {.h2a}

^1^And Balaam saw that it was good in YHWH's eyes to bless Israel, and
he did not go as in previous times to divinations. And he turned his
face to the wilderness,

![image](images/Num_Chap24_01.jpg){height="94" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 24:2*](#Contents.html_rnum24_2)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^2^and Balaam raised his eyes and saw Israel, tenting according to its
tribes, and God's spirit came on him.

![image](images/Num_Chap24_02.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 24:3*](#Contents.html_rnum24_3)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^3^And he took up his pronouncement, and he said:

Word of Balaam, son of Beor,

and word of the man, opened of eye;

![image](images/Num_Chap24_03.jpg){height="70" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 24:4*](#Contents.html_rnum24_4)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^4^word of the one who hears God's sayings,

who sees Shadday's vision,

falling, but with eyes uncovered.

![image](images/Num_Chap24_04.jpg){height="46" width="271"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 24:5*](#Contents.html_rnum24_5)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^5^How good your tents are, Jacob,

your tabernacles, Israel.

![image](images/Num_Chap24_05.jpg){height="22" width="288"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 24:6*](#Contents.html_rnum24_6)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^6^They spread like palms,

like gardens by a river,

like aloes YHWH planted,

like cedars by water.

![image](images/Num_Chap24_06.jpg){height="44" width="248"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 24:7*](#Contents.html_rnum24_7)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^7^It drips water from its branches

and its seed in plentiful water.

And its king will be higher than Agag,

and its kingdom will be elevated.

![image](images/Num_Chap24_07.jpg){height="47" width="240"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 24:8*](#Contents.html_rnum24_8)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^8^God who brought it out from Egypt

is like a wild ox's horns for it.

He shall eat up nations, its foes,

and break their bones

and pierce with His arrows.

![image](images/Num_Chap24_08.jpg){height="73" width="270"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

24:8. **pierce with His arrows**. This is a pun,
*![image](images/hdot.jpg){height="15"
width="7"}![image](images/sdot.jpg){height="11" width="6"}yw
m![image](images/hdot.jpg){height="15"
width="7"}![image](images/sdot.jpg){height="11" width="6"},* which
culminates a chain of puns on the letters of the word "Egypt," Hebrew
*mi![image](images/sdot.jpg){height="11" width="6"}rayim,* in this
verse:

![image](images/inl-page45.jpg){height="17" width="330"}

The chain of alliteration may be wordplay for its own sake, or it may be
an intentional linkage between Egypt and the words for "foes" and
breaking "bones" and "piercing with arrows"---thus conveying through
puns the defeat of Egypt.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 24:9*](#Contents.html_rnum24_9)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^9^It bent, lay down, like a lion;

and, like a feline, who will rouse it?

Those who bless you: he's blessed.

And those who curse you: he's cursed.

![image](images/Num_Chap24_09.jpg){height="47" width="252"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

24:9. **It bent ... who will rouse it**. This line of Balaam's blessing
is almost identical to a line of Jacob's deathbed blessing of his son
Judah (Gen 49:9). We may say that, as a matter of poetic technique, this
was a poetic formula that happened to be used in two different biblical
poems. This occurs elsewhere in the *Tanak* as
[]{#Numbers24.html_page_83}well, as in the case of the famous words
"They shall beat their swords into plowshares and their spears into
pruning hooks," which are said by both Isaiah and Micah (Isa 2:4; Mic
4:3). But we may also ask what it means that both Jacob and Balaam use
these same words in prophecies. It may be understood to convey the
authenticity of Balaam's prophetic experience from the God of Israel.
Israel presumably becomes aware of these prophecies and blessings that
Balaam has pronounced, because they come to be contained in the Torah.
And so this informs Israel that, even though they are spoken by a
foreigner, they are from Israel's God.

24:9. **Those who bless you: he's blessed. And those who curse you: he's
cursed**. God had already promised Abraham generations earlier that
those who bless his descendants would be blessed and that those who
curse them would be cursed. But Balaam's pronounce

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 24:10*](#Contents.html_rnum24_10)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^10^And Balak's anger flared at Balaam, and he wrung his hands, and
Balak said to Balaam, "I called you to execrate my enemies; and, here,
you've *blessed* these three times!

![image](images/Num_Chap24_10.jpg){height="70" width="341"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 24:11*](#Contents.html_rnum24_11)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^11^And now, flee to your place! I said I would *honor* you; and, here,
YHWH has held you back from honor!"

![image](images/Num_Chap24_11.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 24:12*](#Contents.html_rnum24_12)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^12^And Balaam said to Balak, "Didn't I speak to your messengers whom
you sent to me as well, saying,

![image](images/Num_Chap24_12.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 24:13*](#Contents.html_rnum24_13)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^13^'If Balak would give me a houseful of silver and gold of his I
wouldn't be able to go against the mouth of YHWH, to do good or bad from
my own heart. Whatever YHWH speaks, that is what I'll speak.'

![image](images/Num_Chap24_13.jpg){height="74" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 24:14*](#Contents.html_rnum24_14)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^14^And now, here, I'm going to my people. Come. I'll advise you of what
this people will do to your people in the future days."

![image](images/Num_Chap24_14.jpg){height="73" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 24:15*](#Contents.html_rnum24_15)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^15^And he took up his pronouncement, and he said:

Word of Balaam, son of Beor,

and word of the man whose eye is whole;

![image](images/Num_Chap24_15.jpg){height="48" width="333"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 24:16*](#Contents.html_rnum24_16)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^16^word of the one who hears God's sayings

and who knows the Highest's knowledge,

who sees Shadday's vision,

falling, but with eyes uncovered.

![image](images/Num_Chap24_16.jpg){height="47" width="269"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 24:17*](#Contents.html_rnum24_17)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^17^I see it---and not now;

I view it---and not close:

a star has stepped from Jacob,

and a scepter has come up from Israel

and pierced the temples of Moab

and the crown of all the children of Seth.

![image](images/Num_Chap24_17.jpg){height="73" width="267"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

24:17. **pierced the temples of Moab**. Moab is pictured metaphorically
as a man, who is struck at the temples, i.e., the corners of his head.

24:17. **and the crown of all the children of Seth**. In parallel with
the temples of the head comes the crown, i.e., the top of his head. Like
others, I read "crown," translating Hebrew
**![Image](images/inl-page44a.jpg){height="20" width="38"}** instead of
**![Image](images/inl-page44b.jpg){height="21" width="39"}** in the
text. The Hebrew letters *resh* and *dalet* are frequently mistaken for
each other in the old Hebrew script, and there is also support for this
reading in Jer 48:45, which pairs Moab's temple with
**![Image](images/inl-page44c.jpg){height="21" width="42"}**.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 24:18*](#Contents.html_rnum24_18)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^18^And Edom was a possession,

and Seir was a possession of its enemies,

and Israel was making triumph.

![image](images/Num_Chap24_18.jpg){height="48" width="303"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

[*Numbers 24:19*](#Contents.html_rnum24_19)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^19^And one from Jacob dominated

and destroyed a remnant from a city.

![image](images/Num_Chap24_19.jpg){height="49" width="236"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

[*Numbers 24:20*](#Contents.html_rnum24_20)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Amalek was foremost of nations,

and its future arrives at destruction.

How long will Asshur hold you prisoner?

![image](images/Num_Chap24_22.jpg){height="47" width="173"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Woe. Who will live more than what God set him?

![image](images/Num_Chap24_23.jpg){height="48" width="201"}

[]{#Numbers25.html}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 25:1*](#Contents.html_rnum25_1)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **25** {.h2a}

^1^And Israel lived in Shittim, and the people began to prostitute
themselves to the daughters of Moab:

![image](images/Num_Chap25_01.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 25:2*](#Contents.html_rnum25_2)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^2^And they attracted the people to the sacrifices of their gods, and
the people ate and bowed to their gods,

![image](images/Num_Chap25_02.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

25:2. **sacrifices of their gods**. Hebrew
![image](images/f25-2-1.jpg){height="19" width="89"}. Ps 106:28 refers
to these sacrifices at Baal Peor as
![image](images/f25-2-2.jpg){height="16" width="73"}, meaning
"sacrifices of the dead" instead of "sacrifices of their gods." This
episode may therefore refer to ancestor veneration: offering sacrifices
to the spirits of the dead. Forms of ancestor veneration are known in
ancient Israel, involving the provision of food and fluid for ancestors'
tombs. It reflected belief in an afterlife. It was practiced until the
time of King Hezekiah, at which time the devastation of the
[]{#Numbers25.html_page_86}countryside by the Assyrian emperor
Sennacherib cut the people off from the family burial sites, and the
centralization of religion at the Temple prohibited sacrifices anywhere
but at the Temple. (See the comment on Lev 19:31.)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 25:3*](#Contents.html_rnum25_3)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^3^and Israel was associated with Baal Peor. And YHWH's anger flared at
Israel.

![image](images/Num_Chap25_03.jpg){height="46" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 25:4*](#Contents.html_rnum25_4)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^4^And YHWH said to Moses, "Take all of the leaders of the people and
hang them in front of the sun, and YHWH's flaring anger will go back
from Israel."

![image](images/Num_Chap25_04.jpg){height="98" width="340"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

25:4. **in front of the sun**. This expression apparently means
"publicly" (and possibly with a negative connotation). It occurs in one
other place in the *Tanak* (2 Sam 12:12), in the curse on David's house
over his adultery with Bathsheba: he took another man's wife in secret,
but a man will take *David's* wives "in front of the sun."

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 25:5*](#Contents.html_rnum25_5)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^5^And Moses said to Israel's judges, "Each of you, kill those of his
people who are associated with Baal Peor."

![image](images/Num_Chap25_05.jpg){height="47" width="340"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 25:6*](#Contents.html_rnum25_6)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^6^And, here, a man from the children of Israel came and brought forward
a Midianite woman to his brothers before the eyes of Moses and before
the eyes of all the congregation of the children of Israel while they
were mourning at the entrance of the Tent of Meeting.

![image](images/Num_Chap25_06.jpg){height="99" width="340"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

25:6. **they were mourning**. The last reference to mourning was that
"all the house of Israel mourned Aaron thirty days" (20:29). Thus this
episode occurs during the thirty days that they were mourning Aaron.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 25:7*](#Contents.html_rnum25_7)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^7^And Phinehas son of Eleazar son of Aaron, the priest, saw, and he got
up from among the congregation and took a spear in his hand.

![image](images/Num_Chap25_07.jpg){height="70" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 25:8*](#Contents.html_rnum25_8)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^8^And he came after the Israelite man to the enclosure and ran the two
of them through, the Israelite man and the woman, to her stomach, and
the plague was halted from the children of Israel.

![image](images/Num_Chap25_08.jpg){height="75" width="342"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

25:8. **enclosure**. Hebrew
*qubb![Image](images/a-image.jpg){height="11" width="8"}h.* The *qubbãh*
is a tent, as Rashi understood. Moreover, as my teacher Frank Cross
discussed, the *qubb![Image](images/a-image.jpg){height="11"
width="8"}h* is known in the pre-Islamic Near East. It was a tent used
to house sacred objects, and, like the biblical Tabernacle, it was made
of red leather. Since this episode begins precisely at the entrance to
the Tabernacle (the Tent of Meeting; v. 6), it certainly appears that
the *qubb![Image](images/a-image.jpg){height="11" width="8"}h* here
refers to the inside of the Tabernacle. This is confirmed by what
happens next. See the next comment.

25:8. **he ran them through**. Commentators have been troubled by
Phinehas's zeal in killing these two people. But the point is that it is
a *ritual* crime. The crime is not that an Israelite and a Midianite
have sexual relations. It is that they have violated the Tabernacle. As
non-Levites, they are not even permitted to enter it, let alone to have
sexual relations---whether it is for procreation, pleasure, or a
fertility rite. Note that there is no trial. If it were solely an
ethical offense, there would be a trial and an inquiry into their
motives. But, for a ritual violation of the holy place, there is no
trial because there is no possible defense, no satisfactory motive or
explanation. Phinehas, as a priest, can enter, and he executes them---as
the law requires (Num 1:51; 3:10,38; 18:4,7). Thus the Torah goes on to
tell us that (1) this stops a plague and (2) God is pleased with what
Phinehas has done and gives him a reward for it: a covenant of eternal
priesthood. These harsh consequences for ritual offenses---here and in
the case of Nadab and Abihu and elsewhere---are extremely difficult to
comprehend in the present age, in which most people (I think), including
me, respond with shock to their severity.

25:8. **her stomach**. Hebrew
**![Image](images/inl-page41a.jpg){height="33" width="38"}**
(*q![Image](images/a-image.jpg){height="11"
width="8"}b![Image](images/a-image.jpg){height="11"
width="8"}t![Image](images/a-image.jpg){height="11" width="8"}h*), a pun
on the word for the enclosure, Hebrew
**![Image](images/inl-page41b.jpg){height="30" width="31"}**
(*qubb![Image](images/a-image.jpg){height="11" width="8"}h*). The pun
hints that it is an ironic punishment to suit the crime. The word may
mean her genital area rather than her stomach. Either way, though, it is
a most extraordinary sexual image, as the spear strikes the man and then
protrudes from him into her belly or genital area, bizarrely paralleling
their sexual intercourse.

25:8. **the plague**. What plague?! No plague has been mentioned until
now. Some would say that this sudden mention of a plague is the result
of the combining of two originally separate sources to make this
chapter, but I do not think that this point is a source issue. The
plague is a direct, unannounced consequence of the violation of the holy
place, just as it was in the aftermath of the Korah rebellion (Num
17:11--14). This is precisely what is warned in Num 8:19, "so there will
not be a plague ... when the children of Israel would come near to the
Holy." This further confirms that the crime here is the ritual offense
of entering the Holy.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 25:9*](#Contents.html_rnum25_9)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^9^And the dead in the plague were twenty-four thousand.

![image](images/Num_Chap25_09.jpg){height="47" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## P[HINEHAS]{.smallcaps1} {.h201}

^10^And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying,

![image](images/Num_Chap25_10.jpg){height="22" width="233"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 25:11*](#Contents.html_rnum25_11)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^11^"Phinehas son of Eleazar son of Aaron, the priest, has turned back
my fury from the children of Israel by his carrying out my jealousy
among them, so I didn't finish the children of Israel in my jealousy.

![image](images/Num_Chap25_11.jpg){height="97" width="343"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 25:12*](#Contents.html_rnum25_12)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^12^Therefore, say: Here, I'm giving him my covenant of peace,

![image](images/Num_Chap25_12.jpg){height="46" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 25:13*](#Contents.html_rnum25_13)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

priesthood, because he was jealous for his God, and he made atonement
for the children of Israel."

![image](images/Num_Chap25_13.jpg){height="71" width="338"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 25:14*](#Contents.html_rnum25_14)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Midianite woman, was Zimri son of Salu, chieftain of a father's house of
the Simeonites.

![image](images/Num_Chap25_14.jpg){height="70" width="337"}

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[*Numbers 25:15*](#Contents.html_rnum25_15)

------------------------------------------------------------------------
