^20^to love YHWH, your God, to listen to His voice and to cling to Him,
because He is your life and the extension of your days to reside on the
land that YHWH swore to your fathers, to Abraham, to Isaac, and to
Jacob, to give to them."

![Image](images/Deu_Chap30_20.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

30:20. **to cling to Him**. "Cling" is the same term that is used for
man's attachment to woman at the beginning of the Torah (Gen 2:24). This
is part of a string of terms in this passage (vv. 15--20) that call to
mind the opening chapters of the Torah: life, death, good and bad, the
skies and earth, the ground, the day (cf. "in the day" and "in the day
you eat from it," Gen 2:4,17), "your seed," be fruitful, to give, to
tell, to command (cf. Gen 3:11), to watch (or observe, Hebrew
*![image](images/s-image.jpg)mr*), and to listen to the voice (of God;
cf. "You listened to your woman's voice," Gen 3:17). And these are
preceded by references to fruit (Deut 30:9), and to the skies and the
sea (30:12--13). This cluster of language here in the last sentences of
Moses' address forms a great connection back to the start of Genesis and
a reminder of the unity of the Torah.

30:20. **He is your life**. This can also be understood to mean "*It* is
your life." Translators are split on this. The former means that Moses
is saying that *God* is the people's life and the source of their being
in their land a long time. The latter means that the people's choice to
love, listen, and cling to God is their life and the source of lengthy
time in the land. That is, the question is whether it is God or it is
people's feeling about God that brings this about. I do not know which
is correct.

[]{#Deuteronomy31.html}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 31:1](#Contents.html_rDeu31_1)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

**A[ND HE WENT]{.small}**

**31** ^1^And Moses went and spoke these things to all Israel.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap31.jpg)

![Image](images/Deu_Chap31_01.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

31:1. **And Moses went**. Went where? The Septuagint and Qumran texts
have "And Moses finished speaking all these things"---reading Hebrew
**![image](images/Chap31_01a.jpg)** (finished) rather than
**![image](images/Chap31_01b.jpg)** (went), reversing the last two
letters. This makes better sense. It also adds a wordplay on his
finishing (**![image](images/Chap31_01c.jpg)**) in v. 1 and his saying
"I'm not able (**![image](images/Chap31_01d.jpg)**) to go out and come
in anymore" in v. 2.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 31:2](#Contents.html_rDeu31_2)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^2^And he said to them, "I'm a hundred twenty years old today. I'm not
able to go out and come in anymore. And YHWH said to me, 'You shall not
cross this Jordan.'

![Image](images/Deu_Chap31_02.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 31:3](#Contents.html_rDeu31_3)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^3^YHWH, your God: *He* is crossing in front of you. He'll destroy these
nations in front of you, and you'll dispossess them. Joshua: *he* is
crossing in front of you, as YHWH has spoken.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap31_03.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 31:4](#Contents.html_rDeu31_4)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^4^And YHWH will do to them as He did to Sihon and to Og, the kings of
the Amorites, and to their land, that He destroyed them.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap31_04.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 31:5](#Contents.html_rDeu31_5)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^5^And YHWH will put them in front of you, and you shall do to them
according to all of the commandment that I've commanded you.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap31_05.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 31:6](#Contents.html_rDeu31_6)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^6^Be strong and be bold. Don't be afraid and don't be scared in front
of them, because YHWH, your God: He is the one going with you. He won't
let you down and won't leave you."

![Image](images/Deu_Chap31_06.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 31:7](#Contents.html_rDeu31_7)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^7^And Moses called Joshua and said to him before the eyes of all
Israel, "Be strong and be bold, because *you* will come with this people
to the land that YHWH swore to their fathers to give to them, and *you*
will get it for them as a legacy.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap31_07.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

31:7. **Be strong and be bold ...** ***you*** **will get it for them as
a legacy**. This, finally, is the culmination of what Moses told the
people in chapter 3. He had told them that God was angry at him, that he
was not permitted to enter the land, and that God told him to "command
Joshua and strengthen him and make him bold, because ... he will get
them the land that you'll see as a legacy" (3:28). Moses has now
instructed the people in the laws and exhorted them to be faithful to
the covenant, and so he turns to the task that he was assigned: he
passes the leadership to Joshua. Note that it is not just that Moses
dies and Joshua replaces him. Rather, during his lifetime, publicly,
Moses himself identifies Joshua as his successor, charges him with his
task, and *encourages* him to be successful. Although one can easily
imagine Moses envying Joshua, who is replacing him for the fulfillment
of the promises, Moses acts in Joshua's favor: preparing him and
enhancing his stature in the people's eyes. One who begrudges his
successor does not understand time and history.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 31:8](#Contents.html_rDeu31_8)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^8^And YHWH: He is the one who is going in front of you. He will be with
you. He won't let you down and won't leave you. You shall not fear, and
you shall not be dismayed."

![Image](images/Deu_Chap31_08.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 31:9](#Contents.html_rDeu31_9)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^9^And Moses wrote this instruction and gave it to the priests, sons of
Levi, who were carrying the ark of YHWH's covenant and to all of
Israel's elders.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap31_09.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

31:9. **And Moses wrote this instruction** (and 31:24. "And it was when
Moses finished writing the words of this instruction on a scroll"). This
"instruction" (Hebrew *tôr![image](images/a-image.jpg)h*) does not refer
to the entire five books, which came to be known as the Torah long after
this chapter was written. Those who began claiming that Moses was the
author of the entire Torah created much confusion and misinformation by
claiming more for Moses than the Torah itself ascribed to him. It became
a firmly established doctrine by the rabbinic period, and many great
teachers, including Rashi and Ramban, followed it. Ibn Ezra raised
subtle doubts about it, Spinoza openly challenged it, and Jewish, as
well as Christian, scholars came to reject it in the nineteenth and
twentieth centuries. Orthodox Jews still accept it.

The instruction that Moses writes here appears to be most of the law
code that begins in Deuteronomy 12 and ends at 26:15. It may also be
understood to include the list of blessings and curses in Deuteronomy
28. That concluding list of curses especially would account for the
strong reaction that is ascribed to King Josiah when he hears the words
of the scroll of the Torah centuries later (see the comment on 31:26).

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 31:10](#Contents.html_rDeu31_10)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^10^And Moses commanded them, saying, "At the end of seven years, at the
appointed time of the year of the remission, on the Festival of Booths,

![Image](images/Deu_Chap31_10.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

31:10. **seven years**. The biblical requirement was to read "this
torah" (the law code of Deuteronomy) once every seven years, on the
holiday of Sukkot. Later the Jewish practice became to read the entire
Torah (all of the Five Books of Moses), once every year or every three
years, and in weekly portions rather than all at once.

[]{#Deuteronomy31.html_page_101}31:10. **the remission**. See Deut
15:1ff.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 31:11](#Contents.html_rDeu31_11)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^11^when all Israel comes to appear before YHWH, your God, in the place
that He will choose, you shall read this instruction in front of all
Israel in their ears.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap31_11.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 31:12](#Contents.html_rDeu31_12)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^12^Assemble the people---the men and the women and the infants and your
alien who is in your gates---so they will listen and so they will learn
and will fear YHWH, your God, and they will be watchful to do all the
words of this instruction.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap31_12.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

31:12. **your alien**. Five times in the Torah, laws about aliens in
Israel say "*your* alien" (compare "*your* poor" in the Covenant Code;
Exod 23:6)---including in the Decalogue in both Exodus and Deuteronomy.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 31:13](#Contents.html_rDeu31_13)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^13^And their children who have not known will listen and learn to fear
YHWH, your God, all the days that you're living on the land to which
you're crossing the Jordan to take possession of it."

![Image](images/Deu_Chap31_13.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 31:14](#Contents.html_rDeu31_14)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^14^And YHWH said to Moses, "Here, your days to die have come close.
Call Joshua, and stand up in the Tent of Meeting, and I'll command him."
And Moses and Joshua went and stood up in the Tent of Meeting.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap31_14.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 31:15](#Contents.html_rDeu31_15)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^15^And YHWH appeared in the tent in a column of cloud, and the column
of cloud stood at the entrance of the tent.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap31_15.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 31:16](#Contents.html_rDeu31_16)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^16^And YHWH said to Moses, "Here, when you're lying with your fathers,
this people will get up and whore after foreign gods of the land into
which it is coming, and it will leave me and break my covenant that I've
made with it.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap31_16.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

31:16. **it**. God repeatedly speaks of the people in the singular in
this passage---and quotes the people as speaking of itself in the
singular as well: "not present in *me* ... these evils have found *me.*"
The hiding of the face of God, which is predicted here, is understood to
be the fate of the entire people, not of individuals. Divine hiddenness
is not an individual's unique experience, and it is not based on an
individual's behavior. It is the experience of a *community* and
possibly of an *era.* At the time that I am writing this, it is such an
era.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 31:17](#Contents.html_rDeu31_17)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^17^And my anger will flare at it on that day, and I'll leave them, and
I'll hide my face from them, and it will become prey, and many bad
things and troubles will find it. And it will say on that day, 'Isn't it
because my God is not present in me that these evils have found me?'

![Image](images/Deu_Chap31_17.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

31:17. **I'll hide my face**. These words come twice in the passage that
contains the last words that God speaks to Moses before summoning him to
die. They occur in the song that God tells Moses to teach the people as
a witness for the future as well (32:20), and this expression occurs
nearly thirty times more in the *Tanak* after that. These words here
predict a time in which God will be hidden. It is more frightening than
divine punishment: It is one thing for a parent to punish a child; it is
far worse if the parent becomes hidden from
[]{#Deuteronomy31.html_page_102}the child, so the child does not know if
the parent is present or not. The child feels vulnerable, unprotected.
The child cries for the parent, but there is no answer. It is
terrifying.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 31:18](#Contents.html_rDeu31_18)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^18^But I: I'll *hide my face* on that day over all the bad that it has
done, because it turned to other gods.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap31_18.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

31:18. **it**. God repeatedly speaks of the people in the singular in
this passage---and quotes the people as speaking of itself in the
singular as well: "not present in *me* ... these evils have found *me.*"
The hiding of the face of God, which is predicted here, is understood to
be the fate of the entire people, not of individuals. Divine hiddenness
is not an individual's unique experience, and it is not based on an
individual's behavior. It is the experience of a *community* and
possibly of an *era.* At the time that I am writing this, it is such an
era.

31:18. **I'll hide my face**. These words come twice in the passage that
contains the last words that God speaks to Moses before summoning him to
die. They occur in the song that God tells Moses to teach the people as
a witness for the future as well (32:20), and this expression occurs
nearly thirty times more in the *Tanak* after that. These words here
predict a time in which God will be hidden. It is more frightening than
divine punishment: It is one thing for a parent to punish a child; it is
far worse if the parent becomes hidden from the child, so the child does
not know if the parent is present or not. The child feels vulnerable,
unprotected. The child cries for the parent, but there is no answer. It
is terrifying.

The expression *hest![image](images/e1.jpg)r
p![image](images/a-image.jpg)nîm,* the hiding of the face, became a
known idiom in rabbinic literature, and in theological thinking to this
day. I have written about how it develops through the Bible (in *The
Hidden Face of God*). The *Tanak*'s story moves from a time of
extraordinary divine involvement---creation, flood, personal contact,
splitting sea, hearing God's voice at Sinai---to a time in which humans
are left on their own. God is not pictured as speaking or appearing in
the books of Ezra and Nehemiah and is never mentioned in Esther. The
prediction at the end of the Torah comes true at the end of the *Tanak.*
Humans are left not knowing if God exists or not. Thus the *Tanak* tells
a story of a development from a world of direct communication with God
to the world we have known ever since: in which the existence of God is
a matter of faith, or doubt, or search.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 31:19](#Contents.html_rDeu31_19)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^19^So now write this song and teach it to the children of Israel. Set
it in their mouths, so this song will become a witness for me among the
children of Israel.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap31_19.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 31:20](#Contents.html_rDeu31_20)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^20^When I'll bring it to the land that I swore to its fathers, flowing
with milk and honey, and it will eat and be full and get fat and turn to
other gods, and they will serve them and reject me, and it will break my
covenant,

![Image](images/Deu_Chap31_20.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 31:21](#Contents.html_rDeu31_21)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^21^then it will be, when many bad things and troubles will find it,
that this song will testify as a witness in front of it, because it
won't be forgotten from its seed's mouth. Because I know its inclination
that it is doing today even before I bring it to the land that I swore."

![Image](images/Deu_Chap31_21.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

31:21. **I know its inclination**. Again an essential point from the
Torah's beginning returns at its conclusion. The flood story starts and
ends with God's recognition that humans' *inclination* is toward doing
bad: "I won't curse the ground on account of humankind again, for the
inclination of the human heart is bad from their youth" (Gen 6:5; 8:21).
The word "inclination"
(*y![image](images/e1.jpg)![image](images/sdot.jpg)er*) then does not
occur again in the Torah until here. God states that He knows, even
before the people enter the land, that this will be a problem, and so He
has them learn a song that refers to their rebellion and to the hiding
of the face of God so that they will know that they were warned.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 31:22](#Contents.html_rDeu31_22)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^22^And Moses wrote this song on that day, and he taught it to the
children of Israel.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap31_22.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 31:23](#Contents.html_rDeu31_23)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^23^And He commanded Joshua, son of Nun, and said, "Be strong and bold,
because you will bring the children of Israel to the land that I swore
to them, and I shall be with you."

![Image](images/Deu_Chap31_23.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 31:24](#Contents.html_rDeu31_24)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^24^And it was when Moses finished writing the words of this instruction
on a scroll to their end,

![Image](images/Deu_Chap31_24.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 31:25](#Contents.html_rDeu31_25)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^25^and Moses commanded the Levites, who carried the ark of the covenant
of YHWH, saying,

![Image](images/Deu_Chap31_25.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 31:26](#Contents.html_rDeu31_26)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^26^"Take this scroll of instruction and set it at the side of the ark
of the covenant of YHWH, your God, and it will be there for you as a
witness.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap31_26.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

[]{#Deuteronomy31.html_page_103}31:26. **it will be there for you as a
witness**. The scroll that Moses has written stays there beside the ark
for six hundred years until the reign of King Josiah. Apparently it has
long been ignored by then. The priest Hilkiah says there, "I've found
the scroll of instruction (*s![image](images/e1.jpg)per
hatt![image](images/o.jpg)r![image](images/a-image.jpg)h*) in the house
of YHWH." When it is read to King Josiah, he tears his clothes and says,
"YHWH's fury is great that has ignited at us because our fathers didn't
listen to this scroll's words, to act according to everything that's
written about us!" (2 Kings 22:8--13). Especially in light of the curses
in Deuteronomy 28, Josiah's reaction is understandable. And so it is
understood that these laws go ignored for generations and then are
rediscovered. Josiah has the scroll read aloud to the entire people, and
he institutes a renewal of the covenant and a tremendous religious
reform. The narrative concludes: "And before him there was not a king
like him, who came back to YHWH with all his heart and with all his soul
and with all his might, according to all of Moses' instruction, and
after him none rose like him" (2 Kings 23:25). All the kings in the
books of 1 and 2 Kings are measured by whether they fulfilled the laws
of Deuteronomy, and Josiah is rated the highest. Above all, Josiah
institutes the practice of public reading of the Torah, which prevails
to the present day. And, to this day, the measure of a leader among the
Jews must be the degree to which he or she lives by the Torah and draws
others to do so as well.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 31:27](#Contents.html_rDeu31_27)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^27^Because I know your rebellion and your hard neck. Here, while I'm
still alive with you today, you've been rebelling at YHWH, so how much
more after my death!

![Image](images/Deu_Chap31_27.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 31:28](#Contents.html_rDeu31_28)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^28^Assemble all the elders of your tribes and your officers to me so I
may speak these things in their ears and call the skies and the earth to
witness regarding them,

![Image](images/Deu_Chap31_28.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

31:28. **call the skies and the earth to witness**. Moses will do this
in the opening words of the song (32:1): "Listen, skies, so I may speak;
and let the earth hear what my mouth says." Ancient Near Eastern
treaties include a summoning of witnesses, usually the gods of the
parties to the agreement. The biblical covenant cannot include pagan
gods as witnesses, but it must have eternal witnesses since the covenant
is meant to last forever. Therefore, the earth and sky are called on as
witnesses. This summoning of earth and sky rather than gods in an early
poem is further evidence that monotheism existed early in Israelite
history.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 31:29](#Contents.html_rDeu31_29)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^29^because I know, after my death, that you'll be *corrupted,* and
you'll turn from the way that I've commanded you. And the bad thing will
happen to you in the future days when you'll do what is bad in YHWH's
eyes to provoke Him with your hands' work."

![Image](images/Deu_Chap31_29.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

31:29. **you'll be** ***corrupted****.* Another term from the Torah's
beginning returns at its end: the *Hiphil* of the root
*![image](images/s-image.jpg)![image](images/hdot.jpg)t* with the sense
of humans becoming corrupt was used to introduce the flood: "All flesh
had corrupted its way on the earth" (Gen 6:12). It does not occur again
with this meaning until Deut 4:16 and then again here, where Moses says
he *knows* that the people will someday become corrupted. That is, he
understands that the susceptibility to corruption has been part of human
nature from the beginning.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 31:30](#Contents.html_rDeu31_30)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^30^And Moses spoke in the ears of all the community of Israel the words
of this song to their end:

![Image](images/Deu_Chap31_30.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

[]{#Deuteronomy32.html}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:1](#Contents.html_rDeu32_1)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

**L[ISTEN]{.small}**

**32** ^1^Listen, skies, so I may speak and let the earth hear what my
mouth says.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32.jpg)

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_01.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:2](#Contents.html_rDeu32_2)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^2^Let my teaching come down like showers; let my saying emerge like
dew, like raindrops on plants and like rainfalls on herbs.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_02.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

32:2. **plants**. This word, too, occurs among the first words of the
Torah and does not occur again until its appearance here. In Genesis,
God causes the earth to produce plants by His word (Gen 1:11--12). Now
Moses turns this image and sings of his words dropping like rain on the
plants.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:3](#Contents.html_rDeu32_3)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^3^When I call YHWH's name, avow our God's greatness.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_03.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:4](#Contents.html_rDeu32_4)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^4^The Rock: His work is unblemished, for all His ways are judgment. A
God of trust, and without injustice, He's virtuous and right.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_04.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

[]{#Deuteronomy32.html_page_105}32:4. **The Rock**. The first letter of
the word is oversized in the Torah, possibly to distinguish it from
other uses in the song. The song plays on this word, using it seven more
times with several different meanings.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:5](#Contents.html_rDeu32_5)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^5^It corrupted at Him---not His children, their flaw--- a crooked and
twisted generation.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_05.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

32:5. **It corrupted at Him---not His children, their flaw**. The noun,
Hebrew *mûm,* means an injury of the sort that makes a man unacceptable
for the priesthood, or an animal unacceptable for sacrifice. Here I
understand it to be some such damage in humans' nature, as God and Moses
have just said (31:21,27,29). It is not humans, "His children," as such,
but rather this inherent shortcoming in their nature that brings about
corruption in the eyes of the deity.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:6](#Contents.html_rDeu32_6)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^6^*Is it to YHWH* that you repay like this?! Foolish people and unwise!
[]{#Deuteronomy32.html_page_97}Isn't He your father, who created you, He
who made you and reared you?

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_06.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

32:6. ***Is it to YHWH*** **... ?!** The first letter (the Hebrew
interrogative h, which makes the line a question) is oversized in many
manuscripts. This seems to underscore the question, to make it
incredible: "You'd repay *God* this way?!"

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:7](#Contents.html_rDeu32_7)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^7^Remember the days of old. Grasp the years through generations. Ask
your father, and he'll tell you, your elders, and they'll say to you:

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_07.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:8](#Contents.html_rDeu32_8)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^8^When the Highest gave nations legacies, when He dispersed humankind,
He set the peoples' borders to the number of the children of Israel.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_08.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

32:8. **dispersed humankind**. The term "dispersed" refers to the
separation of humankind into nations in Genesis (10:5,18,32; 25:23).

32:8. **to the number of the children of Israel**. In light of the other
occurrences of the phrase "to the number" in the *Tanak* (Josh 4:5,8;
Judg 21:23), it means here "equal to the number of the children of
Israel." This makes little sense: that the number of nations is equal to
the number of the children of Israel. Traditional commentators have
taken this to mean that there are seventy nations (as in Genesis 10),
matching the seventy souls of Jacob's (Israel's) family who go to Egypt
(Exod 1:5), or that there are twelve Canaanite peoples, matching
Israel's twelve sons.

Contemporary scholars have taken account of a Qumran (Dead Sea) scroll
(4QDeut^j^) that says instead: "to the number of the children of the
gods" (or "children of God"---*b![image](images/e-image.jpg)nê
'![image](images/e-image.jpg)l![image](images/o.jpg)hîm*). The phrase
"children \[or: sons\] of the gods" normally means: the gods. Scholars
have hypothesized that this was the original reading of this verse and
that later scribes or editors were distressed by this meaning so they
changed "children of the gods" to "children of Israel." If so, then the
original meaning was that God allotted a nation to each of the
gods---and He took Israel as His own people. Thus the next verse reads:
"YHWH's portion is Israel." One may take this to mean that the author of
this song believed that there were in fact other gods but that YHWH was
supreme. (This is known as henotheism, a step between polytheism and
monotheism.) Or one may understand it to mean that the author's view was
that YHWH allotted the peoples their gods in whom to believe, but that
these gods did not really exist.

To complicate this picture further, the Greek text reads "to the number
of the angels of God." And there are cases in which the
*b![image](images/e-image.jpg)nê
'![image](images/e-image.jpg)l![image](images/o.jpg)hîm* are thought to
be the angels (Gen 6:2; Job 1:6). It is likely that the Greek translator
had the "children of the gods" reading and changed it to "angels of God"
to establish that these were in fact the angels and not the pagan gods.
On this understanding of the *b![image](images/e-image.jpg)nê
'![image](images/e-image.jpg)l![image](images/o.jpg)hîm,* God allots a
nation to each of the angels but retains Israel for Himself.

I believe that Psalm 82 is particularly relevant to this matter. It
pictures the following: "God is standing in the divine council. He
judges among the gods" (82:1). In this extraordinary scene, God
criticizes the gods for failing to judge justly, and He takes away their
immortality, condemning them to die like humans: "I had said you are
gods, children of the Highest, all of you; but you shall die like a
human!" (82:6). Whether meant literally or figuratively, this psalm
tells a myth of the death of the gods. And note that it refers to God as
"the Highest" (*'ely![image](images/o.jpg)n*), as in our verse here in
the Song of Moses (and it refers to the gods as the children of the
Highest); and it ends with God giving *legacies* to the nations
(82:8)---which is also what our verse here says: "When the Highest gave
nations legacies\...." It is likely, therefore, that the passage in the
Song of Moses reflects the idea of Psalm 82: that there once were lesser
gods along with YHWH, and that each was the god of a people, but they
were inadequate, and they no longer exist. This may have been believed
literally, or it may have been conceived as an answer to those who
asked, "What happened to the gods we used to worship?" Or it
[]{#Deuteronomy32.html_page_107}may have been conceived as a metaphor
for the replacement of the belief in many gods by the belief in one.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:9](#Contents.html_rDeu32_9)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^9^For YHWH's portion is His people. Jacob is the share of His legacy.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_09.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:10](#Contents.html_rDeu32_10)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^10^He found it in a wilderness land and in a formless place, a howling
desert. He surrounded it. He attended to it.
[]{#Deuteronomy32.html_page_98}He guarded it, like the pupil of his eye.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_10.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

32:10. **a formless place**. Hebrew *tôhû.* The word has not occurred
since its mention at the very beginning of the Torah: "the earth had
been formless and shapeless" (Gen 1:2). Now Israel's environment in the
wilderness is pictured comparably to the condition of the universe prior
to the acts of creation: a condition of chaos.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:11](#Contents.html_rDeu32_11)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^11^As an eagle stirs its nest, hovers over its young, spreads its
wings, takes it, lifts it on its pinion,

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_11.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

32:11. **hovers**. This word, too, occurred in Gen 1:2 (see the
preceding comment) and never again in the Torah until its appearance
here. YHWH's protection of Israel in its infancy in the wilderness (like
an eagle hovering over its young) is pictured comparably to the divine
spirit hovering over the still-shapeless waters in which the earth and
skies will be formed.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:12](#Contents.html_rDeu32_12)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^12^YHWH, alone, led it, and no foreign god with Him.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_12.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:13](#Contents.html_rDeu32_13)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^13^He had it ride over earth's high places and fed it the field's
bounties and had it suck honey from a rock and oil from a flint rock

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_13.jpg)

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_13a.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:14](#Contents.html_rDeu32_14)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^14^and curds of cattle and milk of the flock and fat of lambs and
Bashan rams and he-goats with fat of innards of wheat
[]{#Deuteronomy32.html_page_99}---and from grape's blood you drank wine.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_14.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:15](#Contents.html_rDeu32_15)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^15^And Jeshurun got fat and kicked ---you got fat, you got wide, you
got stuffed!--- and it left God who made it and took its saving rock for
granted.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_15.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

32:15. **Jeshurun**. Israel.

32:15. **got fat**. This is a pun. The Hebrew consonants
*y![image](images/s-image.jpg)mn* are the same as in the word "desert"
(*y![image](images/e-image.jpg)![image](images/s-image.jpg)im![image](images/o.jpg)n*)
in v. 10. But the wordplay is that the same words consonantally have
nearly opposite meanings: the people ironically go from a desert
(*y![image](images/s-image.jpg)mn*) to getting fat
(*y![image](images/s-image.jpg)mn*).

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:16](#Contents.html_rDeu32_16)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^16^They made Him jealous with outsiders. With offensive things they
made him angry.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_16.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:17](#Contents.html_rDeu32_17)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^17^They sacrificed to demons, a non-god, gods they hadn't known; new
ones, they came of late; []{#Deuteronomy32.html_page_100}your fathers
hadn't been acquainted with them.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_17.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:18](#Contents.html_rDeu32_18)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^18^The rock that fathered you, you ignored, and you forgot God who bore
you.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_18.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:19](#Contents.html_rDeu32_19)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^19^And YHWH saw and rejected from His sons' and His daughters'
angering.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_19.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:20](#Contents.html_rDeu32_20)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^20^And He said, "Let me hide my face from them; I'll see what their
future will be. For they're a generation of overthrows, children with no
trust in them.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_20.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

32:20. **Let me hide my face**. See the comment on Deut 31:17.

32:20. **I'll see what their future will be**. Hebrew
*'a![image](images/hdot.jpg)![image](images/a1.jpg)rît,* often
understood as "end," does not mean this in the sense of humankind's
coming to an end. Rather, it refers to what will happen in the distant
future, the long run. There are two sides to the hiding of the face. It
is a fearful period of estrangement from their creator. It has been
called divine eclipse, *Deus* *Absconditus,* and "death" of God. But,
after living through it, humans are forced to grow up, to become more
responsible for their world. The words of the Song of Moses declare that
God is not simply hiding His face to bring His relationship with humans
to an end. There are rather two halves to the statement: "I'll hide my
face from them; I'll see what their future will be." God gives humans
responsibility for their world. And the only way that they can be forced
to take that responsibility is if God is hidden. At whatever price (even
world wars, even the holocaust?!) and with whatever successes
(rebuilding Israel, conquering diseases, discovering secrets of the
creation of the universe), humans must grow up. And God will see what
their destiny will be.

The Torah does not end with the natural conclusions of the story: the
promised land has not been reached, but it is in sight. Everything lies
in the future: finding a home, fulfillment of the promises, bringing
blessing to all the families of the earth. The divine words resound:
"I'll see what their future will be." The Torah ends leaving us looking
forward to what we can do and what we can be.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:21](#Contents.html_rDeu32_21)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^21^They made me jealous with no-god. They made me angry with their
nothings. And I: I'll make them jealous with no-people. I'll make them
angry with a foolish nation.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_21.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:22](#Contents.html_rDeu32_22)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^22^For fire has ignited in my anger and burned to Sheol at bottom and
consumed land and its crop and set the mountains' foundations ablaze.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_22.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:23](#Contents.html_rDeu32_23)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^23^I'll mass bad things over them. I'll exhaust my arrows on them,

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_23.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:24](#Contents.html_rDeu32_24)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^24^sapped by hunger and devoured by flame. And bitter destruction and
animals' teeth I'll let loose at them with venom of serpents of the
dust.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_24.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

32:24. **serpents of the dust**. Or: things that crawl in the dust. This
is reminiscent of the story of Eden, in which the snake and its
descendants are condemned to be legless, going on their bellies and
eating dust (Gen 3:14); and in which the descendants of the humans and
the snake are cursed with mutual enmity, so that the snake will bite the
human's heel (3:15).

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:25](#Contents.html_rDeu32_25)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^25^Outside: a sword will bereave, and inside: terror, both young man
and virgin, suckling with aged man.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_25.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:26](#Contents.html_rDeu32_26)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^26^I'd say, 'I'll erase them. I'll make their memory cease from
mankind,'

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_26.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

32:26. **erase them ... make their memory cease**. To capture the full
irony and horror of these words, one must recall that this is what is
supposed to happen to Amalek, the people who symbolize Israel's worst
enemies: "wipe out the memory of Amalek" (Deut 25:19).

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:27](#Contents.html_rDeu32_27)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^27^if I didn't fear the enemy's anger, in case their foes would
misread, in case they'd say, '*Our* hand was high, and it wasn't YHWH
who did all this!'

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_27.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:28](#Contents.html_rDeu32_28)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^28^For they're a nation void of counsel, and there's no understanding
in them.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_28.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:29](#Contents.html_rDeu32_29)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^29^If they were wise they'd comprehend this; they'd grasp their future.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_29.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:30](#Contents.html_rDeu32_30)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^30^How could one pursue a thousand and two chase ten thousand if not
that their rock had sold them, that YHWH had turned them over?"

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_30.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

32:30. **turned them over**. This is the same word that is used to refer
to turning an escaped slave over to his master (Deut 23:16). The
preceding colon, which parallels this, refers to *selling* the people.
That is, both lines convey an image of God selling Israel back into
slavery, when it was God who had freed them from slavery in the first
place. Like the last curse of the list in Deuteronomy 28, it is the
ultimate horror for Israel: to return to slavery.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:31](#Contents.html_rDeu32_31)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^31^For their rock is not like our rock, though our enemies are the
judges.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_31.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:32](#Contents.html_rDeu32_32)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^32^For their vine is from Sodom's vine and from Gomorrah's fields.
Their grapes are poison grapes. They have bitter clusters.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_32.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:33](#Contents.html_rDeu32_33)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^33^Their wine is serpents' venom and cruel poison of cobras.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_33.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:34](#Contents.html_rDeu32_34)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^34^"Isn't it stored with me, sealed in my treasuries?

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_34.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:35](#Contents.html_rDeu32_35)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^35^Vengeance and recompense are mine, for the time their foot will
slip; for the day of their ordeal is close and comes fast: things
prepared for them."

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_35.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:36](#Contents.html_rDeu32_36)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^36^For YHWH will judge His people and regret about His servants when
He'll see that their strength is gone and there's none: held back or
left alone.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_36.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:37](#Contents.html_rDeu32_37)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^37^And He'll say, "Where are their gods, the rock in whom they sought
refuge,

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_37.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:38](#Contents.html_rDeu32_38)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^38^who would eat the fat of their sacrifices, would drink the wine of
their libations? Let them get up and help you! Let that be a shelter
over you.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_38.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:39](#Contents.html_rDeu32_39)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^39^See now that I, *I* am He, and there is no god with me. *I* cause
death and give life. I've pierced, and *I'll* heal. And there's no
deliverer from my hand,

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_39.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

32:39. **there is no god with me**. It is *possible* that this might
mean that other gods exist but that they just do not happen to be
present with YHWH at this time, but that seems to me to be a stretch.
Where would a god be who is not in YHWH's presence?! The plain sense of
these words (and other passages on which I have commented) is
monotheistic. It comes in a context of calling on gods to show
themselves if they exist: "Where are their gods\.... Let them get up and
help you!" And this poem shows well-known linguistic signs of being an
early composition. The persistent view in biblical scholarship that
monotheism is a late development in biblical Israel (coming during or
after the Babylonian exile) is contrary to such explicit statements as
this.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:40](#Contents.html_rDeu32_40)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^40^when I raise my hand to the skies, and I say, 'As I live forever,

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_40.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:41](#Contents.html_rDeu32_41)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^41^if I whet the lightning of my sword, and my hand takes hold of
judgment, I'll give back vengeance to my foes and pay back those who
hate me.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_41.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:42](#Contents.html_rDeu32_42)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^42^I'll make my arrows drunk with blood, and my sword will eat flesh
from the blood of the slain and captured, from the head of loose hair of
the enemy.'"

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_42.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:43](#Contents.html_rDeu32_43)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^43^Nations: cheer His people! For He'll requite His servants' blood and
give back vengeance to His foes and make atonement for His land, His
people.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_43.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:44](#Contents.html_rDeu32_44)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^44^And Moses came and spoke all the words of this song in the people's
ears, he and Hoshea son of Nun.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_44.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

32:44. **Hoshea**. Joshua.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:45](#Contents.html_rDeu32_45)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^45^And Moses finished speaking all these things to all of Israel.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_45.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:46](#Contents.html_rDeu32_46)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^46^And he said to them, "Pay attention to all the things that I testify
regarding you today, that you'll command them to your children, to
observe and to do all the words of this instruction.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_46.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:47](#Contents.html_rDeu32_47)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^47^Because it's not an empty thing for you, because it's your *life.*
And through this thing you'll extend days on the land to which you're
crossing the Jordan to take possession of it."

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_47.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

32:47. **it's your** ***life****.* Again we are reminded here at the end
of the Torah that these things are a path back to the tree of life that
was lost at the Torah's beginning. See the comment on Deut 30:19.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:48](#Contents.html_rDeu32_48)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^48^And YHWH spoke to Moses in this very day, saying,

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_48.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:49](#Contents.html_rDeu32_49)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^49^"Go up to this mountain of Abarim, Mount Nebo, which is in the land
of Moab, which is facing Jericho, and see the land of Canaan, which I'm
giving to the children of Israel for a possession.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_49.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:50](#Contents.html_rDeu32_50)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^50^And die in the mountain to which you're going up, and be gathered to
your people, as Aaron, your brother, died in Mount Hor and was gathered
to his people,

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_50.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:51](#Contents.html_rDeu32_51)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^51^because you made a breach with me among the children of Israel at
the waters of Meribah of Kadesh at the wilderness of Zin, because you
didn't make me holy among the children of Israel.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_51.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 32:52](#Contents.html_rDeu32_52)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^52^Because you'll see the land from opposite, but you shall not come
there, to the land that I'm giving to the children of Israel.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap32_52.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

[]{#Deuteronomy33.html}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 33:1](#Contents.html_rDeu33_1)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

**A[ND THIS IS THE BLESSING]{.small}**

**33**

^1^And this is the blessing with which Moses, the man of God, blessed
the children of Israel before his death.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap33.jpg)

![Image](images/Deu_Chap33_01.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 33:2](#Contents.html_rDeu33_2)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^2^And he said: YHWH came from Sinai and rose from Seir for them. He
shone from Mount Paran and came from ten thousands of the holy, slopes
at His right, for them.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap33_02.jpg)

![Image](images/Deu_Chap33_02a.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

33:2. **slopes at His right**. No one knows what this line means. (Frank
Cross and David Noel Freedman once wrote, "Conjectures are almost as
numerous as scholars.") I followed the consonantal text, taking Hebrew
*'![image](images/s-image.jpg)dt* as "slopes," which is what it means in
its only other occurrences in the Torah (Deut 3:17; 4:49). In both of
those occurrences it refers to the slopes of Pisgah, east of the Dead
Sea. In this verse, YHWH shines like the sun coming from the east over
Seir and Paran, in which case the slopes of Pisgah would in fact be at
His right.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 33:3](#Contents.html_rDeu33_3)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^3^Also loving peoples, all holy ones are in your hand. And they knelt
at your feet; they bore your words.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap33_03.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 33:4](#Contents.html_rDeu33_4)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^4^Moses commanded us instruction, a possession, community of Jacob.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap33_04.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 33:5](#Contents.html_rDeu33_5)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^5^And He was king in Jeshurun when the people's heads were gathered:
Israel's tribes together.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap33_05.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 33:6](#Contents.html_rDeu33_6)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^6^Let Reuben live and not die, but his men will be few in number.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap33_06.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

33:6. **few in number**. The territory of Reuben was the most vulnerable
of any tribe: located east of the Dead Sea, cut off from the tribes that
were west of the Jordan. It virtually ceased to exist by the tenth
century [B.C.E]{.smallcaps}.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 33:7](#Contents.html_rDeu33_7)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^7^And this for Judah---and he said: Hear, YHWH, Judah's voice, and
bring him to his people. With his hands he strove for himself, and
you'll be a help from its foes.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap33_07.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 33:8](#Contents.html_rDeu33_8)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^8^And for Levi he said: Your Tummim and your Urim are your faithful
man's, whom you tested at Massah, disputed with him over Meribah's
water.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap33_08.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 33:9](#Contents.html_rDeu33_9)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^9^Who said of his father and his mother: "I haven't seen him," and
didn't recognize his brothers and didn't know his children, for they
observed what you said and kept your covenant.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap33_09.jpg)

![Image](images/Deu_Chap33_09a.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

33:9. **father ... mother ... brothers ... children**. This has been
taken in a narrow sense (by Rashi and others) to refer to the golden
calf episode, in which Moses tells the Levites to "kill, each man, his
brother and, each man, his neighbor and, each man, his relative" (Exod
32:27,29). And it has been understood more broadly, to refer to the
Levites' single-minded performance of their priestly duties, neither
being distracted by one's family nor showing favoritism to them.
Alternatively, it might refer to the requirement that the high priest
may not touch any dead bodies, even of his own parents (Lev 21:11).

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 33:10](#Contents.html_rDeu33_10)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^10^They'll teach your judgments to Jacob and your instruction to
Israel. They'll set incense at your nose, entirely burnt on your altar.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap33_10.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

33:10. **entirely burnt on your altar**. The term for "entirely burnt"
(*k![image](images/a-image.jpg)lîl*) is used in connection with the
offering that the priests make on the day of the high priest's anointing
(Lev 6:12--16). It thus especially symbolizes the priestly status of
Levi.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 33:11](#Contents.html_rDeu33_11)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^11^Bless, YHWH, his wealth and accept his hands' work. Pierce his
adversaries' hips, and those who hate him, so they won't get up.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap33_11.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 33:12](#Contents.html_rDeu33_12)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^12^For Benjamin he said: Beloved of YHWH, he'll dwell in security by
Him. He shelters over him all day as he dwells between his shoulders.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap33_12.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 33:13](#Contents.html_rDeu33_13)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^13^And for Joseph he said: Blessed of YHWH is his land, from the skies'
abundance, from dew, and from the deep, crouching below,

![Image](images/Deu_Chap33_13.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 33:14](#Contents.html_rDeu33_14)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^14^and from the abundance of the sun's produce and from the abundance
of the moon's output

![Image](images/Deu_Chap33_14.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 33:15](#Contents.html_rDeu33_15)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^15^and from the top of the ancient mountains and from the abundance of
the hills of antiquity

![Image](images/Deu_Chap33_15.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 33:16](#Contents.html_rDeu33_16)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^16^and from the abundance of earth and what fills it and the favor of
the one who dwelt in the bush. May it be on Joseph's head, on the top of
the head of the one separate from his brothers.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap33_16.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

33:16. **May it be**. Following Cross and Freedman, I have read the
Hebrew **![image](images/Chap33_16a.jpg)** on the likelihood that it
conflated two readings: "May it come"
(**![image](images/Chap33_16b.jpg)**) and "May it be"
(**![image](images/Chap33_16c.jpg)**). The latter is preferable on the
basis of comparison with the parallel blessing of Joseph in Gen 49:26.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 33:17](#Contents.html_rDeu33_17)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^17^His firstborn bull: it has majesty. And its horns are a wild ox's
horns. It will gore peoples with them, together, the ends of the earth.
And they're Ephraim's ten thousands, And they're Manasseh's thousands.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap33_17.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 33:18](#Contents.html_rDeu33_18)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^18^And for Zebulun he said: Rejoice, Zebulun, when you go out, and
Issachar in your tents.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap33_18.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 33:19](#Contents.html_rDeu33_19)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^19^They'll call peoples to the mountain. There they'll offer the
sacrifices of virtue. For they'll suck the seas' bounty and the sand's
hidden treasures.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap33_19.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 33:20](#Contents.html_rDeu33_20)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^20^And for Gad he said: Blessed is one who enlarges Gad. Like a feline,
abiding and tearing an arm and the top of a head,

![Image](images/Deu_Chap33_20.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 33:21](#Contents.html_rDeu33_21)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^21^so he saw the foremost for himself, for a ruler's share was kept
there. And the heads of the people came. And he did YHWH's justice and
His laws with Israel.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap33_21.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

33:21. **he saw the foremost for himself**. Neither the length nor the
content of this verse suits the tribe of Gad, which is covered in the
previous verse. My colleague David Noel Freedman has suggested that it
is rather intertribal and that the specific references are to Moses as
the leader of the confederation of the tribes. This matches the picture
of Moses in vv. 4--5.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 33:22](#Contents.html_rDeu33_22)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^22^And for Dan he said: Dan is a lion's whelp that leapt from Bashan.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap33_22.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

33:22. **Dan is a lion's whelp**. But in Jacob's blessing "*Judah* is a
lion's whelp"! (Gen 49:9). Why are these two tribes described this way?
Dan is the northernmost tribe, and Judah is the southernmost. So this
produces an image of Israel protected by lions on either side.
(Actually, Simeon is the southernmost tribe, but Simeon is unaccountably
left out of this song.)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 33:23](#Contents.html_rDeu33_23)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^23^And for Naphtali he said: Naphtali is full of favor and filled with
YHWH's blessing. He'll possess west and south.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap33_23.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

33:23. **west and south**. But Naphtali is located in the *north* and
*east* of Israel. So this verse is understood to refer to the west and
south of the Sea of Galilee (known also as Sea of Tiberias, or Sea of
Kinneret).

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 33:24](#Contents.html_rDeu33_24)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^24^And for Asher he said: Blessed out of the sons is Asher. Let him be
favored by his brothers and dipping his foot in oil.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap33_24.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 33:25](#Contents.html_rDeu33_25)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^25^Your lock is iron and bronze, and your strength is as much as your
days.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap33_25.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 33:26](#Contents.html_rDeu33_26)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^26^There's none like the God of Jeshurun, riding skies to help you and
clouds in His majesty.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap33_26.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 33:27](#Contents.html_rDeu33_27)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^27^The ancient God is a refuge; []{#Deuteronomy33.html_page_111}and
below: the arms of the eternal. And He drove out an enemy before you and
said, "Destroy!"

![Image](images/Deu_Chap33_27.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 33:28](#Contents.html_rDeu33_28)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^28^And Israel will dwell secure, Jacob dwells alone. To a land of grain
and wine; and its skies drop dew.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap33_28.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 33:29](#Contents.html_rDeu33_29)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^29^Happy are you, Israel! Who is like you, a people saved by YHWH, your
strong shield who is your majestic sword?! And your enemies will fawn to
you. And you: you'll step on their high places.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap33_29.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

33:29. **Happy are you, Israel!** These are the last words that the
people hear from Moses. Strange: The first half is understandable as his
final words, "Happy are you ... a people saved by YHWH." The second half
is not what we might have expected: God is their strong shield and
sword! "And your enemies will fawn to you. And you: you'll step on their
high places." Why does he end with the defeat of enemies? Apparently an
assurance that Israel does not have to fear enemies was important---and
so it has in fact turned out in the people's history in the three
millennia following Moses. The presence of enemies has been a sad,
constant fact of life. But Moses' last words are an assurance that
Israel will survive to fulfill the destiny that was the first promise to
Abraham: to be a source of blessing for all the earth's families.

33:29. **high places**. See the comment on Lev 26:30.

[]{#Deuteronomy34.html}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 34:1](#Contents.html_rDeu34_1)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

**34** ^1^And Moses went up from the plains of Moab to Mount Nebo, the
top of Pisgah, which is facing Jericho. And YHWH showed him all of the
land, Gilead to Dan,

![Image](images/Deu_Chap34_01.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 34:2](#Contents.html_rDeu34_2)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^2^and all of Naphtali and the land of Ephraim and Manasseh and all the
land of Judah to the far sea

![Image](images/Deu_Chap34_02.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 34:3](#Contents.html_rDeu34_3)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^3^and the Negeb and the plain, the valley of
[]{#Deuteronomy34.html_page_112}Jericho, city of the palms, to Zoar.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap34_03.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 34:4](#Contents.html_rDeu34_4)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^4^And YHWH said to him, "This is the land that I swore to Abraham, to
Isaac, and to Jacob, saying, 'I'll give it to your seed.' I've caused
you to see it with your eyes, but you won't pass there."

![Image](images/Deu_Chap34_04.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

34:4. **Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob**. This is the last mention of them in
the Torah. The Torah returns to them and to the promises to them, but it
does not end with those promises fulfilled. It ends with Moses looking
out at the land, with the fulfillment lying in the future. Some have
said that the original unit was the Hexateuch rather than the
Pentateuch; that is, that the story more properly concludes in the book
of Joshua, when the people come to live in the land that was promised to
Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. But the Torah ends here, with the future,
with the story still to be told. It is a message to the reader that this
is not meant to be just a story of the past. I wrote at the beginning of
this commentary that the Torah's story is rich in background, always
presuming what has come before it (and I shall discuss below how it is
also enriched by its reader's knowledge of what is coming later in the
story). Now I would add that it has a third perspective in that it
always points beyond itself, to the destiny of Israel and humankind in
the rest of the *Tanak* that follows, and, for millions of
[]{#Deuteronomy34.html_page_115}readers, in their continuing life beyond
the conclusion of the Bible.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 34:5](#Contents.html_rDeu34_5)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^5^And Moses, YHWH's servant, died there in the land of Moab by YHWH's
mouth,

![Image](images/Deu_Chap34_05.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 34:6](#Contents.html_rDeu34_6)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^6^and He buried him in the valley in the land of Moab opposite Beth
Peor. And no man knows his burial place to this day.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap34_06.jpg)

![Image](images/hr.jpg){height="17" width="550"}

34:6. **He buried him**. This is usually understood to mean that God
buries Moses. We should note that Qumran, Septuagint, and some
manuscripts of the Samaritan tradition read "*they* buried him." This
presumably means that the people bury him. That would imply a completely
different picture of Moses' death. He does not die alone on the
mountain. After he sees the land from the top of the mountain he goes
down to a lower part of the mountain, where he dies with others around
him, and then his people bury him. It is remarkable that a scribal
difference of a single letter (a *waw* at the end of the word
**![image](images/Chap34_6a.jpg)**, making it plural: "they buried")
leaves open such a significant point: Does Moses end his life with God
or with his fellow human beings? Perhaps it is meaningful (and
appropriate?) that some scribes understood it one way, and other scribes
understood it the other way. Moses lives his life in a zone between God
and humans. We saw in the matter of the golden calf at Sinai that when
Moses speaks to God he represents the people, and when he speaks to the
people he represents God. (See the comment on Exod 32:19.)

34:6. **no man knows his burial place to this day**. Ancestor veneration
is linked with the actual grave site. One can go to the so-called tombs
of Rachel and David in Israel today and see people praying to Rachel and
David there to answer their prayers or to intercede with God. In this
report of the nonknowledge of Moses' burial place, therefore, the
concern is that such veneration and prayer for intercession is not
possible with Moses. It comes as a sharp contrast to all the other
reports about burials in the family tomb in the Torah and elsewhere in
the Bible.

If we understand the first part of this verse to read "*He* buried him,"
then it would suggest that God does not desire such things to take place
at Moses' tomb. If we read "*they* buried him," it is harder to
interpret why the Israelites themselves would make his burial site
unknown. One possibility is that their leaders, in particular the high
priest Eleazar, likewise do not want veneration at Moses' tomb. Another
is that they are burying Moses in Moab, outside the land of Israel, and
so they fear that the grave would be mistreated by foreign people. They
cannot take Moses' body to the promised land with them because
[]{#Deuteronomy34.html_page_116}of the biological necessity of immediate
burial. The bodies of Jacob and Joseph are carried back to Israel for
burial, but in those cases we are specifically informed that the bodies
have been embalmed (Gen 50:1,26).

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*[Deuteronomy 34:7](#Contents.html_rDeu34_7)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

^7^And Moses was a hundred twenty years old at his death. His eye was
not dim, and his vitality had not fled.

![Image](images/Deu_Chap34_07.jpg)
